topics.title: Introduction to Hydraulics, Pascal's Law, Conservation of Energy, Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Hydraulics, Pascal's Law, Conservation of Energy, Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Hydraulics?

**Hydraulics** is the applied science and engineering discipline that deals with the mechanical behavior of **liquids** (primarily incompressible fluids such as mineral oils, water-glycol mixtures, and synthetic fluids) at rest and in motion, and the transmission of **force and energy** through a confined liquid medium. The word itself originates from the Greek *hydor* (ὕδωρ) meaning "water," although modern hydraulics almost never uses water in industrial systems because of corrosion, freezing, and lubrication concerns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *Hydraulics is the branch of science and engineering that deals with the transmission of power through the use of a pressurized incompressible liquid as the working medium.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Solid Bar" Model

Imagine pushing a long, perfectly rigid steel rod through a wall. Whatever force you apply at one end instantly appears at the other end, undiminished, undiminished by the rod's length, and undiminished by bends in the rod. A hydraulic fluid enclosed in a sealed chamber behaves in an exactly analogous way. The liquid is essentially "incompressible," meaning it refuses to change volume under pressure, so the mechanical "push" is delivered faithfully from the input piston to the output piston, just like a rigid rod.

This is precisely why a 5 kg person standing on a tiny 1 cm² piston can lift a 5-tonne truck — the *pressure* (force per unit area) is the same everywhere, and since the truck sits on a much larger piston, the *force* on the truck side is enormous.

### 1.3 Why Liquids? Why not Gases?

| Property | Hydraulic Liquid | Pneumatic Gas |
|---|---|---|
| Compressibility | Negligible (~0.5%) | Very high (~100% at 7 bar) |
| Energy Storage | High density (≈ **780 kg/m³** for oil) | Low density (≈ **1.2 kg/m³** for air) |
| Speed of Response | Fast, but limited by bulk modulus | Very fast, near-instant |
| Lubrication | Self-lubricating (mineral oil) | Requires external lubrication |
| Leakage Cost | Expensive fluid loss | Cheap, safe fluid loss |
| Fire Risk | High (oil) | None (air) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 PESFT752 distinguishes **Hydraulics** (liquids) from **Pneumatics** (gases) as the two principal branches of *Fluid Power*. Both obey the same governing laws (Pascal, Bernoulli, Continuity) but with different compressibility constants.

### 1.4 Pressure — The Master Variable of Fluid Power

**Pressure (P)** is defined as the **normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area** on any real or imaginary surface in contact with it.

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

where **F** is the force in **Newtons (N)** and **A** is the area in **square meters (m²)**. The **SI unit** of pressure is the **Pascal (Pa)**, defined as **1 N/m²**. For practical industrial use, **bar** (**1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa**) is the standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Reference States of Pressure (Board-Favorite Question!):**
> 1. **Absolute Pressure (P_abs):** Measured from a perfect vacuum (zero reference). *Can never be negative.*
> 2. **Gauge Pressure (P_gauge):** Measured from local atmospheric pressure. *Can be positive or negative (vacuum).*
> 3. **Atmospheric Pressure (P_atm):** Standard value at sea level = **1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa = 14.696 psi**.

The relationship is:

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pascal's Law — The First Principle of Fluid Power

**Statement (Verbatim, KTU Board Standard):**
> *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid and acts perpendicular to the surfaces of the containing vessel."*

#### 2.1.1 Logical Breakdown of Pascal's Law

The statement contains **four engineering-critical assertions**. Each one is the basis of an entire hydraulic component:

1. **"Pressure applied"** — There must be an external mechanical input (pump, hand lever, ram).
2. **"Enclosed incompressible fluid at rest"** — The fluid must be **trapped** (sealed system) and **incompressible** (liquids, not gases).
3. **"Transmitted equally and undiminished"** — The pressure at every point in the fluid is identical. This is the *equal pressure principle*.
4. **"Perpendicular to the surfaces"** — The fluid pushes on walls *normally*; it has no shear component at a static wall.

#### 2.1.2 Mathematical Form of Pascal's Law

Consider a small fluid element in equilibrium. For it not to accelerate, the net force on every face must be zero. Summing forces on opposing faces of area *A* separated by distance *dx*:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 \cdot A - P_2 \cdot A &= m \cdot a = \rho \cdot A \cdot dx \cdot 0 \quad (\text{fluid at rest} \Rightarrow a=0) \\
\Rightarrow P_1 &= P_2
\end{aligned}$$

Hence the **Pascal Identity**:

$$\boxed{P_1 = P_2 = P_3 = \cdots = P_n \quad \text{(constant throughout the fluid)}}$$

#### 2.1.3 The Hydraulic Press — Pascal's Law in Action

The **hydraulic press** (or *hydraulic lift*, *hydraulic jack*) is a direct mechanical application. Two pistons of areas *A₁* (small) and *A₂* (large) sit in a U-tube filled with oil.

Applying Pascal's Law at the oil–piston interfaces:

$$P_1 = P_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

Solving for the output force:

$$\boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2}$$

The ratio $A_2/A_1$ is called the **Mechanical Advantage (MA)** or **Force Multiplication Factor** of the press.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** This exact principle powers **vehicle jacks** (lifting 1 ton with one hand), **hydraulic presses** in forging shops (compressing cars into cubes), **brake systems** in cars and aircraft (your foot's force multiplies 50× to clamp a disc), and the **landing-gear actuators** of every commercial airliner.

### 2.2 Conservation of Energy in Fluid Power

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** (conservation of energy) applied to a steady-flowing fluid in a hydraulic line is expressed by the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**:

$$\boxed{h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + z_1 = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + h_L}$$

where each term has the units of **head** (meters of fluid):

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $h$ | Pump/impeller head added |
| $\dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Kinetic (velocity) head |
| $\dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Pressure (flow) head |
| $z$ | Potential (elevation) head |
| $h_L$ | Head loss (friction + minor losses) |

#### 2.2.1 Bernoulli's Equation (The Ideal, Lossless Form)

If we set $h = 0$ (no pump), $h_L = 0$ (ideal, no friction), and assume incompressible flow, we recover **Daniel Bernoulli's 1738 equation**:

$$\boxed{\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}}$$

This is the **ideal-flow** version. Real hydraulic systems always have $h_L > 0$, so the right side **decreases** in the direction of flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Bernoulli's equation applies to **ideal (inviscid, incompressible, steady) flow** along a **single streamline**. Many students incorrectly apply it across streamlines, or to compressible air, or in pumps and turbines. Do not.

#### 2.2.2 Hydraulic Power Transmission

The **power** delivered by a hydraulic pump is:

$$\boxed{P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = \Delta P \cdot Q \quad [\text{Watts}]}$$

where $\Delta P$ is in **Pa** and $Q$ is in **m³/s**. In practical units:

$$P_{hyd} \, [\text{kW}] = \frac{\Delta P \, [\text{bar}] \times Q \, [\text{L/min}]}{600}$$

### 2.3 Hydrostatic Pressure — Pressure in a Stationary Liquid

In a fluid at rest, the only stress present is **pressure** (no shear). Balancing the weight of a vertical column of fluid of height *h*:

$$\boxed{P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h}$$

This is the **hydrostatic pressure** at depth *h*. The pressure acts equally in all directions at any point (Pascal's Law in disguise). It is the reason dams are built thicker at the base than at the top, and why a submarine's hull must withstand higher pressure the deeper it dives.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = F/A$ | Pressure definition | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Pressure reference conversion | Pa |
| 3 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure | Pa |
| 4 | $P_1 = P_2$ (Pascal) | Equal pressure throughout | Pa |
| 5 | $F_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1)$ | Hydraulic press force | N |
| 6 | $Q = A \cdot V$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 7 | $\dot{V} = A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | Continuity equation | m³/s |
| 8 | $P + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = \text{const}$ | Bernoulli (ideal) | Pa |
| 9 | $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | Hydraulic power | W |
| 10 | $1\,\text{bar} = 10^5\,\text{Pa} = 100\,\text{kPa}$ | Unit conversion | — |
| 11 | $1\,\text{psi} = 6.895\,\text{kPa}$ | Unit conversion | — |
| 12 | $1\,\text{atm} = 1.01325\,\text{bar}$ | Unit conversion | — |

### 2.5 Why This System is Central to Fluid Power Safety

A textbook hydraulic press can multiply force by a factor of **1000 or more**. This enormous mechanical advantage is also a **safety hazard** if the system is mishandled. A stuck valve can build pressure to **700 bar** in seconds; a 5 mm pin-hole leak at this pressure can slice through human skin like a razor blade and inject oil into the bloodstream. Hence, an engineer who does not understand Pascal, energy, and pressure **cannot** design a safe fluid power system — which is why KTU has placed this topic at the very front of the PESFT752 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure $P = \rho g h$ from a Force Balance

Consider a horizontal slab of fluid at depth *h* below the free surface, with cross-sectional area *A*. The slab has:
- Mass: $m = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot h$
- Weight: $W = m \cdot g = \rho g A h$

The forces acting vertically on the slab are:

1. Pressure from above: $P_{top} \cdot A$ (pushing **down**)
2. Pressure from below: $P_{bottom} \cdot A$ (pushing **up**)
3. Weight of the slab: $W = \rho g A h$ (pushing **down**)

**Step 1.** Apply Newton's Second Law (slab is at rest, so net force = 0):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum F_{vertical} &= 0 \\
P_{bottom} \cdot A - P_{top} \cdot A - \rho g A h &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2.** Cancel *A* (non-zero) from every term:

$$P_{bottom} - P_{top} - \rho g h = 0$$

**Step 3.** If the top surface is open to the atmosphere, $P_{top} = P_{atm}$ and the **gauge** pressure at the bottom is $P_{bottom} - P_{atm}$:

$$P_{gauge} = \rho g h$$

**Step 4.** The result is independent of *A*, meaning hydrostatic pressure depends **only on depth, fluid density, and gravity** — not on the size or shape of the container. This counter-intuitive result is called the **hydrostatic paradox**.

$$\boxed{P_{hydrostatic} = \rho g h \quad \text{(derived from Newton's 2nd Law)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pascal's Law from a Control Volume Argument

Consider a small triangular fluid element in a static body of fluid, with two faces of area $dA$ meeting at angle $\theta$, and the third face (the hypotenuse) of area $dA/\sin\theta$.

**Step 1.** Identify the three pressure forces:

- On vertical face: $P_v \cdot dA$ (acting horizontally)
- On horizontal face: $P_h \cdot dA$ (acting vertically)
- On inclined face: $P \cdot (dA/\sin\theta)$ (acting normal to the surface)

**Step 2.** Resolve the inclined-face force into components. The inclined face has area $dA/\sin\theta$ and is normal to itself, so the force is $P \cdot (dA/\sin\theta)$ at angle $\theta$ from vertical. Its components are:

- Horizontal: $P \cdot (dA/\sin\theta) \cdot \sin\theta = P \cdot dA$
- Vertical: $P \cdot (dA/\sin\theta) \cdot \cos\theta = P \cdot dA \cdot \cot\theta$

**Step 3.** Apply static equilibrium ($\sum F_x = 0$ and $\sum F_y = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum F_x &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; P_v \cdot dA - P \cdot dA = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; P_v = P \\
\sum F_y &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; P_h \cdot dA - P \cdot dA \cdot \cot\theta - \rho g \cdot dV = 0
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4.** For an infinitesimally small element, $dV \to 0$, so the gravity term vanishes:

$$P_h \cdot dA = P \cdot dA \cdot \cot\theta \;\Rightarrow\; P_h = P \cdot \cot\theta$$

Wait — this depends on $\theta$? **Yes**, for a finite element where weight is significant. But as $dV \to 0$, the element becomes a *point*, and we get:

$$\boxed{P_v = P_h = P \quad \text{(pressure is isotropic at a point)}}$$

This is Pascal's Law: **at any point in a static fluid, the pressure is the same in every direction.**

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Continuity Equation $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$

Consider a pipe whose cross-section changes from $A_1$ to $A_2$. In a small time $dt$, the mass of fluid entering section 1 must equal the mass leaving section 2 (conservation of mass for an incompressible fluid).

**Step 1.** Mass entering at section 1 in time $dt$:

$$dm_1 = \rho \cdot A_1 \cdot V_1 \cdot dt$$

**Step 2.** Mass leaving at section 2 in time $dt$:

$$dm_2 = \rho \cdot A_2 \cdot V_2 \cdot dt$$

**Step 3.** Equate them (mass conservation):

$$\rho A_1 V_1 \, dt = \rho A_2 V_2 \, dt$$

**Step 4.** Cancel $\rho$ and $dt$:

$$\boxed{A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \quad \text{(constant volumetric flow rate)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why nozzles (small $A$) produce high velocity streams, and why hydraulic lines that branch must size their cross-sections so that $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 + A_3 V_3$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bernoulli's Equation from Energy Conservation

Apply the **Work-Energy Theorem** to a fluid particle moving from point 1 to point 2 along a streamline in an ideal (inviscid, incompressible) steady flow.

**Step 1.** The four contributions to net work done on the fluid:
- Pressure work at inlet: $P_1 A_1 ds_1 = P_1 dV$
- Pressure work at outlet: $-P_2 A_2 ds_2 = -P_2 dV$
- Gravity work: $-mg(z_2 - z_1)$
- Friction work: **0** (ideal flow)

**Step 2.** Net work equals change in kinetic energy:

$$P_1 dV - P_2 dV - mg(z_2 - z_1) = \tfrac{1}{2} m (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$

**Step 3.** Substitute $m = \rho dV$ and divide throughout by $dV$:

$$P_1 - P_2 - \rho g (z_2 - z_1) = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$

**Step 4.** Rearrange:

$$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$

**Step 5.** Each term has units of pressure (Pa). For head form, divide by $\rho g$:

$$\boxed{\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = H \quad \text{(total head)}}$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Hydraulic Press Force-Multiplication Identity

**Step 1.** Apply Pascal's Law at the bottom of the U-tube, where the small and large pistons meet the common oil body:

$$P_1 = P_2$$

**Step 2.** Substitute the pressure definition $P = F/A$:

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 3.** Solve for the output force $F_2$:

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 4.** If the pistons are circular with diameters $D_1$ and $D_2$:

$$\boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2}$$

**Step 5 (Displacement Trade-off).** Conservation of volume requires $A_1 \cdot s_1 = A_2 \cdot s_2$ (where $s_i$ is the piston travel), so:

$$s_2 = s_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = s_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2$$

The **displacement is reduced by the same factor that the force is amplified** — energy is conserved ($F_1 s_1 = F_2 s_2$, neglecting friction).

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

#### Example A (Hydrostatic Pressure)

> A hydraulic reservoir is open to atmosphere. Find the gauge pressure at a depth of 4 m. Take $\rho_{oil} = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Solution:**

$$P = \rho g h = 870 \times 9.81 \times 4 = 34\,138.8 \text{ Pa} \approx 34.14 \text{ kPa} = 0.341 \text{ bar}$$

#### Example B (Hydraulic Press)

> A hydraulic press has a small piston of 25 mm diameter and a large piston of 200 mm diameter. A force of 150 N is applied to the small piston. Find (a) pressure in the oil, (b) force on the large piston, (c) distance moved by the large piston when the small piston moves 80 mm.

**Solution:**

(a) Pressure:

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{150}{\pi (0.025)^2 / 4} = \frac{150}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.056 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 3.056 \text{ bar}$$

(b) Force on large piston:

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 3.056 \times 10^5 \times \frac{\pi (0.200)^2}{4} = 9.6 \text{ kN}$$

(c) Distance moved:

$$s_2 = s_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = 80 \times \left(\frac{25}{200}\right)^2 = 80 \times 0.015625 = 1.25 \text{ mm}$$

(Verify: $F_1 s_1 = 150 \times 0.080 = 12 \text{ J} = F_2 s_2 = 9600 \times 0.00125 = 12 \text{ J}$ ✓)

#### Example C (Manometer / Pressure Difference)

> A U-tube manometer connects two points in a horizontal water pipeline. The manometer fluid is mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13\,600 \text{ kg/m}^3$). The mercury level difference is 100 mm, with the higher side connected to point A. Find $P_A - P_B$.

**Solution:**

Writing the manometer equation from A to B through the mercury:

$$P_A + \rho_w g (h_1) - \rho_{Hg} g \cdot h_{Hg} - \rho_w g (h_2) = P_B$$

For a horizontal pipe where A and B are at the same elevation and neglecting the small water columns above the mercury:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_{Hg} g h_{Hg} = 13\,600 \times 9.81 \times 0.100 = 13\,342 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.133 \text{ bar}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Hydraulic Force-Multiplication System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input Force F1]:::in --> B1[Small Piston Area A1]:::mech
    B1 --> C1[Pascal Transmission Through Oil]:::pasc
    C1 --> D1[Large Piston Area A2]:::mech
    D1 --> E1[Output Force F2 Amplified]:::out
    C1 --> F1[Pressure P Equal Everywhere]:::pasc
    F1 -.feeds back.-> B1
    F1 -.feeds back.-> D1
    classDef in fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#166534,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mech fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pasc fill:#6b21a8,stroke:#7e22ce,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Pascal's Law Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N1([Start]):::start --> S1[Apply External Force F1 to Small Piston]:::stage
    S1 --> S2[Compute Pressure P equals F1 divided by A1]:::calc
    S2 --> S3{Pascal Identity: P constant everywhere?}:::gate
    S3 -- yes --> S4[Transmit P to All Wetted Surfaces]:::prop
    S4 --> S5[Large Piston Receives Pressure P]:::stage
    S5 --> S6[Compute Output Force F2 equals P times A2]:::calc
    S6 --> S7[Compute Displacement s2 equals s1 times A1 divided by A2]:::calc
    S7 --> N2([End]):::end
    classDef start fill:#0f766e,stroke:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    classDef end fill:#475569,stroke:#64748b,color:#ffffff
    classDef stage fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef calc fill:#b45309,stroke:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef prop fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#a855f7,color:#ffffff
    classDef gate fill:#be123c,stroke:#e11d48,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Pressure Reference Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V[Vacuum: P equals 0 absolute]:::zero --> ABS[Absolute Pressure Pabs]:::abs
    ATM[Atmospheric: P equals 1.01325 bar]:::atm --> ABS
    ABS --> G[Positive Gauge: Pabs greater than Patm]:::pos
    ATM --> NEG[Vacuum Gauge: Pabs less than Patm]:::neg
    ABS --> ABS2{Pabs greater than or equal 0?}:::gate
    G --> F1[Formula: Pabs equals Pgauge plus Patm]:::f
    NEG --> F2[Formula: Pvac equals Patm minus Pabs]:::f
    classDef zero fill:#000000,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef abs fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    classDef atm fill:#365314,stroke:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
    classDef pos fill:#9a3412,stroke:#c2410c,color:#ffffff
    classDef neg fill:#581c87,stroke:#7e22ce,color:#ffffff
    classDef gate fill:#9f1239,stroke:#be123c,color:#ffffff
    classDef f fill:#52525b,stroke:#71717a,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Energy Flow in a Hydraulic Circuit (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Electric Motor]:::elec --> P1[Hydraulic Pump]:::mech
    P1 --> E1[Pressure Energy in Oil]:::energy
    E1 --> V1[Flow Control Valve]:::ctrl
    V1 --> A1[Hydraulic Actuator Cylinder]:::mech
    A1 --> W1[Mechanical Work Output]:::out
    E1 -.loss.-> L1[Pressure Drop and Heat Loss]:::loss
    V1 -.loss.-> L1
    A1 -.loss.-> L1
    L1 --> T1[Oil Tank Reservoir]:::tank
    T1 --> P1
    classDef elec fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#eab308,color:#000000
    classDef mech fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#2563eb,color:#ffffff
    classDef energy fill:#7e22ce,stroke:#a855f7,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#be123c,stroke:#e11d48,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#166534,stroke:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    classDef loss fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#991b1b,color:#ffffff
    classDef tank fill:#475569,stroke:#64748b,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The colored nodes trace four distinct engineering quantities — electrical input (yellow), mechanical conversion (blue), pressure energy transport (purple), and dissipation/loss (red). This block-level abstraction is the **only** safe way to represent fluid-power safety concepts at the KTU 2024 examination level; detailed physical drawings are out of syllabus scope.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**State Pascal's Law. Mention any two engineering applications where Pascal's Law is used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1.5 marks):** *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, acting perpendicular to the surfaces of the container."*

**Applications (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Hydraulic press / jack** — used in vehicle lifting, forging, and metal forming.
2. **Hydraulic brake system** — used in automobiles, where the driver's foot force is multiplied to clamp the disc/drum.

*(Alternative valid applications: hydraulic steering, hydraulic lift in aircraft landing gear, hydraulic punch, hydraulic crane.)*

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between Absolute Pressure, Gauge Pressure, and Atmospheric Pressure. Establish the mathematical relationship between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Reference | Range |
|---|---|---|
| Absolute Pressure (P_abs) | Perfect vacuum (zero) | ≥ 0 always |
| Gauge Pressure (P_gauge) | Local atmospheric | Can be + or − |
| Atmospheric Pressure (P_atm) | Sea-level standard | 1.01325 bar (constant for conversions) |

**Relationship (1 mark):**

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$

**Example (0.5 mark):** A tyre reads 2.2 bar on the gauge. Its absolute pressure = 2.2 + 1.013 = 3.213 bar.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You are expected to attempt ONE of the two alternatives provided.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified Past Paper]**

**(a)** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter 30 mm and a large piston of diameter 240 mm. An input force of 200 N is applied to the small piston.
   **(i)** Calculate the pressure developed in the oil.
   **(ii)** Calculate the force exerted by the large piston.
   **(iii)** If the small piston is pushed down by 100 mm, find the distance moved by the large piston. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the working of a hydraulic lift. State Pascal's Law. Mention any four industrial applications of hydraulic systems. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Given:** $D_1 = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030 \text{ m}$; $D_2 = 240 \text{ mm} = 0.240 \text{ m}$; $F_1 = 200 \text{ N}$; $s_1 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$.

**(i) Pressure developed (2 marks):**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.030)^2}{4} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.829 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2.829 \text{ bar}$$

**(ii) Force on large piston (3 marks):**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.240)^2}{4} = 4.524 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = 2.829 \times 10^5 \times 4.524 \times 10^{-2} = 12\,800 \text{ N} = 12.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Alternative (verification via Pascal):**

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot (D_2/D_1)^2 = 200 \times (240/30)^2 = 200 \times 64 = 12\,800 \text{ N} \checkmark$$

**(iii) Distance moved by large piston (2 marks):**

$$s_2 = s_1 \cdot (D_1/D_2)^2 = 100 \times (30/240)^2 = 100 \times 0.015625 = 1.5625 \text{ mm}$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Working of hydraulic lift (3 marks):**

A hydraulic lift consists of two cylinders of different cross-sectional areas connected at the base and filled with hydraulic oil. A small piston (of area $A_1$) in the smaller cylinder is connected to a hand pump or motor pump. When force $F_1$ is applied on the small piston, it creates pressure $P = F_1/A_1$ in the oil. By Pascal's Law, this pressure is transmitted undiminished to the large piston (area $A_2$) in the larger cylinder. The large piston therefore experiences force $F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$, which is many times greater than $F_1$, lifting a load placed on it.

**Pascal's Law (1 mark):** Verbatim statement as given in Q1 above.

**Industrial applications (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Hydraulic jacks and lifts** in automobile workshops.
2. **Hydraulic presses** in forging, stamping, and metal forming.
3. **Hydraulic brakes and clutches** in road vehicles, locomotives, and aircraft.
4. **Hydraulic excavators, cranes, and bulldozers** in earth-moving equipment.

*(Valid alternatives: hydraulic steering, hydraulic shock absorbers, hydraulic actuators in robots, plastic injection moulding machines.)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Restyled for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A hydraulic system uses oil of density 870 kg/m³. The reservoir is open to atmosphere. Find:
   **(i)** The absolute pressure at a point 6 m below the oil surface.
   **(ii)** The corresponding gauge pressure.
   **(iii)** The force exerted by the oil on a circular inspection cover of diameter 80 mm located at that depth.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and prove Bernoulli's equation for the steady, incompressible, inviscid flow of a fluid along a streamline. Mention any three practical limitations of this equation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Given:** $\rho_{oil} = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$; $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$; $h = 6 \text{ m}$; $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$; $P_{atm} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.

**(i) Absolute pressure (2 marks):**

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h = 1.01325 \times 10^5 + 870 \times 9.81 \times 6$$

$$= 1.01325 \times 10^5 + 51\,208.2 = 1.5244 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.5244 \text{ bar}$$

**(ii) Gauge pressure (2 marks):**

$$P_{gauge} = \rho g h = 51\,208.2 \text{ Pa} = 0.5121 \text{ bar}$$

**(iii) Force on the inspection cover (3 marks):**

Area of cover:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Force on cover (using gauge pressure for force due to oil only; absolute pressure would include atmospheric force on the outside):

$$F = P_{gauge} \cdot A = 51\,208.2 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 257.4 \text{ N}$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Statement of Bernoulli's equation (1 mark):**
*"For a steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless) flow of a fluid along a streamline, the sum of the pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head remains constant."*

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$$

**Proof (4 marks):** Follow Derivation 4 from Section 3.4 above, step-by-step.

**Practical limitations (2 marks — 0.67 each):**
1. **No friction assumption** — Real fluids are viscous; significant head loss $h_L$ is always present.
2. **Incompressibility** — Cannot be applied to high-speed gas flows (Mach > 0.3).
3. **Single streamline** — Not valid across streamlines in rotational or turbulent flow.
4. **Steady flow only** — Cannot be used for rapidly varying (pulsating) flows.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Pascal's Law & Pressure Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting Pascal requires incompressibility** — full 0.5 to 1 mark lost if applied to gases. (2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing gauge and absolute pressure** — particularly in reservoir and manometer problems. State explicitly which you are using. (1–2 marks)
> 3. **Skipping units in the final answer** — always write **bar** or **Pa** or **N/m²**. Bare numbers lose half a mark. (0.5 mark)
> 4. **Failing to convert mm → m** in area calculations — $A = \pi D^2/4$ with $D$ in mm gives answers that are wrong by a factor of $10^6$. (1–2 marks)
> 5. **In hydraulic press questions, the displacement ratio is the inverse of the force ratio** — students often write $s_2 = s_1 (D_2/D_1)^2$ (inverted). (1 mark)
> 6. **In energy / Bernoulli questions, the height datum (z) must be stated clearly** — examiners explicitly deduct 0.5 mark if the reference is undefined. (0.5 mark)
> 7. **Forgetting the pressure acts perpendicular to submerged surfaces** — a sketch showing the force at an angle is an instant 0.5 mark deduction. (0.5 mark)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Memorize These 12 Points:**

- [x] **Hydraulics** = transmission of force/power through an *incompressible liquid*; **Pneumatics** = through a *compressible gas*.
- [x] **Pascal's Law (verbatim):** Pressure on an enclosed incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally in all directions.
- [x] **Hydraulic press:** $F_2 = F_1 (A_2/A_1) = F_1 (D_2/D_1)^2$ — force multiplied; **displacement divided** by the same factor.
- [x] **Continuity equation:** $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q$ — for an incompressible fluid in a closed pipe.
- [x] **Bernoulli's equation (ideal):** $P/\rho g + V^2/2g + z = $ constant along a streamline.
- [x] **Real hydraulic equation:** include $h_L$ (head loss) and pump head $h$.
- [x] **Hydrostatic pressure:** $P = \rho g h$ — independent of container shape (*hydrostatic paradox*).
- [x] **Pressure units:** 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa ≈ 14.5 psi. 1 atm = 1.01325 bar.
- [x] **Three pressure references:** Absolute (from vacuum), Gauge (from atmosphere), Vacuum (below atmosphere).
- [x] **Key identity:** $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
- [x] **Hydraulic power:** $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q$; in kW: $P_{hyd} = (P_{bar} \cdot Q_{LPM})/600$.
- [x] **Safety fact:** Hydraulic oil at 700 bar through a 0.5 mm orifice reaches velocities > 100 m/s — enough to penetrate human skin (fluid injection injury).

> **End of Module 1 — Topic: Introduction to Hydraulics, Pascal's Law, Conservation of Energy, Pressure**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work and Power, Principles of Power Hydraulics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Work and Power, Principles of Power Hydraulics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Work in Fluid Power Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work (W)** is defined as the product of force applied on a body and the distance through which the body moves in the direction of the force.
>
> **Mathematical Expression:** $W = F \times d$
>
> **SI Unit:** Joule (J) or Newton-metre (N·m)

In the context of **fluid power systems**, work is performed when pressurized fluid exerts force on a piston, ram, or rotating shaft, causing it to displace through a measurable distance. Unlike mechanical work done directly by a rigid linkage, fluid power work is mediated by an incompressible (or compressible) hydraulic medium transmitting energy.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine pushing a car. You apply a force (F) and the car moves some distance (d). The total energy you transfer to the car is your "work." Now imagine doing the same job using a hydraulic jack — you still accomplish the same work, but a small input force is multiplied through fluid pressure. The work output equals work input (minus losses), but the force-distance trade-off is changed by Pascal's principle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydraulic Work** is energy transmitted by pressurized fluid. When fluid pushes against a piston with force **F** over a stroke length **L**, the work done on the load is exactly **W = F × L**. This is independent of whether the force is generated mechanically or hydraulically.

### 1.2 Power in Fluid Power Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power (P)** is the time rate of doing work, i.e., how quickly energy is transferred or work is accomplished.
>
> **Mathematical Expression:** $P = \dfrac{W}{t} = F \times v = \dfrac{F \times d}{t}$
>
> **SI Unit:** Watt (W) = 1 J/s

**Hydraulic Power** specifically refers to the rate at which energy is delivered by a pressurized hydraulic fluid to do useful work. It is fundamentally a function of two measurable fluid parameters: **pressure (p)** and **flow rate (Q)**.

### 1.3 Principles of Power Hydraulics — Foundational Laws

The discipline of power hydraulics rests on three immutable physical laws:

1. **Pascal's Law** — Pressure applied to a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in every direction throughout the fluid.
2. **Continuity Equation** — For an incompressible fluid, mass (and therefore volumetric) flow rate is conserved: $Q_1 = Q_2$.
3. **Conservation of Energy** — Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; hydraulic systems merely transform mechanical energy → hydraulic energy → mechanical energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (Mathematical Form):**
>
> $$\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2} = p$$
>
> This implies a force multiplication factor of $A_2/A_1$ when a small piston drives a larger one — the foundation of every hydraulic press, jack, and lift.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Units (KTU High-Yield)

- **Standard gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$
- **Density of standard hydraulic oil:** $\rho \approx 850 - 900 \; kg/m^3$
- **1 Horsepower (mechanical):** $1 \; HP = 745.7 \; W \approx 0.746 \; kW$
- **1 Metric Horsepower:** $1 \; PS = 735.5 \; W$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $p_{atm} = 101.325 \; kPa = 1.013 \; bar$
- **Pressure unit conversions:** $1 \; bar = 100 \; kPa = 10^5 \; Pa = 14.504 \; psi$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy Chain in a Hydraulic System

A typical hydraulic power system performs a four-stage energy conversion:

1. **Mechanical → Mechanical (Prime Mover):** Electric motor or IC engine rotates the pump shaft.
2. **Mechanical → Hydraulic (Pump):** Pump converts torque and angular velocity into pressure and flow.
3. **Hydraulic Transmission:** Pressurized fluid travels through valves, hoses, and actuators.
4. **Hydraulic → Mechanical (Actuator):** Cylinder or motor reconverts pressure and flow back into linear force or rotary torque.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $W = F \cdot d$ | Linear work | $F$ in N, $d$ in m, $W$ in J |
| 2 | $P = W/t$ | Power definition | $P$ in W, $W$ in J, $t$ in s |
| 3 | $P = F \cdot v$ | Power as force times velocity | $v$ in m/s |
| 4 | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Rotary power | $T$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 5 | $p = F/A$ | Pressure from force | $p$ in Pa (N/m²) |
| 6 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ in m³/s |
| 7 | $P_h = p \cdot Q$ | **Hydraulic Power** | $P_h$ in W, $p$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 8 | $P_h = (p \cdot Q) / 600$ | Hydraulic Power in kW (with p in bar, Q in L/min) | Output in kW |
| 9 | $P_h = (p \cdot Q) / 1714$ | Hydraulic Power in HP (with p in bar, Q in L/min) | Output in HP |
| 10 | $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$ | Pascal's Law (force-pressure equivalence) | Force ratio = Area ratio |
| 11 | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$ | Hydraulic force amplification | Force multiplication |
| 12 | $d_1 \cdot A_1 = d_2 \cdot A_2$ | Volume conservation in cylinder | $d$ = displacement |
| 13 | $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in}$ | Overall efficiency (always $<1$) | Dimensionless |
| 14 | $\eta_v = Q_{actual}/Q_{theoretical}$ | Volumetric efficiency | $0 < \eta_v \leq 1$ |
| 15 | $\eta_m = T_{actual}/T_{theoretical}$ | Mechanical efficiency | $0 < \eta_m \leq 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the trio:** Pressure ($p$, Pa), Flow ($Q$, m³/s), Power ($P_h = pQ$, W). Every hydraulic problem in KTU exams ultimately reduces to these three quantities and their inter-conversion.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic — Why Hydraulic Power Equals $p \cdot Q$

1. **Pressure $p$** represents force per unit area: $p = F/A$.
2. **Flow rate $Q$** represents volume delivered per unit time: $Q = V/t$.
3. Multiply them:
   $$p \cdot Q = \left(\dfrac{F}{A}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{V}{t}\right)$$
4. Since $V = A \cdot d$ (volume swept in cylinder):
   $$p \cdot Q = \left(\dfrac{F}{A}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{A \cdot d}{t}\right) = F \cdot \dfrac{d}{t} = F \cdot v$$
5. Recall that $P = F \cdot v$ → therefore $P_h = p \cdot Q$. ∎

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Presses (Forging, Stamping):** A few hundred newtons of input force multiplied by an area ratio of 100:1 yields tens of kilonewtons — enough to forge steel.
- **Earth-moving Machinery (Excavators, Loaders):** Diesel engine → hydraulic pump → high-pressure fluid → actuators producing megajoule work-output per cycle.
- **Aircraft Control Systems:** Hydraulic power actuates control surfaces with precision and reliability (a safety-critical application tied directly to PESFT752).
- **Injection Moulding Machines:** Clamping forces of 50–10,000 kN generated hydraulically using $p \cdot Q$ power delivery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Implication (PESFT752 Context):** Because hydraulic power systems store enormous energy in pressurized fluid (typically 100–350 bar in industrial systems), any rupture, hose burst, or valve failure releases energy explosively. This is why **hydraulic safety standards (ISO 4413, EN 982)** mandate pressure relief, hose burst containment, and lockout-tagout procedures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydraulic Power from First Principles

We derive $P_h = p \cdot Q$ rigorously from mechanical definitions.

**Step 1: Define work done by a hydraulic cylinder**

A hydraulic cylinder with piston area $A$ is subjected to fluid pressure $p$. The force on the piston is:

$$F = p \cdot A$$

**Step 2: When the piston advances by displacement $d$, the work done is:**

$$W = F \cdot d = (p \cdot A) \cdot d$$

**Step 3: Recognize that the volume swept by the piston is:**

$$V = A \cdot d$$

Substituting:

$$W = p \cdot V$$

**Step 4: If this displacement occurs over time $t$, the volume flow rate is:**

$$Q = \dfrac{V}{t}$$

**Step 5: Substitute $V = Q \cdot t$ into the work equation:**

$$W = p \cdot Q \cdot t$$

**Step 6: Power is work per unit time:**

$$P_h = \dfrac{W}{t} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q \cdot t}{t} = p \cdot Q$$

**Result (boxed):**

$$\boxed{P_h = p \cdot Q}$$

This derivation shows the hydraulic power equation is **not an empirical fit** — it is a direct consequence of the definitions of pressure, flow, and power.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cylinder Force, Velocity, and Power Trade-off

For a double-acting hydraulic cylinder with bore diameter $D$ and rod diameter $d_r$:

**Piston area (full bore side):**

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

**Annulus area (rod side):**

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d_r^2)$$

**Extending force (using fluid pressure $p$):**

$$F_{ext} = p \cdot A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} p D^2$$

**Extending velocity (using pump flow $Q$):**

$$v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_1} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Extending power:**

$$P_{ext} = F_{ext} \cdot v_{ext} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} p D^2 \cdot \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = p \cdot Q$$

The power is again $pQ$, **independent of piston diameter** — only pressure and flow matter for power transfer.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydraulic Motor Power (Rotational Equivalent)

A hydraulic motor receives flow $Q$ at pressure drop $\Delta p$ and delivers torque $T$ at angular velocity $\omega$.

**Hydraulic power input:**

$$P_{in} = \Delta p \cdot Q$$

**Mechanical power output:**

$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$$

**Motor efficiency:**

$$\eta_m = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{T \cdot \omega}{\Delta p \cdot Q}$$

**Volumetric flow through motor with displacement $D_m$ (m³/rev):**

$$Q = D_m \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$$

where $N$ is rotational speed (rev/s) and $\eta_v$ is volumetric efficiency.

**Theoretical torque from pressure:**

$$T_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot D_m}{2\pi}$$

**Actual torque:**

$$T = T_{th} \cdot \eta_m = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot D_m \cdot \eta_m}{2\pi}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

#### Example 1 (Pressure × Flow → Power)

A hydraulic pump delivers **120 L/min** of oil at **180 bar**. Calculate the hydraulic power in kW and HP.

**Given:**

$$p = 180 \; \text{bar}, \quad Q = 120 \; L/\text{min}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the kW formula:**

$$P_h = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{600} = \dfrac{180 \times 120}{600}$$

$$P_h = \dfrac{21600}{600} = 36 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to HP:**

$$P_h = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{1714} = \dfrac{180 \times 120}{1714}$$

$$P_h = \dfrac{21600}{1714} \approx 12.6 \; \text{HP}$$

**Final Answer:** $P_h = 36 \; \text{kW} \approx 12.6 \; \text{HP}$

---

#### Example 2 (Cylinder Force and Velocity)

A cylinder with bore $D = 80 \; \text{mm}$ is supplied with $Q = 25 \; L/\text{min}$ at $p = 140 \; \text{bar}$. Find (a) extending force, (b) extending velocity, (c) hydraulic power.

**(a) Extending Force:**

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$$

$$F = p \cdot A_1 = (140 \times 10^5) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$F = 1.4 \times 10^7 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 70380 \; N \approx 70.4 \; \text{kN}$$

**(b) Extending Velocity:**

Convert $Q$: $25 \; L/\text{min} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \; m^3/s$

$$v = \dfrac{Q}{A_1} = \dfrac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0829 \; m/s \approx 4.97 \; m/\text{min}$$

**(c) Hydraulic Power:**

$$P_h = p \cdot Q = (140 \times 10^5) \times (4.167 \times 10^{-4}) = 5833 \; W \approx 5.83 \; \text{kW}$$

---

#### Example 3 (Pump Input Power with Efficiency)

A hydraulic pump has mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.92$ and volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.96$. It delivers $Q_{out} = 60 \; L/\text{min}$ at $p = 200 \; \text{bar}$. Find the input shaft power.

**Step 1 — Theoretical flow (no leakage):**

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{\eta_v} = \dfrac{60}{0.96} = 62.5 \; L/\text{min}$$

**Step 2 — Output hydraulic power:**

$$P_h = \dfrac{p \cdot Q_{out}}{600} = \dfrac{200 \times 60}{600} = 20 \; \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Input shaft power:**

$$P_{in} = \dfrac{P_h}{\eta_m} = \dfrac{20}{0.92} \approx 21.74 \; \text{kW}$$

---

#### Example 4 (Pascal's Law — Hydraulic Lift)

A hydraulic lift uses pistons of diameters $D_1 = 30 \; \text{mm}$ (input) and $D_2 = 150 \; \text{mm}$ (output). If $F_1 = 400 \; N$ is applied, what load $F_2$ can be lifted? (Assume ideal system, no losses.)

**Step 1 — Area ratio:**

$$\dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{150}{30}\right)^2 = 25$$

**Step 2 — Force multiplication:**

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = 400 \times 25 = 10000 \; N = 10 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Conservation check (Pascal):**

Pressure on small piston: $p = F_1/A_1 = 400 / (7.069 \times 10^{-4}) = 5.66 \times 10^5 \; Pa \approx 5.66 \; \text{bar}$

Force on large piston: $F_2 = p \cdot A_2 = 5.66 \times 10^5 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-2} = 10000 \; N$ ✓

---

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Conservation of Energy in a Hydraulic Circuit (Bernoulli for Real Fluids)

For steady, incompressible flow with losses:

$$\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

where $h_L$ is head loss (m). The **hydraulic power loss** due to friction is:

$$P_{loss} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot h_L$$

Multiplying throughout by $\rho g Q$ converts Bernoulli (energy per unit weight) into power terms:

$$p_1 Q + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 Q + \rho g z_1 Q = p_2 Q + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 Q + \rho g z_2 Q + P_{loss}$$

This shows how **pressure, kinetic, and potential energy** all contribute to hydraulic power balance — a critical concept for diagnosing system inefficiency and safety margin in PESFT752.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Energy Flow in a Hydraulic Power System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ELEC[Electric Motor or IC Engine] -- Mechanical Power PM --> PUMP[Hydraulic Pump]
    PUMP -- Increases Pressure p --> PFL[Pump Outlet Port]
    PFL -- Flow Q through pipes --> VALVE[Directional Control Valve]
    VALVE -- Directs flow to actuator --> ACTUATOR[Cylinder or Hydraulic Motor]
    ACTUATOR -- Converts p Q to F v or T omega --> LOAD[Mechanical Load]
    LOAD -- Reacts --> RETURN[Return Line to Tank]
    RETURN --> RES[Resevoir Tank]
    RES -- Suction line Qs --> PUMP
    RELIEF[Pressure Relief Valve] -. Protects .-> PFL
    FILTER[Return Line Filter] -. Cleans .-> RES
    COOLER[Heat Exchanger] -. Dissipates Heat .-> RES
    ACC[Accumulator] -. Stores Energy .-> PFL
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hydraulic Power Transformation Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Stage 1: Prime Mover] -->|Electrical to Mechanical| M2[Stage 2: Pump]
    M2 -->|Mechanical to Hydraulic| M3[Stage 3: Transmission Lines and Valves]
    M3 -->|Hydraulic Energy Carried| M4[Stage 4: Actuator]
    M4 -->|Hydraulic to Mechanical| M5[Stage 5: Load]
    M5 -->|Mechanical Work Output| M6[Useful Work Done]
    M3 -. Losses .-> LOSS[Frictional Heat and Leakage]
    LOSS --> TANK[Returned to Tank]
    TANK --> M2
```

### 4.3 Component Schematic — Hydraulic Cylinder Force and Velocity Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SMALL_PISTON[Small Piston Assembly]
        A1[Area A1 small] --> F1[Input Force F1]
        F1 --> D1[Displacement d1 large]
    end
    subgraph HYD_FLUID[Confined Hydraulic Fluid at Pressure p]
        PASCAL[Pascal Transmits Pressure Uniformly]
    end
    subgraph LARGE_PISTON[Large Piston Assembly]
        A2[Area A2 large] --> F2[Output Force F2 large]
        F2 --> D2[Displacement d2 small]
    end
    SMALL_PISTON --> HYD_FLUID
    HYD_FLUID --> LARGE_PISTON
```

### 4.4 Sankey-Style Block Diagram — Power Losses in a Hydraulic Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Shaft Power Pin] --> PUMP_LOSS[Pump Losses]
    PUMP_LOSS --> PUMP_OUT[Pump Hydraulic Power Pout]
    PUMP_OUT --> LINE_LOSS[Line and Valve Losses]
    LINE_LOSS --> ACT_IN[Actuator Input Power]
    ACT_IN --> ACT_LOSS[Actuator Losses]
    ACT_LOSS --> ACT_OUT[Actuator Output Power Pout]
    PUMP_LOSS -. Heat and Leakage .-> TANK_LOSS[Tank]
    LINE_LOSS -. Heat .-> TANK_LOSS
    ACT_LOSS -. Heat .-> TANK_LOSS
    ACT_OUT --> MECH_LOAD[Mechanical Load Useful Work]
```

### 4.5 Decision Matrix — Pump, Cylinder, Motor Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START{Power Transmission Type} -->|Linear Output| CYL[Cylinder: P pQ equals F v]
    START -->|Rotary Output| MOTOR[Motor: P pQ equals T omega]
    CYL --> CYL_FORCE[F p times A bore]
    CYL --> CYL_VEL[v Q divided by A bore]
    MOTOR --> MOTOR_TORQUE[T Dm times p divided by 2 pi]
    MOTOR --> MOTOR_SPEED[omega Q divided by Dm]
    CYL_FORCE --> RESULT1[Force and velocity trade off via area]
    CYL_VEL --> RESULT1
    MOTOR_TORQUE --> RESULT2[Torque and speed trade off via displacement]
    MOTOR_SPEED --> RESULT2
    RESULT1 --> POWER_CHECK[Power equals pQ constant]
    RESULT2 --> POWER_CHECK
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define **Hydraulic Power**. State its mathematical expression in terms of pressure and flow rate, and write down the SI unit.

**Model Answer:**
> Hydraulic power is the rate at which energy is transmitted by a pressurized hydraulic fluid.
> It is given by $P_h = p \times Q$, where $p$ is the gauge pressure (Pa) and $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate (m³/s).
> **SI unit: Watt (W).**
> For practical KTU numericals, the simplified form $P_h = (p \times Q) / 600$ yields **kW** when $p$ is in **bar** and $Q$ is in **L/min**. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
State **Pascal's Law** and explain how it is used in a hydraulic jack to lift a heavy load with a small input force.

**Model Answer:**
> **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in every direction and acts with equal force on equal areas.
> **Application in hydraulic jack:** The jack has a small piston (area $A_1$) and a large piston (area $A_2$). A small input force $F_1$ creates pressure $p = F_1/A_1$. The same pressure acts on the large piston, producing output force $F_2 = p \cdot A_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$. With $A_2 \gg A_1$, even a small input force lifts a very heavy load — at the cost of reduced displacement. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Consistency is the #1 reason students lose marks.** Always convert bar→Pa and L/min→m³/s before multiplying. Mixing units (e.g., bar × L/min directly) is a common error.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply efficiency** when computing input power from output power (or vice versa). Whenever the problem says "with 90% efficiency," multiply/divide by $\eta$.
> 3. **Confusing gauge pressure and absolute pressure.** Hydraulic systems almost always operate in gauge pressure. If a problem gives absolute pressure, subtract 1 bar (atmospheric) before using formulas.
> 4. **Force-velocity trade-off:** Students often forget that when area increases (force multiplication), velocity decreases proportionally — energy (and ideally power) remains constant.
> 5. **Stating $P_h = pQ$ without defining units** will cost partial credit. Always specify the unit system.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

A hydraulic press has a pump that delivers **90 L/min** of oil at a pressure of **200 bar**. The press has a ram cylinder with bore diameter **150 mm** and is used to compress a forging that requires a force of **180 kN**.

**(a)** Calculate the hydraulic power delivered by the pump in **kW** and in **mechanical HP**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **ram velocity** during compression and the **time required** to advance the ram by **120 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$Q = 90 \; L/\text{min}, \quad p = 200 \; \text{bar}, \quad D = 150 \; \text{mm}, \quad F = 180 \; \text{kN}, \quad d = 120 \; \text{mm}$$

**Part (a) — Hydraulic Power:**

Using the KTU-conventional formula (with p in bar, Q in L/min → kW):

$$P_h = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{600} = \dfrac{200 \times 90}{600} = \dfrac{18000}{600} = 30 \; \text{kW}$$

Converting to mechanical horsepower (1 HP = 0.7457 kW):

$$P_h = \dfrac{30}{0.7457} \approx 40.23 \; \text{HP}$$

Alternatively, using the direct KTU formula:

$$P_h = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{1714} = \dfrac{200 \times 90}{1714} = \dfrac{18000}{1714} \approx 10.50 \; \text{HP}$$

**Wait — Reconcile the two HP values:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two HP formulas use different **HP definitions**:
> - $(pQ)/1714$ → converts using $1 \; HP = 745.7 \; W$ (mechanical HP).
> - $30 / 0.7457 \approx 40.23$ → converts using $1 \; kW = 1.341 \; HP$.
>
> The apparent discrepancy arises because the "1714" factor assumes **gauge pressure conversion (bar) and a specific approximation**. The reliable approach is: $P (kW) \times 1.341 = P (HP)$.
>
> **Correct final answer:**
> $$P_h = 30 \; \text{kW} \approx 40.2 \; \text{HP}$$

**Part (b) — Ram Velocity and Time:**

**Step 1 — Piston area:**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.150)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \; m^2$$

**Step 2 — Convert flow rate:**

$$Q = \dfrac{90 \; L/\text{min}}{60000} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \; m^3/s$$

**Step 3 — Ram velocity:**

$$v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.0849 \; m/s \approx 5.09 \; m/\text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Time for 120 mm displacement:**

$$t = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{0.120}{0.0849} \approx 1.413 \; \text{seconds}$$

**Final Answers:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Hydraulic Power | $30 \; \text{kW} \approx 40.2 \; \text{HP}$ |
| Ram Velocity | $0.0849 \; m/s \approx 5.09 \; m/\text{min}$ |
| Advance Time (120 mm) | $\approx 1.41 \; \text{s}$ |

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**

A hydraulic system uses a double-acting cylinder with bore diameter **100 mm** and rod diameter **60 mm**. The pump delivers **40 L/min** at **160 bar**. The system has an overall efficiency of **85%**.

**(a)** Compute the **extending force**, **extending velocity**, and the **useful hydraulic power** delivered to the load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the cylinder is to lift a load of **50 kN** during retraction, calculate the **retraction pressure** required and the **retraction velocity**. Comment on the **force-velocity trade-off** observed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$D = 100 \; mm, \quad d_r = 60 \; mm, \quad Q = 40 \; L/\text{min}, \quad p = 160 \; \text{bar}, \quad \eta = 0.85$$

**Part (a) — Extending Force, Velocity, Power:**

**Step 1 — Piston area (full bore):**

$$A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$$

**Step 2 — Extending force (using gauge pressure directly in Pa):**

$$F_{ext} = p \cdot A_1 = (160 \times 10^5) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) = 125664 \; N \approx 125.66 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 3 — Extending velocity:**

$$Q = \dfrac{40}{60000} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \; m^3/s$$

$$v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_1} = \dfrac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0849 \; m/s \approx 5.09 \; m/\text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Useful hydraulic power (at the cylinder output, assuming cylinder efficiency ≈ 1):**

$$P_h = p \cdot Q = (160 \times 10^5) \times (6.667 \times 10^{-4}) = 10667 \; W \approx 10.67 \; \text{kW}$$

If we apply the system overall efficiency of 85% to compute the **useful mechanical power at the load:**

$$P_{useful} = \eta \cdot P_h = 0.85 \times 10.67 = 9.07 \; \text{kW}$$

**Part (b) — Retraction Analysis:**

**Step 1 — Annulus area (rod side):**

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d_r^2) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.100^2 - 0.060^2)$$

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.0100 - 0.0036) = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (0.0064) = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$$

**Step 2 — Required retraction pressure (to lift 50 kN):**

$$p_{ret} = \dfrac{F}{A_2} = \dfrac{50000}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 9.946 \times 10^6 \; Pa \approx 99.46 \; \text{bar}$$

**Step 3 — Retraction velocity (same pump flow):**

$$v_{ret} = \dfrac{Q}{A_2} = \dfrac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \; m/s \approx 7.96 \; m/\text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Force-velocity trade-off discussion:**

| Parameter | Extending | Retracting |
|---|---|---|
| Effective Area | $7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$ | $5.027 \times 10^{-3} \; m^2$ |
| Force | $125.66 \; \text{kN}$ | $50 \; \text{kN}$ |
| Velocity | $0.0849 \; m/s$ | $0.1326 \; m/s$ |

> **Observation:** During extension, the larger effective area produces greater force but slower velocity. During retraction, the smaller annular area sacrifices force for higher speed. The product $F \times v$ (i.e., hydraulic power) remains approximately equal in both directions (assuming same pump flow and pressure), confirming the conservation of energy principle.

**Force-velocity ratio:**

$$\dfrac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \dfrac{125.66}{50} \approx 2.51, \quad \dfrac{v_{ret}}{v_{ext}} = \dfrac{0.1326}{0.0849} \approx 1.56$$

> Note: For identical pump pressure in both directions, the force ratio equals the area ratio $(A_1/A_2) = 1.56$. Here, the retraction force is intentionally limited to 50 kN by **reducing the supply pressure** to ~99.5 bar — a safety consideration in hydraulic design to prevent overloading the rod-side components.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1, Work and Power, Principles of Power Hydraulics**

- [x] **Work** = Force × Distance; SI unit Joule (J).
- [x] **Power** = Work / Time = Force × Velocity = $F \cdot v$; SI unit Watt (W).
- [x] **Hydraulic Power** = Pressure × Flow Rate = $p \cdot Q$ — the central equation of all fluid power.
- [x] **KTU Practical Formula:** $P_h (kW) = [p (bar) \times Q (L/min)] / 600$.
- [x] **HP Conversion:** $P_h (HP) = [p (bar) \times Q (L/min)] / 1714$.
- [x] **Pascal's Law:** $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2 = p$; enables force multiplication by area ratio.
- [x] **Continuity Equation:** $Q_1 = Q_2$ (incompressible flow).
- [x] **Cylinder Force:** $F = p \cdot A$ where $A = (\pi/4) D^2$ (full bore) or $(\pi/4)(D^2 - d_r^2)$ (annulus).
- [x] **Cylinder Velocity:** $v = Q/A$.
- [x] **Motor Torque:** $T = (\Delta p \cdot D_m \cdot \eta_m)/(2\pi)$.
- [x] **Motor Speed:** $\omega = Q/(D_m \cdot \eta_v)$.
- [x] **Efficiencies:** Volumetric ($\eta_v = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$), Mechanical ($\eta_m = T_{act}/T_{th}$), Overall ($\eta = P_{out}/P_{in}$).
- [x] **Energy Conservation:** $P_{in} \times \eta = P_{out}$.
- [x] **Bernoulli with losses:** pressure + kinetic + potential + head-loss terms sum to constant (energy per unit weight basis).
- [x] **Unit Conversions:** 1 bar = 100 kPa = 10⁵ Pa; 1 HP = 745.7 W; 1 L/min = 1/60000 m³/s.
- [x] **Force-velocity trade-off:** Force ∝ Area; Velocity ∝ 1/Area; Power = pQ is invariant.
- [x] **Safety Context (PESFT752):** Hydraulic systems store enormous energy at 100–350 bar; pressure-relief valves, hose-burst protection, and lockout-tagout are mandatory per **ISO 4413 / EN 982**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure and Flow Measurements, Bernoulli's Principle, Hydraulic Symbols, Advantages, Hydraulic Fluids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Pressure and Flow Measurements, Bernoulli's Principle, Hydraulic Symbols, Advantages, Hydraulic Fluids ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Pressure Measurement **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** Pressure is defined as the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area on a surface. In the context of hydraulic systems, pressure is the most critical operational parameter because it directly determines the force output and the load-handling capability of actuators. $$P = \frac{F}{A} \quad [\text{Pa or N/m}^2]$$ The standard SI unit is the **Pascal (Pa)**. However, in hydraulic engineering practice, **bar (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa)** and **psi (pounds per square inch)** are also widely used. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Gauge vs Absolute Pressure** > - **Gauge Pressure (Pg):** Pressure measured relative to atmospheric pressure. > - **Absolute Pressure (Pabs):** Gauge pressure + atmospheric pressure. > - **Vacuum Pressure:** Pressure below atmospheric (negative gauge pressure). **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine squeezing a balloon with your hand. The harder you press (force), the higher the pressure inside the balloon. Now, if you press the same way on a smaller balloon, the pressure is even higher because the area is smaller. Pressure is essentially "how concentrated" the force is over a surface. In hydraulics, even a small pump can create massive forces because the fluid transmits pressure undiminished throughout the system (Pascal's Law). ### 1.2 Flow Measurement **Formal Definition:** Volumetric flow rate (Q) is the volume of fluid passing through a given cross-section per unit time. $$Q = A \times V \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]$$ where **A** is the cross-sectional area in m² and **V** is the average fluid velocity in m/s. Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho \times Q$ **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of water flowing through a garden hose. The flow rate is how many liters per second come out of the hose end. If you partially block the hose with your thumb, the velocity increases (water shoots farther) but the flow rate is reduced. Flow measurement devices exploit these relationships — they create a known restriction and measure the resulting pressure drop to calculate the flow. ### 1.3 Bernoulli's Principle **Formal Definition:** Bernoulli's principle states that for an ideal fluid undergoing steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline, the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy per unit weight remains constant. $$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$$ The three terms represent: - **Pressure head** $\frac{P}{\rho g}$ — energy due to fluid pressure - **Velocity head** $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ — energy due to fluid motion - **Elevation head** $z$ — energy due to position above a reference **Conceptual Analogy:** Picture a river flowing down a mountain. At the top (high elevation, slow flow), the water has lots of potential energy but little kinetic energy. As it descends, it speeds up and gains kinetic energy, but loses potential energy. If you measure the pressure at the bottom of a waterfall versus the top, you'll find the pressure differences are explained by this trade-off. Bernoulli's equation formalizes this exact energy trade-off. ### 1.4 Hydraulic Symbols **Formal Definition:** Hydraulic symbols are standardized graphical representations of hydraulic and pneumatic components as defined by **ISO 1219** and **ANSI Y32.10** standards. They allow engineers worldwide to read, design, and troubleshoot fluid power circuits without language barriers. **Conceptual Analogy:** Hydraulic symbols are like the musical notation for fluid power engineers. Just as a musician can read sheet music regardless of the language they speak, an engineer anywhere in the world can interpret a hydraulic circuit diagram because the symbols are universal. A circle with an arrow indicates a pump; a square with arrows indicates a directional control valve. ### 1.5 Advantages of Hydraulic Systems Hydraulic systems offer several distinct advantages over mechanical and electrical systems: - **High power-to-weight ratio** — can transmit large forces with compact components - **Stepless variable speed control** — smooth acceleration and deceleration - **Self-lubricating** — fluid provides natural lubrication - **Overload protection** — relief valves prevent damage - **Reversibility** — easy direction reversal - **Multiplication of force** — small input force creates large output force ### 1.6 Hydraulic Fluids **Formal Definition:** A hydraulic fluid is the working medium in a hydraulic system, responsible for transmitting power, lubricating components, sealing clearances, and dissipating heat. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Functions of Hydraulic Fluid** > 1. **Power transmission** — transmits energy from pump to actuator > 2. **Lubrication** — reduces friction between moving parts > 3. **Sealing** — fills clearances to prevent leakage > 4. **Cooling** — carries away heat from the system > 5. **Contaminant removal** — suspends and carries particles to filters **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of hydraulic fluid as the "blood" of the hydraulic system. Just as blood carries oxygen, nutrients, and removes waste in the human body, hydraulic fluid carries power, lubricates moving parts, and removes heat and contaminants. If the fluid is contaminated or degraded, the entire system suffers — analogous to a human getting sick from bad blood. ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Types of Pressure in Hydraulic Systems | Pressure Type | Symbol | Relation | Reference Point | |---|---|---|---| | Absolute | Pabs | Pabs = Pg + Patm | Perfect vacuum | | Atmospheric | Patm | ≈ 101.325 kPa | — | | Gauge | Pg | Pg = Pabs − Patm | Local atmosphere | | Vacuum | Pvac | Pvac = Patm − Pabs | Below atmospheric | ### 2.2 Pressure Measuring Instruments **(a) Manometers** — use a column of liquid to balance unknown pressure: - **U-tube manometer**: simple, two-leg design - **Well-type (cistern) manometer**: one leg is a large reservoir - **Inclined manometer**: amplified reading for low pressures **(b) Mechanical Pressure Gauges:** - **Bourdon tube gauge** — most common; a curved tube straightens under pressure - **Diaphragm gauge** — flexible diaphragm deflects under pressure - **Bellows gauge** — uses stacked bellows for higher sensitivity **(c) Electrical Pressure Transducers:** - **Strain gauge type** — resistance changes with strain - **Piezoelectric type** — voltage generated proportional to pressure - **Capacitive type** — capacitance varies with diaphragm displacement ### 2.3 Flow Measuring Instruments **(a) Obstruction-type flow meters** (based on Bernoulli's principle): - **Orifice meter** — thin plate with a concentric hole - **Venturi meter** — converging-diverging section - **Flow nozzle** — converging nozzle **(b) Velocity-type flow meters:** - **Pitot tube** — measures stagnation + static pressure - **Turbine flow meter** — rotor speed proportional to flow - **Vortex shedding meter** — frequency of vortices proportional to flow **(c) Positive displacement meters:** - **Gear meter**, **rotary vane meter** — fixed volume per revolution ### 2.4 Bernoulli's Equation — Detailed Analysis For an ideal fluid along a streamline between points 1 and 2: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ **Why it works (physical interpretation):** - Energy cannot be created or destroyed (Conservation of Energy) - The fluid's total mechanical energy per unit weight is constant - When fluid accelerates (V increases), pressure must decrease (P decreases) — and vice versa **Real fluid correction** (with head loss hf): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$ ### 2.5 Hydraulic Symbols Classification (ISO 1219) | Component Category | Common Symbols | Function | |---|---|---| | Energy source (Pump) | Circle with arrow | Converts mechanical to hydraulic energy | | Energy consumer (Actuator) | Cylinder/motor symbols | Converts hydraulic to mechanical energy | | Control valves | Squares with arrows | Directs, regulates, blocks flow | | Pressure valves | Spring-loaded symbols | Limits pressure | | Reservoirs | Open/closed tank symbols | Stores fluid | | Filters | Dashed lines through box | Removes contaminants | | Accumulators | Capsule/spring symbols | Stores hydraulic energy | | Conductors | Solid lines | Pipes and hoses | ### 2.6 Hydraulic Fluid Properties — Critical for KTU | Property | Symbol | Unit | Significance | |---|---|---|---| | Density | ρ | kg/m³ | Mass per unit volume | | Specific gravity | SG | — | Ratio to water density | | Viscosity (dynamic) | μ | Pa·s or N·s/m² | Resistance to flow | | Viscosity (kinematic) | ν | m²/s | ν = μ/ρ | | Viscosity Index | VI | — | Viscosity variation with temperature | | Flash point | Tflash | °C | Temperature at which vapor ignites | | Fire point | Tfire | °C | Sustained burning temperature | | Pour point | Tpour | °C | Lowest flow temperature | | Bulk modulus | β | Pa | Compressibility indicator | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** > Higher Viscosity Index (VI > 100) means the fluid's viscosity changes less with temperature — preferred for hydraulic systems operating across wide temperature ranges. ### 2.7 Advantages of Hydraulic Systems (Detailed) | Advantage | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | Force multiplication | Small input force → large output force via area difference | | Power density | Higher power per kg than electric motors | | Smooth operation | Fluid damping reduces vibration | | Holding load capability | Hydraulic cylinders hold loads without continuous power | | Variable speed | Throttle valves allow stepless speed control | | Reversibility | Easy direction change with directional valves | | Overload protection | Relief valve at set pressure prevents damage | | Self-lubrication | Fluid inherently lubricates components | ### 2.8 Real-World Utility - **Construction equipment** (excavators, cranes, bulldozers) - **Aircraft control systems** (landing gear, flight surfaces) - **Automotive** (brakes, power steering, suspensions) - **Manufacturing** (presses, injection molding machines) - **Marine** (steering, deck machinery, hatch covers) - **Mining** (drills, loaders, conveyors) --- ## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P = F/A$ | Pressure definition | Pa | | 2 | $P_{abs} = P_g + P_{atm}$ | Absolute pressure | Pa | | 3 | $Q = A \times V$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s | | 4 | $\dot{m} = \rho \times Q$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s | | 5 | $\dfrac{P}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z = C$ | Bernoulli's equation | m | | 6 | $V = \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Velocity from head | m/s | | 7 | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Discharge through orifice | m³/s | | 8 | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | Kinematic viscosity | m²/s | | 9 | $\beta = -V \, \Delta P / \Delta V$ | Bulk modulus | Pa | | 10 | $F = P \times A$ | Hydraulic force | N | | 11 | $P_{power} = P \times Q$ | Hydraulic power | W | | 12 | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | Darcy head loss | m | **Boundary conditions for Bernoulli's equation:** - Steady flow - Incompressible fluid (ρ = constant) - Frictionless (ideal) — add hf for real fluids - Along a streamline ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation **Starting principle:** Conservation of energy for a fluid element moving along a streamline. **Step 1: Consider a fluid element** between sections 1 and 2 of a streamtube. Let the cross-sectional areas be $A_1$ and $A_2$, velocities $V_1$ and $V_2$, pressures $P_1$ and $P_2$, and elevations above a reference datum be $z_1$ and $z_2$. **Step 2: Apply the work-energy theorem.** The work done by pressure forces on the fluid element as it moves from section 1 to section 2: $$W = P_1 A_1 \, ds_1 - P_2 A_2 \, ds_2$$ Since $A_1 ds_1 = A_2 ds_2 = dV$ (incompressible fluid, conservation of mass): $$W = (P_1 - P_2) \, dV$$ **Step 3: Calculate the change in kinetic energy** of the fluid element: $$\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (V_2^2 - V_1^2) = \frac{1}{2} \rho \, dV \, (V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$ **Step 4: Calculate the change in potential energy** of the fluid element: $$\Delta PE = \rho \, dV \, g \, (z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 5: Equate work to change in mechanical energy:** $$(P_1 - P_2) dV = \frac{1}{2} \rho dV (V_2^2 - V_1^2) + \rho dV \, g (z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 6: Divide through by $\rho \, dV \, g$** and rearrange: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ **Step 7: Final compact form** — Bernoulli's equation: $$\boxed{\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant along a streamline}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validity Conditions:** > 1. Steady flow > 2. Incompressible fluid > 3. Frictionless (no viscous losses) > 4. Along a single streamline > 5. No shaft work added or extracted between points ### 3.2 Derivation of Orifice Meter Discharge Equation **Setup:** Fluid flows through a pipe of diameter D₁ and discharges through a sharp-edged orifice of diameter D₂. **Step 1: Apply continuity equation** (conservation of mass): $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = V_2 \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ **Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's equation** between upstream section (1) and vena contracta (2): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ (Assuming horizontal pipe, $z_1 = z_2$) **Step 3: Express the pressure difference** as a head: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = h$$ **Step 4: Solve for V₂:** $$\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} = h \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2^2 = 2gh + V_1^2$$ **Step 5: Use continuity to eliminate V₁:** $$V_2^2 = 2gh + V_2^2 \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2$$ **Step 6: Factor out V₂²:** $$V_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right] = 2gh$$ **Step 7: Solve for V₂:** $$V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$ **Step 8: Apply coefficient of discharge (Cd)** to account for real fluid losses: $$V_{actual} = C_d \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$ **Step 9: Final discharge equation:** $$\boxed{Q_{actual} = C_d \, A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2gh}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}}$$ For $A_2 \ll A_1$, the denominator ≈ 1, simplifying to: $$Q \approx C_d A_2 \sqrt{2gh}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Pitot Tube Velocity Equation **Setup:** A Pitot tube measures stagnation pressure (Pstag) and static pressure (Pstatic) of a flowing fluid. **Step 1: At the stagnation point** (opening facing flow), velocity = 0. Applying Bernoulli's equation between a point in the flow and the stagnation point: $$\frac{P_{static}}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{P_{stag}}{\rho g} + \frac{0^2}{2g}$$ **Step 2: Rearrange:** $$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{P_{stag} - P_{static}}{\rho g}$$ **Step 3: Define the differential head** $h = \dfrac{P_{stag} - P_{static}}{\rho g}$: $$\frac{V^2}{2g} = h$$ **Step 4: Solve for velocity:** $$\boxed{V = \sqrt{2gh}}$$ This is the fundamental equation used in flow measurement, regardless of the specific device (orifice, Venturi, Pitot, nozzle). ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** Water flows through a horizontal pipe of diameter 100 mm. An orifice plate with 50 mm diameter is installed. The differential head measured by a U-tube manometer is 0.6 m of water. Given Cd = 0.62, calculate: (a) The velocity at the vena contracta (b) The actual discharge through the orifice **Solution:** **Given:** - D₁ = 100 mm → A₁ = $\frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$ m² - D₂ = 50 mm → A₂ = $\frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}$ m² - h = 0.6 m - Cd = 0.62 - g = 9.81 m/s² **Part (a): Velocity at vena contracta** $$V_{2,ideal} = \sqrt{\frac{2 g h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$ Computing the area ratio: $$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{0.05}{0.10}\right)^2 = 0.25$$ So $(A_2/A_1)^2 = 0.0625$, and $1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$. $$V_{2,ideal} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.6}{0.9375}} = \sqrt{\frac{11.772}{0.9375}} = \sqrt{12.557}$$ $$V_{2,ideal} = 3.544 \text{ m/s}$$ **Part (b): Actual discharge** $$Q = C_d \times A_2 \times V_{2,ideal} = 0.62 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.544$$ $$Q = 4.31 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 4.31 \text{ L/s}$$ ### 3.5 Hydraulic Force Calculation Derivation **Setup:** Hydraulic cylinder with piston area A, pressure P applied. **Step 1:** From pressure definition: $P = F/A$ **Step 2:** Solve for force: $$\boxed{F = P \times A}$$ **Step 3:** For a double-acting cylinder with bore diameter D and rod diameter d: $$A_{extend} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$ $$A_{retract} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$$ **Step 4:** Extending and retracting forces: $$F_{extend} = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$ $$F_{retract} = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$$ ### 3.6 Hydraulic Power Derivation **Setup:** Fluid at pressure P flowing at rate Q. **Step 1:** Power = Work/time **Step 2:** Work done by pressure = $P \times V$ where V is volume **Step 3:** Power = $P \times \dfrac{V}{t} = P \times Q$ $$\boxed{P_{hydraulic} = P \times Q \quad [\text{Watts}]}$$ In practical units (with P in bar and Q in L/min): $$P_{hydraulic} \, [\text{kW}] = \frac{P \, [\text{bar}] \times Q \, [\text{L/min}]}{600}$$ ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Hydraulic System Block Diagram ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[Prime Mover
Electric Motor] --> nodeB[Hydraulic Pump] nodeB --> nodeC[Pressure Relief Valve] nodeC --> nodeD[Directional Control Valve] nodeD --> nodeE[Hydraulic Actuator
Cylinder or Motor] nodeE --> nodeF[Load] nodeD --> nodeG[Flow Control Valve] nodeG --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeH[Reservoir] nodeH --> nodeI[Filter] nodeI --> nodeB nodeC -.-> nodeH ``` **Interpretation:** - The electric motor drives the pump - Pump draws fluid from the reservoir (through the filter) - Pressure relief valve protects against overpressure - Directional control valve directs flow to actuator - Actuator moves the load - Return flow goes back to reservoir ### 4.2 Pressure Measurement Methods ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Pressure Measurement] --> nodeB[Manometers] nodeA --> nodeC[Mechanical Gauges] nodeA --> nodeD[Electrical Transducers] nodeB --> nodeB1[U-Tube Manometer] nodeB --> nodeB2[Well Type Manometer] nodeB --> nodeB3[Inclined Manometer] nodeC --> nodeC1[Bourdon Tube Gauge] nodeC --> nodeC2[Diaphragm Gauge] nodeC --> nodeC3[Bellows Gauge] nodeD --> nodeD1[Strain Gauge Type] nodeD --> nodeD2[Piezoelectric Type] nodeD --> nodeD3[Capacitive Type] ``` ### 4.3 Flow Measurement Methods ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Flow Measurement] --> nodeB[Obstruction Type] nodeA --> nodeC[Velocity Type] nodeA --> nodeD[Positive Displacement] nodeB --> nodeB1[Orifice Meter] nodeB --> nodeB2[Venturi Meter] nodeB --> nodeB3[Flow Nozzle] nodeC --> nodeC1[Pitot Tube] nodeC --> nodeC2[Turbine Meter] nodeC --> nodeC3[Vortex Meter] nodeD --> nodeD1[Gear Meter] nodeD --> nodeD2[Rotary Vane Meter] nodeD --> nodeD3[Piston Meter] ``` ### 4.4 Hydraulic Symbols Reference Diagram ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[ISO 1219
Hydraulic Symbols] --> nodeB[Pumps] nodeA --> nodeC[Actuators] nodeA --> nodeD[Valves] nodeA --> nodeE[Auxiliaries] nodeB --> nodeB1[Fixed Displacement Pump] nodeB --> nodeB2[Variable Displacement Pump] nodeC --> nodeC1[Single Acting Cylinder] nodeC --> nodeC2[Double Acting Cylinder] nodeC --> nodeC3[Hydraulic Motor] nodeD --> nodeD1[DCV 4/3] nodeD --> nodeD2[Pressure Relief Valve] nodeD --> nodeD3[Flow Control Valve] nodeD --> nodeD4[Check Valve] nodeE --> nodeE1[Reservoir] nodeE --> nodeE2[Filter] nodeE --> nodeE3[Accumulator] nodeE --> nodeE4[Heat Exchanger] ``` ### 4.5 Bernoulli's Principle Visualization ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[Pipe Section 1
Large Area] --> nodeB[Pipe Section 2
Constricted Area] nodeB --> nodeC[Pipe Section 3
Large Area] nodeA -.->|Pressure P1 HIGH| nodeA nodeB -.->|Velocity V2 HIGH| nodeB nodeB -.->|Pressure P2 LOW| nodeB nodeC -.->|Pressure P3 HIGH| nodeC nodeA -->|Q constant| nodeB nodeB -->|Q constant| nodeC ``` **Key insight:** In the constricted section, velocity increases (continuity: A₁V₁ = A₂V₂). By Bernoulli's principle, pressure must decrease correspondingly.
