## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**List any three industrial applications of compressed air. Why is compressed air called the "fourth utility"?**

**Model Answer:**

Compressed air is widely used in industry for: (i) **power transmission** in pneumatic cylinders and motors for assembly-line automation, (ii) **process control** in pneumatic control valves that regulate flow, pressure, and temperature in chemical plants, and (iii) **material handling** through vacuum lifting devices and pneumatic conveying of granular solids.

It is called the **"fourth utility"** because, like electricity, water, and natural gas, compressed air is delivered through a permanent distribution network throughout a factory and consumed at multiple points. It is reliable, clean, safe (non-flammable), and indispensable — qualifying it as a utility-grade energy medium.

**Mark Distribution (as per KTU):**
- Concept clarity: 1 mark
- Three applications: 1.5 marks (0.5 each)
- Justification of "fourth utility": 0.5 mark

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between positive displacement and dynamic compressors with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Positive Displacement Compressor | Dynamic Compressor |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Traps fixed mass, then reduces volume mechanically | Accelerates fluid by impeller; converts KE to pressure |
| Pressure variation | Theoretical pressure rise independent of flow | Pressure rise depends strongly on flow rate |
| Flow behaviour | Pulsating flow | Smooth, continuous flow |
| Typical pressure ratio | High (up to 100 in reciprocating) | Moderate (up to 6–8 per stage) |
| **Example** | **Reciprocating piston compressor** | **Centrifugal compressor** |
| Best suited for | High pressure, low flow | High flow, moderate pressure |

**Mark Distribution:** Comparison points: 2 marks (1 each), Example: 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for work done during **polytropic compression** of air from pressure $P_1$ to $P_2$, stating clearly the meaning of each term. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A two-stage single-acting reciprocating compressor compresses **0.05 m³ of air per second** at intake conditions of **1 bar and 27 °C** to a discharge pressure of **25 bar**. The intercooler cools the air back to **27 °C** between stages. Assuming **polytropic compression with $n = 1.3$** and mechanical efficiency of **85 %**, calculate: (i) optimum intercooler pressure, (ii) total work of compression per second, (iii) shaft power required. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** Already covered in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The examiner expects the steps from $W = -\int p\,dV$, substitution of $p = P_1 V_1^n / V^n$, integration, and final expression:

$$
W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1 \!\left[\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]
$$

With $n = 1.3$, $(n-1)/n = 0.3/1.3 = 0.2308$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $V_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar} = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_2 = 25 \text{ bar} = 25 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $T_1 = 27 \text{ °C} = 300 \text{ K}$ (used only for FAD conversion if needed)
- $n = 1.3$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.85$

**(i) Optimum Intercooler Pressure:**

$$
P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2} = \sqrt{1 \times 25} = 5 \text{ bar}
$$

**(ii) Total Work of Compression (with perfect intercooling):**

Each stage starts from $V_1 = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ (since intercooling returns air to $T_1$).

Stage 1 work (per second):

$$
W_1 = \frac{n}{n-1}\,P_1 V_1 \!\left[\left(\frac{P_x}{P_1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]
$$

$$
W_1 = \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times (1 \times 10^5) \times 0.05 \times \left[(5)^{\,0.2308} - 1\right]
$$

$$
W_1 = 4.333 \times 5000 \times \left[5^{\,0.2308} - 1\right]
$$

Computing $5^{\,0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \ln 5} = e^{0.2308 \times 1.6094} = e^{0.3715} = 1.4499$.

$$
W_1 = 4.333 \times 5000 \times (1.4499 - 1) = 21666.67 \times 0.4499 = 9749.6 \text{ J/s}
$$

Stage 2 work (per second): Since $P_x/P_1 = 5$ and $P_2/P_x = 5$ (symmetric), $W_2 = W_1$.

$$
W_2 \approx 9749.6 \text{ J/s}
$$

Total:

$$
W_{total} = W_1 + W_2 = 19499.2 \text{ J/s} \approx 19.5 \text{ kW (work input)}
$$

**(iii) Shaft Power:**

$$
P_{shaft} = \frac{W_{total}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{19499.2}{0.85} = 22940 \text{ W} \approx 22.94 \text{ kW}
$$

**Alternative check (single-stage for comparison):**

$$
W_{single} = \frac{1.3}{0.3} \times 5000 \times \left[(25)^{\,0.2308} - 1\right]
$$

$25^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times \ln 25} = e^{0.2308 \times 3.2189} = e^{0.7430} = 2.1023$.

$$
W_{single} = 21666.67 \times 1.1023 = 23882.6 \text{ J/s} \approx 23.88 \text{ kW}
$$

**Savings from two-stage:** $(23882.6 - 19499.2)/23882.6 = 18.35\%$ — confirms significant efficiency gain.

**Mark Distribution (per KTU 2024 valuation key):**
- (a) Derivation: 7 marks (assumptions 1, integration 3, final form 2, n=1 substitution 1)
- (b-i) Optimum pressure: 1 mark
- (b-ii) Work calculation: 4 marks (formula 1, stage 1 evaluation 1.5, stage 2 evaluation 1, total 0.5)
- (b-iii) Shaft power: 2 marks (formula 0.5, substitution 1, final answer 0.5)

---

#### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **components of a complete compressed air system** used in industry. Describe the role of the **air receiver, aftercooler, and FRL unit**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A single-stage reciprocating compressor has a **cylinder bore of 150 mm, stroke of 200 mm, clearance volume of 200 cm³**, and runs at **300 rpm**. It compresses air polytropically with $n = 1.3$ from **1.013 bar to 7 bar**. Calculate: (i) swept volume per second, (ii) volumetric efficiency, (iii) free air delivery (FAD) at intake conditions. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Compressed Air System Components** — refer to the **Block Diagram in Section 4.2** of these notes.

**Roles of Key Components:**

**Air Receiver Tank** — A horizontal/vertical pressure vessel that:
- Stores compressed air to **buffer demand peaks** and decouple compressor cycling.
- Provides volume for **moisture condensation** (acts as primary water separator).
- Stabilises line pressure fluctuations.
- Sized typically to hold **1 to 10 minutes** of compressor capacity.

**Aftercooler** — A heat exchanger placed immediately downstream of the compressor discharge that:
- Removes the **heat of compression** (typically cools air to within **8–10 °C** of ambient).
- Causes moisture to condense out before the air enters the receiver.
- Protects downstream components (dryer, filters) from thermal damage.

**FRL Unit (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator)** — A compact conditioning assembly installed near point-of-use that:
- **Filter:** Removes solid particulates and entrained water/oil aerosols (typically down to 5–40 µm).
- **Regulator:** Maintains constant downstream pressure regardless of upstream fluctuations or flow variations.
- **Lubricator:** Adds controlled fine oil mist to the airstream for pneumatic motor/tool lubrication.

**Mark Distribution (part a):** Diagram 3 marks, Roles 4 marks (1.5 each for receiver and aftercooler, 1 for FRL).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Bore $D = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- Stroke $L = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Clearance volume $V_c = 200 \text{ cm}^3 = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$
- $N = 300 \text{ rpm}$
- $P_1 = 1.013 \text{ bar}$, $P_2 = 7 \text{ bar}$, $n = 1.3$

**(i) Swept Volume per Second:**

Swept volume per cycle:

$$
V_s = \frac{\pi}{4}\,D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.15)^2 \times 0.2
$$

$$
V_s = 0.7854 \times 0.0225 \times 0.2 = 3.534 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \text{ per cycle}
$$

Number of working strokes per second (single-acting, 1 stroke per revolution):

$$
\text{Strokes/sec} = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{300}{60} = 5 \text{ strokes/s}
$$

$$
V_s/\text{sec} = 3.534 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(ii) Volumetric Efficiency:**

Clearance ratio:

$$
C = \frac{V_c}{V_s} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{3.534 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0566
$$

Pressure ratio:

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{7}{1.013} = 6.910
$$

Polytropic exponent for volumetric efficiency: $1/n = 1/1.3 = 0.7692$.

$$
\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} = (6.910)^{0.7692}
$$

Compute: $\ln(6.910) = 1.9329$, multiplied by 0.7692 = 1.4868, exponentiated: $e^{1.4868} = 4.425$.

$$
\eta_v = 1 + C - C\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} = 1 + 0.0566 - 0.0566 \times 4.425
$$

$$
\eta_v = 1.0566 - 0.2505 = 0.8061 = 80.61\%
$$

**(iii) Free Air Delivery (FAD) per Second:**

$$
Q_{FAD} = \eta_v \times V_s/\text{sec} = 0.8061 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
Q_{FAD} = 1.425 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to m³/min:

$$
Q_{FAD} = 1.425 \times 10^{-2} \times 60 = 0.855 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Mark Distribution (per KTU key):**
- (a) Diagram 3 marks (proper labelled block diagram, not just a list), roles 4 marks
- (b-i) Swept volume: 2 marks (formula 1, calculation 1)
- (b-ii) Volumetric efficiency: 3 marks (C calculation 1, formula 1, final 1)
- (b-iii) FAD: 2 marks (formula 0.5, calculation 1, conversion 0.5)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing clearance ratio symbol:** $C$ is dimensionless ($V_c/V_s$); students sometimes mistakenly take $C = V_c$ directly.
> 2. **Wrong exponent in polytropic work formula:** The exponent is $(n-1)/n$ in the bracket — not $(n-1)$ alone. A frequent slip.
> 3. **Two-stage work formula missing the "-2" term:** Forgetting the constant subtractive term when $P_x = P_1$ or $P_x = P_2$ degenerates the multi-stage formula to single-stage.
> 4. **Units mix-up:** $V_1$ must be in **m³** (not litres) when paired with $P_1$ in **Pa** to get Joules. Pressure in **bar** needs conversion.
> 5. **Forgetting mechanical efficiency** when asked for shaft power — work output vs. shaft input distinction.
> 6. **Optimum intercooling condition** derivation: writing $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$ without derivation loses 2–3 marks in Part B.
> 7. **Drawing omission:** In "explain with neat sketch" questions, a missing or unlabelled diagram costs **3 marks** outright.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Production of Compressed Air**

- ✅ **Compressed air** = atmospheric air pressurised above ambient; ranks as the **fourth industrial utility**.
- ✅ **Compressors** classified into **Positive Displacement** (reciprocating, rotary) and **Dynamic** (centrifugal, axial).
- ✅ **Three compression processes:** Isothermal ($n=1$), Polytropic (real), Adiabatic ($n=\gamma = 1.4$).
- ✅ **Isothermal work:** $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ — theoretical **minimum**.
- ✅ **Polytropic work:** $W_{poly} = \dfrac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1\right]$.
- ✅ **Adiabatic work:** Same form as polytropic with $n = \gamma = 1.4$.
- ✅ **Multi-stage compression** with intercooling **reduces work** and **lowers discharge temperature**.
- ✅ **Optimum intercooler pressure:** $P_x = \sqrt{P_1 \cdot P_2}$ (geometric mean).
- ✅ **Volumetric efficiency:** $\eta_v = 1 + C - C(P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$ — **decreases** with rising pressure ratio.
- ✅ **FAD** = $\eta_v \cdot V_s \cdot N$ — actual intake volume delivered per unit time.
- ✅ **Shaft power** = $W/\eta_{mech}$ — accounts for mechanical losses.
- ✅ **System components** (in flow order): Intake filter → Compressor → Intercooler → Aftercooler → Receiver → Dryer → Filters → Regulator → Lubricator → Distribution → Actuators.
- ✅ **Safety elements:** Pressure relief valves on receiver, pressure gauges, drain valves, isolation valves — all mandatory per **IS 7938 / ASME BPVC VIII / OSHA 1910.169**.
- ✅ **Multi-stage advantages** (recall all four): less work, lower $T_2$, better $\eta_v$, balanced bearing loads.
- ✅ **Reciprocating vs. Rotary:** Reciprocating → high pressure, pulsating flow; Rotary → moderate pressure, smooth flow, oil-flooded designs need extensive oil-removal filtration.
- ✅ **Always convert units:** bar → Pa ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$), litres → m³ ($1 \text{ L} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$) before computing work in Joules.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor:** Remember the formula trio by structure — all have the form $\dfrac{\text{index}}{\text{index}-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[(P_2/P_1)^{(\text{index}-1)/\text{index}} - 1\right]$. Only the **index** changes: 1 (isothermal), $n$ (polytropic), $\gamma$ (adiabatic).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Receivers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Air Receivers

# 📘 Air Receivers – KTU 2024 Premium Study Notes

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> An **Air Receiver** (also called an *air reservoir*, *air tank*, or *compressed air storage vessel*) is a pressure vessel designed to store compressed air generated by a compressor and to deliver it in a stabilized, cooled, and moisture-free state to downstream pneumatic components. It is engineered, fabricated, and tested in accordance with the **Indian Standard IS 2825** (Unfired Pressure Vessels) and **ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII**.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank on the roof of your house**. The municipal pump fills it continuously, but you only use water intermittently (for bathing, cooking, washing). The tank:
- **Stores** water when demand is low,
- **Smooths out** the pump's on/off cycling,
- **Maintains steady pressure** at the tap.

An **Air Receiver does the exact same thing for compressed air**. The compressor "fills" it (during its *loaded* period), and the pneumatic actuators "draw" from it (during *unloaded* periods). Without the receiver, every actuation would cause violent pressure drops and would require an oversized compressor running continuously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)**
> - Atmospheric Pressure $P_{atm} = \mathbf{1.01325 \; bar} \approx \mathbf{1\;bar}$
> - Standard test pressure for air receivers = $\mathbf{1.5 \times \text{Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)}}$
> - Standard atmospheric temperature reference = $\mathbf{15^{\circ}C}$ (ISA)
> - Design Code Reference: **IS 2825 / ASME Section VIII Div. 1**

### Primary Functions of an Air Receiver

1. **Storage of Compressed Air** – Acts as a buffer between supply (compressor) and demand (actuators).
2. **Pressure Stabilization** – Dampens pulsations and stabilizes line pressure.
3. **Heat Dissipation** – Allows hot compressed air from the compressor to cool before use.
4. **Moisture Separation** – Cool air condenses water vapor, which collects at the bottom for drainage.
5. **Emergency Reserve** – Provides compressed air during compressor shutdown or maintenance.
6. **Compressor Cycling Reduction** – Allows the compressor to operate on **load–unload** cycles, saving energy and extending life.
7. **Peak Demand Handling** – Meets short-duration peak demands exceeding compressor capacity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 Why is an Air Receiver Needed?

The compressor operates in **two modes**:
- **Loaded Mode** → Air is pumped into the receiver, pressure builds up.
- **Unloaded Mode** → Compressor idles (or vents) as the receiver continues to supply downstream load.

The receiver's volume directly determines:
- How **long** the compressor can stay unloaded (energy savings),
- How **stable** the line pressure remains during actuator operation,
- How well **moisture and heat** are removed.

### 📊 Types of Air Receivers (Classification)

| **Type** | **Description** | **Application** |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| **Vertical Receiver** | Stands upright; small floor footprint | Workshops, small industries |
| **Horizontal Receiver** | Lying on saddles; easy to mount accessories | Large industrial plants |
| **Portable Receiver** | Smaller capacity, mounted on wheels/skid | Construction sites, mobile units |
| **Underground Receiver** | Buried to save space and reduce noise | Limited-space installations |

### 🛠️ Mandatory Safety Accessories on an Air Receiver

Every air receiver **must** be fitted with the following (IS 2825 / Factory Act compliance):

1. **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** – Set at or below MAWP.
2. **Pressure Gauge** – Indicates internal pressure.
3. **Drain Valve (Moisture Trap)** – Removes condensate (manual or automatic).
4. **Inspection Manhole / Handhole** – For internal inspection and cleaning.
5. **Inlet & Outlet Isolation Valves** – For maintenance isolation.
6. **Safety Rupture Disc** (optional backup to PRV).
7. **Name Plate** – Stamped with design pressure, test pressure, year of manufacture, manufacturer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners specifically ask about the **safety accessories**, **purpose**, and **sizing formula** of air receivers. Master all three.

---

### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|---------------|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| Receiver Volume (Standard Sizing) | $V_r$ | $V_r = \dfrac{C \cdot P_{atm} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{d} - 1\right)}{P_{max} - P_{min}}$ | m³ |
| Compressor Free Air Delivery per Cycle | $C$ | $C = \dfrac{Q_{FAD}}{N}$ | m³/cycle |
| Duty Ratio | $d$ | $d = \dfrac{t_{loaded}}{t_{loaded} + t_{unloaded}}$ | dimensionless |
| Power Required to Refill Receiver | $W$ | $W = V_r \cdot (P_{max} - P_{min})$ | bar·m³ or kJ |
| Hydraulic Test Pressure | $P_{test}$ | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times \text{MAWP}$ | bar |
| Wall Thickness (Cylindrical Shell) | $t$ | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P}$ | mm |
| Maximum Allowable Stress | $S$ | From ASME Table (carbon steel ≈ 137 MPa at 200°C) | MPa |
| Joint Efficiency | $E$ | 0.70 to 1.00 (depending on weld type) | dimensionless |

> **Where:**
> - $Q_{FAD}$ = Free Air Delivery of compressor (m³/min)
> - $N$ = Number of compressor cycles per minute
> - $R$ = Internal radius of the cylindrical shell
> - $P$ = Internal design pressure
> - $E$ = Joint efficiency of the welded shell

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention for KTU 2024:** Always express pressure in **bar (gauge)** for industrial receivers and **Pa (absolute)** for thermodynamic derivations. Marks are deducted if units are inconsistent.

### 🌍 Real-World Utility

Air receivers are **mandatory in every industry** that uses compressed air:
- **Automotive assembly lines** (tooling, painting)
- **Petrochemical plants** (instrument air, purging)
- **Pharmaceutical & Food** (clean compressed air systems)
- **Pneumatic conveying systems** (cement, grain, plastic pellets)
- **Braking systems in locomotives and heavy vehicles**

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔍 Derivation 1: Receiver Sizing Formula

**Statement:** Derive the expression for the volume of an air receiver required to allow a compressor operating in load–unload mode to meet a fluctuating downstream demand.

#### Step 1 — Define the System Parameters

Let:
- $Q_{FAD}$ = Free Air Delivery of compressor (m³/min, measured at atmospheric conditions)
- $N$ = Number of load/unload cycles per minute
- $d$ = Duty ratio (fraction of time compressor is *loaded*)
- $P_{max}$ = Maximum receiver pressure (bar)
- $P_{min}$ = Minimum receiver pressure (bar)
- $P_{atm}$ = Atmospheric pressure (bar)
- $V_r$ = Receiver volume (m³)
- $C$ = Air delivered per cycle

#### Step 2 — Express Air Delivered Per Cycle

During one complete cycle (loaded + unloaded), the compressor supplies a volume of air equal to its free air delivery per cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{Q_{FAD}}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** If $Q_{FAD}$ is the rate (per minute) and there are $N$ cycles per minute, dividing gives the volume per cycle.

#### Step 3 — Apply the Duty Ratio

The compressor delivers air **only when loaded**. During one cycle of duration $1/N$ minutes, the loaded time is $d/N$ minutes. Therefore, the **effective air supplied per cycle** to the receiver is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{effective} &= Q_{FAD} \cdot \frac{d}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Apply Ideal Gas Law (Boyle's Law at Constant Temperature)

Assuming isothermal conditions (a standard engineering approximation for receiver sizing), the air drawn from the receiver at pressure $P_{max}$ corresponds to a much larger volume at $P_{atm}$.

The total free air equivalent that the receiver can deliver during one cycle is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{released} &= V_r \cdot \frac{P_{max} - P_{min}}{P_{atm}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The receiver drops from $P_{max}$ to $P_{min}$ during the unload phase; the difference multiplied by $V_r$ gives the "draw volume" in absolute pressure terms.

#### Step 5 — Energy Balance Per Cycle

The compressor must supply air equal to what the system consumes during the unload period. Equating the supply to the receiver with the demand:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{FAD} \cdot \frac{d}{N} &= V_r \cdot \frac{P_{max} - P_{min}}{P_{atm}} \cdot \left(\frac{1}{d} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** During loaded time $d/N$, the compressor *refills* the receiver. During unload time $(1-d)/N$, the system *draws* air. The factor $(1/d - 1)$ arises from the ratio of unload time to load time.

#### Step 6 — Rearrange to Solve for $V_r$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= \frac{Q_{FAD} \cdot P_{atm} \cdot d \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{d} - 1\right)}{N \cdot (P_{max} - P_{min})} \\[6pt]
V_r &= \frac{C \cdot P_{atm} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{d} - 1\right)}{P_{max} - P_{min}}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **This is the standard KTU 2024 Receiver Sizing Formula.**

---

### 🔍 Derivation 2: Wall Thickness of Cylindrical Shell (Pressure Vessel Theory)

For a thin-walled cylindrical receiver under internal pressure $P$ with internal radius $R$, the hoop stress $\sigma_h$ governs the design:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_h &= \frac{P \cdot R}{t} \leq \frac{S \cdot E}{1} \quad \text{(using safety factor via } S \cdot E\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging to solve for **minimum required wall thickness**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The denominator accounts for the fact that allowable stress is reduced by the simultaneous axial stress component ($0.6P$ is the contribution of longitudinal stress).

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Condition:** This formula is valid only for **thin-walled vessels** where $t \leq R/10$. For thick-walled receivers, the **Lame's equations** must be used (rarely examined in KTU but conceptually important).

---

### 🔢 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A reciprocating compressor delivers free air at $Q_{FAD} = 10 \;\text{m}^3/\text{min}$ and operates at $N = 5$ cycles/min. The duty ratio is $d = 0.4$. The receiver pressure swings between $P_{min} = 6$ bar and $P_{max} = 8$ bar. Atmospheric pressure is $1.013$ bar. Calculate the required receiver volume.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Air per cycle:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{Q_{FAD}}{N} = \frac{10}{5} = 2 \;\text{m}^3/\text{cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Apply the formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= \frac{C \cdot P_{atm} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{d} - 1\right)}{P_{max} - P_{min}} \\[6pt]
V_r &= \frac{2 \times 1.013 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{0.4} - 1\right)}{8 - 6} \\[6pt]
V_r &= \frac{2.026 \times (2.5 - 1)}{2} \\[6pt]
V_r &= \frac{2.026 \times 1.5}{2} = \mathbf{1.5195 \; m^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Required Receiver Volume ≈ 1.52 m³ (1520 litres)**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 📊 Block Diagram: Air Receiver in a Pneumatic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> B[Air Filter]
    B --> C[Compressor Unit]
    C --> D[After Cooler]
    D --> E[Moisture Separator]
    E --> F[Air Receiver Tank]
    F --> G[Pressure Relief Valve]
    F --> H[Pressure Gauge]
    F --> I[Drain Valve Bottom]
    F --> J[Outlet Isolation Valve]
    J --> K[Dryer Unit]
    K --> L[Pressure Regulator]
    L --> M[Pneumatic Actuators and Tools]
    F -.Backup PRV.-> N[Rupture Disc]
```

### 🔧 Functional Architecture of an Air Receiver System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUPPLY[Compressed Air Supply Side]
        S1[Compressor] -->|Hot Wet Air| S2[After Cooler]
        S2 -->|Warm Moist Air| S3[Primary Separator]
    end

    subgraph RECEIVER[Air Receiver Functions]
        R1[Air Storage Volume]
        R2[Pressure Stabilization]
        R3[Heat Dissipation]
        R4[Condensate Collection]
        R5[Emergency Reserve]
    end

    subgraph DEMAND[Downstream Pneumatic Demand]
        D1[Dryer] --> D2[Filter Bank] --> D3[Regulator and Lubricator] --> D4[Actuators]
    end

    S3 --> R1
    S3 --> R2
    S3 --> R3
    S3 --> R4
    R1 --> R5
    R5 --> D1
```

### 📋 Cross-Sectional Schematic of an Air Receiver (Mermaid Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SHELL[Cylindrical Pressure Vessel Shell]
        P1[Inlet Pipe with Check Valve] --> P2[Internal Air Space]
        P2 --> P3[Outlet Pipe with Isolation Valve]
    end
    subgraph SAFETY[Safety and Monitoring Accessories]
        M1[Pressure Relief Valve on Top]
        M2[Pressure Gauge on Top]
        M3[Rupture Disc as Backup]
    end
    subgraph DRAIN[Moisture Management]
        N1[Condensate Collection Zone at Bottom]
        N2[Manual Drain Valve]
        N3[Automatic Float Drain Trap]
    end
    P2 -.Pressure Monitoring.-> M2
    P2 -.Overpressure Protection.-> M1
    P2 -.Condensation.-> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N1 --> N3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
*List any **six** essential safety accessories that must be fitted on an air receiver as per IS 2825.*

**Model Answer:**
The six mandatory safety accessories on an air receiver are:
1. **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** – Set at or below MAWP, vents air on overpressure.
2. **Pressure Gauge** – Provides continuous indication of internal pressure.
3. **Drain Valve (Condensate Trap)** – Removes accumulated moisture at the bottom.
4. **Inspection Manhole / Handhole** – For internal examination and cleaning.
5. **Inlet and Outlet Isolation Valves** – Allow maintenance without depressurizing the entire system.
6. **Rupture Disc** (Optional backup) – Provides overpressure protection if PRV fails.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term **"duty ratio"** of a compressor in relation to air receiver sizing.*

**Model Answer:**
The **duty ratio ($d$)** of a compressor is defined as the fraction of total cycle time during which the compressor operates in the **loaded mode** (i.e., actually pumping air into the receiver). It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d = \frac{t_{loaded}}{t_{loaded} + t_{unloaded}}
\end{aligned}
$$

A duty ratio of $d = 0.5$ means the compressor is loaded for half the time and unloaded (idling) for the other half. A **lower duty ratio** requires a **larger receiver** to bridge the unloaded periods.

---

### 📚 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

## 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **primary functions** of an air receiver in a pneumatic system. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A two-stage compressor delivers $Q_{FAD} = 15$ m³/min of free air at atmospheric conditions. It cycles $N = 4$ times per minute with a duty ratio of $d = 0.3$. The receiver pressure varies from $P_{min} = 7$ bar to $P_{max} = 10$ bar. Determine the required receiver volume. Take $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer:

**(a) Primary Functions of an Air Receiver (7 Marks):**

1. **Storage of Compressed Air** – Decouples supply from demand.
2. **Pressure Stabilization** – Reduces pulsations and maintains steady downstream pressure.
3. **Heat Dissipation** – Allows the hot compressed air from the compressor aftercooler to cool further before entering distribution lines.
4. **Moisture Separation** – As air cools, water vapor condenses and collects at the bottom for drainage through a moisture trap.
5. **Peak Demand Handling** – Supplies air bursts exceeding compressor capacity.
6. **Compressor Cycling Improvement** – Permits load–unload operation, reducing motor wear and energy consumption.
7. **Emergency Reserve** – Provides compressed air during compressor trip or power failure for critical operations (e.g., valve closure in process plants).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Step 1: Free air per cycle:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C = \frac{Q_{FAD}}{N} = \frac{15}{4} = 3.75 \;\text{m}^3/\text{cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2: Apply the sizing formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_r &= \frac{C \cdot P_{atm} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{d} - 1\right)}{P_{max} - P_{min}} \\[6pt]
V_r &= \frac{3.75 \times 1.013 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{0.3} - 1\right)}{10 - 7} \\[6pt]
V_r &= \frac{3.79875 \times (3.333 - 1)}{3} \\[6pt]
V_r &= \frac{3.79875 \times 2.333}{3} \\[6pt]
V_r &= \frac{8.8657}{3} = \mathbf{2.955 \; m^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Required Receiver Volume ≈ 2.96 m³ (≈ 2960 litres)**

---

## 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Pattern]**
*(a)* With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **constructional features** and **mandatory safety fittings** of a vertical air receiver. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* Discuss in detail the **inspection, testing, and maintenance procedures** prescribed by IS 2825 for air receivers used in industrial fluid power systems. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer:

**(a) Constructional Features and Safety Fittings (7 Marks):**

A vertical air receiver is a **cylindrical pressure vessel** with a **domed top** (torispherical or ellipsoidal head) and a **flat or dished bottom**, mounted on a steel saddle base.

**Constructional Details:**
- **Shell** – Rolled carbon steel plate (IS 2062 grade), welded longitudinally and circumferentially.
- **Heads** – Formed by pressing; may be torispherical or elliptical.
- **Nozzles** – Inlet (top), outlet (bottom side), drain (bottom), pressure gauge (top), PRV (top).
- **Saddle Support** – Welded to the bottom for floor mounting.
- **Nameplate** – Riveted/Welded on the side, stamped with design pressure, test pressure, capacity, year.

**Mandatory Safety Fittings (See Cross-Sectional Diagram in Section 4):**
1. **Pressure Relief Valve** (top) – Set ≤ MAWP.
2. **Pressure Gauge** (top) – 0 to 1.5 × MAWP range.
3. **Rupture Disc** (optional, top) – Backup overpressure protection.
4. **Drain Valve** (bottom) – Manual ball valve or automatic float trap.
5. **Inspection Manhole** (side) – 300–500 mm diameter for internal access.
6. **Inlet Check Valve** – Prevents backflow to compressor.
7. **Outlet Isolation Valve** – For maintenance isolation.

**(b) Inspection, Testing & Maintenance (7 Marks):**

**1. Initial Testing (Before Commissioning):**
- **Hydrostatic Pressure Test** at $1.5 \times \text{MAWP}$ for 30 minutes.
- Visual inspection of all welds.
- Verification of safety valve calibration.

**2. Periodic Inspection (As per IS 2825 / Factory Act):**
- **External Inspection:** Every 12 months — Check for corrosion, leaks, deformation, support integrity.
- **Internal Inspection:** Every 2–4 years — Requires draining, venting, manhole opening; check for internal corrosion, scaling, pitting.
- **Hydraulic Re-Test:** Every 4–8 years (depending on service) at $1.5 \times$ MAWP.

**3. Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):**
- **Ultrasonic Thickness Gauging (UTG)** — Wall thickness mapping.
- **Dye Penetrant Test (DPT)** — Surface crack detection.
- **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — Weld defect identification.

**4. Routine Maintenance:**
- Daily: Drain condensate, verify pressure gauge reading.
- Weekly: Check PRV operation (manual lift test).
- Monthly: Inspect supports, paint condition, leak tightness.
- Annually: Calibrate pressure gauge and PRV.

**5. Documentation:**
- Maintain a **register** with details of every inspection, test, repair, and modification.
- Maintain a **valid certificate of fitness** (mandatory under the Factories Act, 1948).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - ❌ **Do NOT** use absolute pressure ($P_{abs}$) and gauge pressure ($P_{gauge}$) interchangeably in the sizing formula. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for unit ambiguity.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** forget to include $P_{atm}$ in the numerator. This is the **most common mistake** in numerical problems.
> - ❌ **Do NOT** state the duty ratio as the fraction of *loaded time* without specifying the denominator (total cycle time).
> - ❌ **Do NOT** omit **safety accessories** in construction diagrams — drawing a bare tank guarantees loss of **2–3 marks**.
> - ✅ **Always** mention the design code reference (**IS 2825** or **ASME Section VIII**) at least once in any 14-mark question on air receivers.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🎯 **Air Receiver** is a **pressure vessel** designed per **IS 2825 / ASME Sec VIII**.
- 🎯 Primary functions: **Storage, Stabilization, Cooling, Moisture Removal, Peak Buffering, Emergency Reserve**.
- 🎯 Vertical receivers save floor space; horizontal receivers are easier to maintain.
- 🎯 **Mandatory accessories:** PRV, Pressure Gauge, Drain Valve, Manhole, Isolation Valves.
- 🎯 **Test pressure = 1.5 × MAWP** (Hydraulic Test).
- 🎯 **Duty ratio** $d = \dfrac{t_{loaded}}{t_{cycle}}$, always between 0 and 1.
- 🎯 **Sizing Formula:** $\;V_r = \dfrac{C \cdot P_{atm} \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{d} - 1\right)}{P_{max} - P_{min}}\;$
- 🎯 **Wall Thickness:** $\;t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P}\;$
- 🎯 **Inspection frequency:** External – yearly; Internal – 2–4 years; Hydro-test – 4–8 years.
- 🎯 NDT methods: UTG, DPT, RT — used for fitness-for-service assessment.
- 🎯 Lower duty ratio → Larger receiver → More energy-efficient compressor operation.
- 🎯 **Always cite IS 2825** in KTU answers — it is the Indian regulatory backbone for unfired pressure vessels.
- 🎯 Air receivers are inspected under **Factories Act 1948** and require a valid **Certificate of Fitness**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accumulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Accumulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> A **hydraulic accumulator** is a pressure vessel that stores hydraulic energy in the form of a compressed gas (typically nitrogen) and a pressurized fluid (usually hydraulic oil). It functions as a *reservoir of potential energy* within a fluid power circuit, capable of releasing stored energy instantaneously on demand, smoothing pressure pulsations, supplementing pump flow, and providing emergency power in case of pump or power failure.

The accumulator essentially acts as a **rechargeable energy storage device** analogous to a battery in an electrical circuit, but instead of storing electrons, it stores pressurized fluid and compressed gas.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Water Tower with a Pressurized Roof**
> Imagine a tall water tank with a sealed, pressurized air cushion on top of the water. When the municipal pump pushes water in during low-demand periods (night), the air gets compressed and stores energy. When a sudden demand spike occurs (morning showers across a city), the compressed air pushes the water out *instantly* without waiting for the pump. The hydraulic accumulator performs the exact same function in a fluid power system — it is the *hydraulic equivalent of a pressurized water tower*.

A second, simpler analogy: **A bicycle pump (piston) + a pressurized soda bottle (gas pre-charge)** combined in a steel shell. The compressed gas is the "spring," and the hydraulic oil is the working medium.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (Industry) | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-charge gas** | **Nitrogen (N₂)** — chemically inert, dry, oil-free | — |
| **Standard pre-charge pressure (P_pre)** | **0.9 × P_max** (bladder/diaphragm) | bar |
| **Standard pre-charge pressure (P_pre)** | **0.75 × P_max** (piston type) | bar |
| **Polytropic index (n)** | **1.0 (isothermal)** to **1.4 (adiabatic)** | dimensionless |
| **Typical working pressure** | **100 – 700 bar** (industrial) | bar |
| **Typical volume** | **1 – 50 litres** | L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests the *gas law relationships*, the *function* of accumulators, and the *safety relief devices* (burst discs, isolation valves) associated with them. Always quote nitrogen as the pre-charge gas — examiners *deduct marks* for saying "air" because atmospheric air contains moisture and oxygen, both of which degrade hydraulic oil and create explosion risk.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Structured Logic

A hydraulic accumulator operates on the principle of **force balance between a compressible gas and an incompressible hydraulic fluid** across a separating element (piston, bladder, or diaphragm). The cycle is always:

1. **Charging (Loading) Phase:** Pump delivers fluid → fluid enters accumulator → gas gets compressed → energy is stored.
2. **Hold Phase:** System is at equilibrium, gas pressure equals circuit pressure (minus the small force needed to move the separating element).
3. **Discharging (Unloading) Phase:** System pressure drops momentarily → gas expands → stored fluid is expelled → energy is released.

The **Why:** Hydraulic pumps cannot respond instantly to sudden demand spikes (e.g., a cylinder reaching end-of-stroke and stalling). The accumulator bridges that millisecond-to-second gap.

The **How:** By using a compressible gas as the storage medium, energy density becomes very high — a 10 L accumulator at 200 bar can store roughly the same energy as 200 L of fluid at atmospheric pressure.

## 2.2 Types of Accumulators (KTU 2024 — High-Yield)

| Type | Separator | Best Use | KTU Exam Tip |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bladder-type** | Rubber bladder (NBR/HNBR) | High-flow, fast response | Most common in industry |
| **Piston-type** | Steel piston with seals | High pressure, large volume | Best for > 200 bar |
| **Diaphragm-type** | Metal-reinforced rubber diaphragm | Compact, low-volume | Used in mobile hydraulics |
| **Weight-loaded** | Dead-weight on piston | Constant pressure | Rare, mostly obsolete |
| **Spring-loaded** | Mechanical spring | Low pressure, small volume | Used in lubrication circuits |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for Part B (14-mark) derivations in KTU ESE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 \cdot V_1^{\,n} &= P_2 \cdot V_2^{\,n} \quad \text{(General Polytropic Gas Law)}
\end{aligned}
$$

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | **Boyle's Law** (isothermal, $n=1$) | Slow discharge / static analysis |
| 2 | $P_1 V_1^{1.4} = P_2 V_2^{1.4}$ | **Adiabatic** ($n = 1.4$) | Fast discharge / dynamic response |
| 3 | $V_{acc} = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}}$ | **Accumulator sizing** | KTU most-asked derivation |
| 4 | $P_{pre} \le 0.9 \, P_{max}$ | Pre-charge rule (bladder) | Safety limit |
| 5 | $P_{pre} \le 0.75 \, P_{max}$ | Pre-charge rule (piston) | Safety limit |
| 6 | $\Delta V = V_1 \!\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/n}\right]$ | **Effective fluid volume** discharged | Energy calculation |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{n-1}$ | **Stored energy** (adiabatic/isothermal) | Energy audit problems |
| 8 | $Q_{out} = -A_{sep} \dfrac{dx}{dt}$ | Discharge flow rate | Dynamic modelling |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Use $\vert$ or \mid in any table — **never** use the literal pipe symbol `|` for absolute value, because it breaks the markdown table structure. Example: write $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never $\vert x \vert$ inside a `|...|` cell.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pulsation damping** in piston pump circuits (reduces vibration and noise).
- **Emergency backup** in machine-tool clamping circuits (KTU safety application).
- **Shock absorption** in long hydraulic pipelines (water-hammer equivalent).
- **Energy regeneration** in hybrid and mobile machinery (e.g., excavator boom lowering).
- **Leakage compensation** in vertical cylinder circuits.
- **Pressure compensation** in press circuits to maintain constant clamp force.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Discharge Volume (Boyle's Law, Isothermal Case)

This is the **single most-asked derivation** in KTU ESE for accumulators. We derive the volume of hydraulic fluid an accumulator can deliver from pressure $P_{max}$ down to $P_{min}$.

**Given:** A bladder accumulator of total gas volume $V_1$ pre-charged to $P_{pre}$, connected to a circuit whose pressure varies from $P_{max}$ (during charging) to $P_{min}$ (during discharge). Assume **isothermal** process ($n = 1$).

**Step 1 — Initial state (gas fully charged to maximum pressure):**
The gas occupies volume $V_1$ at pressure $P_{max}$.

**Step 2 — Final state (gas expanded to minimum allowable pressure):**
The gas occupies volume $V_2$ at pressure $P_{min}$. By the conservation of gas mass (isothermal, Boyle's Law):

$$
P_{max} \cdot V_1 = P_{min} \cdot V_2
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the expanded gas volume $V_2$:**

$$
V_2 = \frac{P_{max} \cdot V_1}{P_{min}}
$$

**Step 4 — Effective fluid volume discharged ($\Delta V$):**
The fluid pushed out equals the increase in gas volume:

$$
\Delta V = V_2 - V_1
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $V_2$:**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{P_{max} \cdot V_1}{P_{min}} - V_1
$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $V_1$:**

$$
\Delta V = V_1 \left(\frac{P_{max}}{P_{min}} - 1\right) = V_1 \left(\frac{P_{max} - P_{min}}{P_{min}}\right)
$$

**Step 7 — Generalise to polytropic index $n$ (isothermal $n=1$, adiabatic $n=1.4$):**

$$
\Delta V = V_1 \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of conversion:** We moved from a *static gas state equation* to a *useful engineering design equation* by computing the *change* in gas volume, which is exactly the volume of hydraulic oil that leaves the accumulator.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Accumulator Sizing for a Given Flow Demand

**Problem context:** A hydraulic circuit requires an additional flow $Q$ (L/min) for a duration of $t$ seconds, while the pressure may drop from $P_{max}$ to $P_{min}$. Find the required accumulator volume $V_1$.

**Step 1 — Volume of fluid required from accumulator:**

$$
V_{req} = Q \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Equate required volume to effective discharge volume:**

$$
Q \cdot t = V_1 \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $V_1$ (the total gas volume of the accumulator to be purchased):**

$$
V_1 = \frac{Q \cdot t}{1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical sanity check (typical values):**
Let $Q = 20$ L/min, $t = 5$ s, $P_{max} = 200$ bar, $P_{min} = 150$ bar, $n = 1.4$ (fast discharge).

$$
V_1 = \frac{(20/60) \times 5}{1 - (150/200)^{1/1.4}} = \frac{1.667}{1 - (0.75)^{0.714}}
$$

Compute $(0.75)^{0.714}$:

$$
\ln(0.75) = -0.2877, \quad 0.714 \times (-0.2877) = -0.2054, \quad e^{-0.2054} \approx 0.814
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_1 = \frac{1.667}{1 - 0.814} = \frac{1.667}{0.186} \approx 8.96 \text{ L}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** A 10 L standard accumulator would be selected. Always round **up** in safety-critical sizing — never round down. Examiners love this 1-mark rounding step.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Isothermal vs. Adiabatic)

| Property | Isothermal ($n=1$) | Adiabatic ($n=1.4$) | KTU Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process speed | Slow (> 1 min) | Fast (< 1 min) | Choose $n$ by application |
| Energy stored | Lower | Higher (by factor $\sim 1.15$) | Use adiabatic for impact |
| Design equation | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | $P_1 V_1^{1.4} = P_2 V_2^{1.4}$ | Most exam problems use $n=1$ |
| Real accumulator | Approximates bladder | Approximates piston | Mention both in answers |

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Pre-charge Pressure Setting (Worked Approach)

**Step 1 — KTU 2024 Rule (bladder & diaphragm):**

$$
P_{pre} = 0.9 \times P_{max, working}
$$

**Step 2 — Verification logic:** Pre-charge must be **below** the minimum system pressure; otherwise the bladder would collapse fully and never recover.

**Step 3 — Numerical example:** If $P_{max} = 200$ bar, then $P_{pre} = 180$ bar. Gas side pressure must never exceed hydraulic side pressure during charging, otherwise the bladder ruptures.

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-loss trap:** A common student error is to set $P_{pre} = 0.9 \times P_{min}$ by mistake. The correct reference is the **maximum working pressure**, not the minimum. KTU examiners explicitly test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Accumulator in a Fluid Power Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electric Motor] --> B[Hydraulic Pump]
    B --> C[Pressure Relief Valve]
    B --> D[Check Valve]
    D --> E[Directional Control Valve]
    E --> F[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    D --> G[Accumulator]
    G --> H[Pressure Gauge]
    G --> I[Safety Burst Disc]
    G --> J[Isolation Valve]
    G --> K[Gas Charging Valve]
    K --> L[N2 Cylinder]
    J --> M[System Pressure Line]
    H --> N[Operator Display]
    I --> O[Safe Vent to Atmosphere]
```

**Reading guide:** The accumulator `G` sits in parallel with the load, fed through the check valve `D` (which prevents reverse flow into the pump during discharge). The gas charging valve `K` allows nitrogen topping from cylinder `L`. The **burst disc `I`** is the mandatory safety device — it ruptures at ~110% of design pressure to protect against over-pressurisation. The **isolation valve `J`** allows safe removal of the accumulator for service.

## 4.2 Internal Construction — Bladder-Type Accumulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Shell Body - Forged Steel] --> B[Oil Port - Bottom]
    A --> C[Gas Valve - Top]
    A --> D[Poppet Valve]
    D --> E[Anti-extrusion Ring]
    E --> F[Bladder NBR/HNBR]
    F --> G[Gas Pre-charge N2]
    B --> H[Poppet Closes When Discharged]
    H --> I[Trapped Oil Remains In Shell]
    I --> J[Acts As Suction Strainer]
```

**Reading guide:** When oil pressure falls below gas pressure, the poppet `D` closes, trapping residual oil at the bottom. This trapped oil is *not* available for discharge, but it lubricates the poppet and acts as a fine strainer. KTU frequently asks: *"Why doesn't the bladder collapse fully?"* — Answer: the poppet protects it.

## 4.3 State Diagram — Charge / Hold / Discharge Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Precharged
    Precharged --> Charging: Pump pressure > Ppre
    Charging --> Charged: Pressure reaches Pmax
    Charged --> Holding: No flow demand
    Holding --> Discharging: Load demands flow and pressure drops
    Discharging --> Precharged: Pressure falls to Pmin
    Discharging --> Charged: Pump refills
    Precharged --> [*]: Maintenance depressurise
```

**Reading guide:** The state diagram clarifies the *thermodynamic* view of the accumulator. The transitions labelled "Charging" and "Discharging" correspond to the compression and expansion of nitrogen. KTU frequently diagrams this as a *P-V curve* showing the polytropic path.

## 4.4 Safety Interlock Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Accumulator Vessel] --> B[Pressure Relief Valve]
    A --> C[Burst Disc]
    A --> D[Pressure Gauge]
    A --> E[Drain Valve]
    B --> F[Safe Return to Tank]
    C --> G[Atmosphere]
    D --> H[Control Panel]
    E --> I[Tank]
    H --> J[Alarm: Over Pressure]
    H --> K[Alarm: Low Pre-charge]
    H --> L[Shutdown: P > 105% of Pmax]
```

**Reading guide:** KTU's `PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY` explicitly requires the *redundancy* of safety devices. **Two independent over-pressure protections** are mandated by ISO 4413 / KTU syllabus: a relief valve and a burst disc, never relying on a single device.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**List any three functions of a hydraulic accumulator in a fluid power system.**

**Model Answer (3 points):**

1. **Energy storage:** Stores hydraulic energy as compressed gas + pressurised fluid, releasing it instantaneously on demand — used for emergency power during pump failure. *(1 mark)*
2. **Pulsation damping:** Absorbs pressure ripples from piston pumps, reducing vibration, noise, and fatigue in pipework. *(1 mark)*
3. **Leakage / shock compensation:** Maintains pressure in vertical cylinder circuits and absorbs water-hammer shocks in long hydraulic lines. *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional Test 2]**
**State the standard pre-charge gas and the standard pre-charge pressure rule for a bladder-type accumulator.**

**Model Answer (3 points):**

- **Gas:** Dry, oil-free **nitrogen (N₂)** — never air, because air contains moisture and oxygen which degrade hydraulic oil and create fire risk. *(1 mark)*
- **Rule:** $P_{pre} = 0.9 \times P_{max, working}$ for bladder/diaphragm types; $P_{pre} = 0.75 \times P_{max}$ for piston types. *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Part A:** Students often write "air" or omit the inert-gas justification. *Half a mark is lost* per omission. Quote **nitrogen** with reasoning.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **bladder-type hydraulic accumulator**. Mention the role of the poppet valve. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A bladder accumulator of **10 L** total gas volume is pre-charged with nitrogen to **80 bar**. It is connected to a circuit whose pressure rises to **200 bar** during charging. Calculate the volume of hydraulic oil the accumulator can deliver if the circuit pressure then drops to **150 bar**. Assume **isothermal** conditions. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Construction (3 marks):**
A bladder-type accumulator consists of:
- A **forged-steel shell** rated for the maximum working pressure. *(0.5)*
- A **gas charging valve** at the top for nitrogen pre-charge. *(0.5)*
- A **synthetic rubber bladder** (NBR or HNBR) separating the gas and oil sides. *(0.5)*
- A **poppet valve** at the oil port that closes when the bladder is fully discharged, protecting it from collapsing against the port. *(0.5)*
- An **anti-extrusion ring** (orifice ring) preventing bladder damage at the port. *(0.5)*
- An **oil port** at the bottom connected to the hydraulic line. *(0.5)*

**Working (3 marks):**
- **Charging:** Pump pressure exceeds gas pre-charge → oil enters through poppet → bladder compresses → nitrogen pressure rises → energy is stored. *(1 mark)*
- **Discharging:** System pressure drops below gas pressure → bladder expands → oil is pushed out through the poppet into the circuit. *(1 mark)*
- **Fully discharged:** Bladder reaches the poppet; poppet closes; residual oil is trapped and lubricates the poppet. *(1 mark)*

**Role of the poppet valve (1 mark):**
The poppet **prevents the bladder from extruding through the oil port** when fully discharged, which would otherwise cause rupture. It also traps a small volume of oil that lubricates the moving parts.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $V_1 = 10$ L (gas volume at maximum pressure)
- $P_{max} = 200$ bar
- $P_{min} = 150$ bar
- $n = 1$ (isothermal)

**Formula:**

$$
\Delta V = V_1 \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}\right]
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$
\Delta V = 10 \left[1 - \left(\frac{150}{200}\right)^{1}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the ratio:**

$$
\frac{150}{200} = 0.75
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate (since $n=1$, the power is 1):**

$$
\Delta V = 10 \,[1 - 0.75]
$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$
\Delta V = 10 \times 0.25 = 2.5 \text{ L}
$$

**Answer:** The accumulator can deliver **2.5 litres** of hydraulic oil as the pressure drops from 200 bar to 150 bar under isothermal conditions.

**Marking distribution (b):** Formula (2) + substitution (1) + simplification (2) + final answer with units (1) + logical comment (1).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**
> **(a)** Compare **bladder, piston, and diaphragm** type accumulators based on construction, pressure rating, response time, and typical applications. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Derive the **accumulator sizing equation** $V_1 = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}}$ starting from the polytropic gas law. State the meaning of each symbol. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

| Criterion | Bladder | Piston | Diaphragm |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Separator** | Rubber bladder | Steel piston + seals | Reinforced rubber diaphragm |
| **Pressure rating** | Up to ~350 bar | Up to ~700 bar | Up to ~250 bar |
| **Response time** | Very fast | Moderate (seal friction) | Very fast |
| **Volume range** | 1 – 50 L | 10 – 500 L | 0.1 – 5 L |
| **Cost** | Moderate | High (precision seals) | Low |
| **Application** | Industrial general use | Heavy press, offshore | Mobile, compact equipment |
| **Maintenance** | Bladder replacement | Seal replacement | Diaphragm replacement |

*(1 mark per row × 6 rows = 6 marks, 1 mark for neat tabular presentation and concluding remark)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Symbols (1 mark):**
- $V_1$ = total gas volume of accumulator (L)
- $Q$ = additional flow demand (L/s or L/min)
- $t$ = duration of demand (s)
- $P_{max}$ = maximum working pressure (bar)
- $P_{min}$ = minimum allowable pressure (bar)
- $n$ = polytropic index (1 = isothermal, 1.4 = adiabatic)

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Step 1 — Polytropic gas law** (initial and final states):

$$
P_{max} \cdot V_1^{\,n} = P_{min} \cdot V_2^{\,n}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_2$:**

$$
V_2 = V_1 \left(\frac{P_{max}}{P_{min}}\right)^{1/n}
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Volume of fluid discharged = increase in gas volume:**

$$
\Delta V = V_2 - V_1
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_2$:**

$$
\Delta V = V_1 \left(\frac{P_{max}}{P_{min}}\right)^{1/n} - V_1
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Factor and rearrange:**

$$
\Delta V = V_1 \left[\left(\frac{P_{max}}{P_{min}}\right)^{1/n} - 1\right] = V_1 \left[\frac{1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}}{1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}} \cdot \ldots \right]
$$

*Cleaner direct form:*

$$
\Delta V = V_1 \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}\right]
$$

*(1 mark — note the sign inversion: $\Delta V = V_2 - V_1$ is positive when $P_{max} > P_{min}$.)*

**Step 6 — Required fluid volume equals $Q \cdot t$:**

$$
Q \cdot t = V_1 \left[1 - \left(\frac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}\right]
$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 7 — Solve for $V_1$:**

$$
V_1 = \frac{Q \cdot t}{1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}}
$$

*(1 mark — final boxed equation)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — Part B (b):** Students frequently lose **2 marks** by:
> (i) forgetting to invert the ratio inside the brackets (writing $P_{max}/P_{min}$ instead of $P_{min}/P_{max}$),
> (ii) omitting the **$1/n$ exponent** (writing the term to the first power),
> (iii) failing to state **assumptions** ($n=1$ isothermal, ideal gas, no heat transfer).
> Always state assumptions explicitly at the start of a derivation — KTU rewards this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read Twice Before the Exam**

- ✅ Accumulator = **pressurised gas + pressurised fluid** in a vessel — energy storage device.
- ✅ Standard pre-charge gas is **dry nitrogen (N₂)** — never atmospheric air.
- ✅ Pre-charge rule: **$P_{pre} = 0.9 \times P_{max}$** (bladder/diaphragm), **$0.75 \times P_{max}$** (piston).
- ✅ Governing equation: **$P_1 V_1^{\,n} = P_2 V_2^{\,n}$** where $n = 1$ (isothermal) or $n = 1.4$ (adiabatic).
- ✅ Sizing formula: **$V_1 = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}}$**.
- ✅ Effective discharge: **$\Delta V = V_1 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_{min}}{P_{max}}\right)^{1/n}\right]$**.
- ✅ **Three types** to know: bladder (most common), piston (high pressure), diaphragm (compact).
- ✅ **Functions:** energy storage, pulsation damping, shock absorption, leakage compensation, emergency backup.
- ✅ **Mandatory safety devices:** pressure relief valve, burst disc, isolation valve, pressure gauge, drain valve.
- ✅ **KTU safety rule:** Always install **two independent** over-pressure protections (ISO 4413).
- ✅ **Always** round accumulator size **up** to the next standard size — never down.
- ✅ **Units consistency:** Convert flow $Q$ to **L/s** if $t$ is in seconds; convert to **L/min** if $t$ is in minutes.
- ✅ **Poppet valve** in bladder type — prevents bladder extrusion; trapped oil lubricates the poppet.
- ✅ **Pre-charge pressure** must be checked **every 6 months**; nitrogen slowly leaks through the bladder over time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dry and Oil Free Compressed Air, Pneumatic Control, Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dry and Oil Free Compressed Air, Pneumatic Control, Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Dry & Oil-Free Compressed Air, Pneumatic Control & Components

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dry Compressed Air — Formal Definition

**Dry Compressed Air** is atmospheric air that has been mechanically compressed and then passed through a series of treatment stages (filtration, refrigeration, adsorption, or membrane separation) so that the **water vapour content is reduced to a specified Pressure Dew Point (PDP)** suitable for the downstream application. "Dryness" is never absolute — it is always quantified by a dew-point temperature measured at a specific line pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> *Dry compressed air is air in which the concentration of water vapour has been reduced to a level where its pressure dew point is at least **10 °C below** the lowest ambient temperature encountered at any point in the distribution network.*

The international benchmark is **ISO 8573-1:2010**, which classifies compressed-air purity into discrete classes for **solid particulates, water, and oil (aerosol + vapour)**.

### 1.2 Oil-Free Compressed Air — Formal Definition

**Oil-Free Compressed Air** is compressed air from which **all lubricant-derived contamination has been removed**, such that the total oil content (aerosol + vapour) is at or below the limits specified for **ISO 8573-1 Class 0** or **Class 1** depending on the application.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two interpretations exist in industry and KTU papers expect you to know both:**
> 1. **Technically Oil-Free (Class 0):** Oil content ≤ 0.01 mg/m³ — used in pharmaceutical, food & beverage, electronics, and medical breathing-air lines.
> 2. **Oil-Free by Design:** Air produced by a compressor that does **not** use oil in the compression chamber (e.g., water-injected screw, scroll, tooth-roots, or piston with PTFE rings).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — "The Electric Kettle Story":**
> Imagine boiling water in a kettle. As the air above the water cools, you see droplets form on the lid — that is **condensation**. Now imagine pressurising that same moist air inside a pipe network: as the air cools while travelling through long pipelines, it behaves like the kettle lid — water condenses inside your pneumatic valves and cylinders.  
> **Dust and rust** behave like the chalky residue you see in a bathroom shower-head — they choke small orifices. **Oil aerosols** are like an invisible greasy film that ruins paint jobs, spoils food, and degrades pharmaceutical products.
>
> **Dry + Oil-Free Compressed Air = "Clean, dehumidified, breathable air"** for machines, instruments, and processes.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Constants & Standards

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Class | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric air water vapour (saturated, 25 °C) | $w_{sat}$ | **23.0** | g/m³ |
| Atmospheric air absolute humidity (tropical Kerala) | $w_{amb}$ | **~25–30** | g/m³ |
| Industrial instrument-air dew point | $T_{dp}$ | **+3 °C PDP @ 7 bar** | °C |
| Pharmaceutical / breathing-air dew point | $T_{dp}$ | **−40 °C PDP** | °C |
| Class 0 oil aerosol limit | $C_{oil}$ | **≤ 0.01** | mg/m³ |
| Class 1 oil aerosol limit | $C_{oil}$ | **≤ 0.01** | mg/m³ |
| Atmospheric pressure (sea-level) | $p_{atm}$ | **101.325** | kPa |

### 1.5 Why Dry & Oil-Free? — The Four Engineering Drivers

1. **Process Quality:** Moisture + oil = defective paint finishes, spoiled food, contaminated drugs.
2. **Equipment Reliability:** Water causes valve icing, rusting of cylinders, and washing away of lubricating grease.
3. **Energy Efficiency:** Wet air requires more compression work; rust scales narrow pipes.
4. **Safety:** Oil aerosol in oxygen lines can ignite explosively; moisture in instrument air causes false readings.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Contamination in Compressed Air

Understanding **where** contamination enters is the first step in designing any pneumatic purification system. There are five principal contamination sources:

- **Atmospheric Intake Air:** Contains dust, pollen, microbes, water vapour, and ambient hydrocarbons (from vehicle exhaust).
- **Compressor Itself:** Lubricating oil is atomised into microscopic aerosols; hot surfaces vaporise oil completely.
- **Aftercooler & Receiver:** Hot, saturated air cools → water condenses → emulsified oil-water sludge forms.
- **Distribution Pipelines:** Rust, weld slag, and pipe-scale particles are shed downstream.
- **Compressor Heat of Compression:** A 7-bar compression of 25 °C ambient air can push outlet temperature past **180 °C** — every 10 °C rise doubles water vapour carrying capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A standard oil-flooded screw compressor without treatment delivers compressed air with **~30–40 mg/m³ of oil aerosol** and **saturated water vapour** at the aftercooler outlet. ISO 8573 Class 1–2 purity requires **four-stage treatment** (filter → refrigerated dryer → adsorption dryer → activated-carbon filter).

## 2.2 Pressure Dew Point (PDP) vs Atmospheric Dew Point

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students confuse *atmospheric dew point* with *pressure dew point*. They are NOT the same. The **pressure dew point (PDP)** is the temperature at which condensation begins **at line pressure**. Always quote dew point as **"X °C PDP @ Y bar(g)"**.

The conversion between pressure dew point ($T_{dp,p}$) and atmospheric dew point ($T_{dp,atm}$) for the same moisture content uses the ideal-gas-based relation:

$$
T_{dp,atm} \;\approx\; T_{dp,p} \cdot \left(\frac{p_{atm}}{p_{line}}\right)^{\,0.05}
$$

For $p_{line} = 7 \text{ bar(g)}$ and $p_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$, this gives $T_{dp,atm} \approx T_{dp,p} \times 0.71$.

## 2.3 Air-Drying Technologies — Comparative Theory

| Drying Method | Operating Principle | Achievable PDP | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Refrigerated (Chilled) Dryer** | Cools air to **+2 °C**; condensate drained | **+3 °C PDP** | General factory pneumatics |
| **Desiccant Twin-Tower (Adsorption)** | Water adsorbs onto silica-gel/alumina beads; one tower dries while the other regenerates | **−40 °C to −70 °C PDP** | Outdoor pipelines, instrument air, pharma |
| **Membrane Dryer** | Water vapour permeates selectively through hollow-fibre membrane | **−40 °C PDP** | Remote/point-of-use drying |
| **Deliquescent Dryer** | Salt tablets dissolve in captured water | **−10 °C PDP** | Low-budget intermittent-duty systems |
| **Heat-of-Compression (HOC) Dryer** | Re-uses compressor's own heat to regenerate desiccant | **−20 °C to −40 °C PDP** | Large oil-free screw compressors |

## 2.4 Oil-Removal Technologies — Comparative Theory

| Method | Mechanism | Residual Oil |
|---|---|---|
| **Coalescing Filter (Grade AO/AA/ACS)** | Fibreglass element captures oil droplets; small ones merge into larger ones | ≤ 0.01 mg/m³ |
| **Activated-Carbon Adsorber** | Oil vapours bond to carbon micro-pores | ≤ 0.003 mg/m³ (vapour) |
| **Oil-Free Compressor Design** | No oil used in compression chamber at all | Inherently oil-free |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these six equations — they appear in every Part B (14-mark) question on this module.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w = \dfrac{p_{v}}{R_{v} \cdot T}$ | Absolute humidity (vapour density) | kg/m³ |
| 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{p_{v}}{p_{sat}(T)} \times 100\%$ | Relative humidity | % |
| 3 | $\dfrac{p_{1} V_{1}}{T_{1}} = \dfrac{p_{2} V_{2}}{T_{2}}$ | Combined gas law for air flow | Pa·m³/K |
| 4 | $W_{comp} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} \cdot R T_{1} \cdot \left[\left(\dfrac{p_{2}}{p_{1}}\right)^{\!\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Polytropic compression work | J/kg |
| 5 | $m_{w} = \rho_{air} \cdot Q \cdot (\omega_{in} - \omega_{out})$ | Water removed by dryer | kg/s |
| 6 | $PDP_{atm} = PDP_{line} \cdot \left(\dfrac{p_{atm}}{p_{line}}\right)^{0.05}$ | Dew-point conversion | °C |

> **Where:**
> - $p_{v}$ = partial pressure of water vapour (Pa)
> - $R_{v}$ = specific gas constant for water vapour = **461.5 J/(kg·K)**
> - $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats for air = **1.4** (isothermal: 1.0)
> - $\omega$ = humidity ratio (kg water / kg dry air)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** ISO 8573-1 Class 0, 1, 2 mandated for product-contact air.
- **Food & Beverage (F&B):** Lubricant-free air for direct product contact (drying, conveying).
- **Paint Shops:** Even **1 mg/m³** oil can cause fish-eyes and cratering.
- **Medical Breathing Air:** Compressed-air purity must meet European Pharmacopoeia monographs.
- **Pneumatic Instruments (KTU Lab View):** Instrument air must be **3 µm filtered, dried to +3 °C PDP, oil-free**.
- **Semiconductor Manufacturing:** Class 0 mandatory — even nano-scale contamination destroys chips.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Mass of Water Removed by a Refrigerated Dryer

> **Problem Statement (typical 14-mark KTU Part):**  
> A factory compressor delivers **$Q = 10 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$** of compressed air at **$p = 7 \text{ bar(g)}$** and **$T_{in} = 35 \text{ °C}$**, saturated with water vapour. It enters a refrigerated dryer that cools it to **$T_{out} = +3 \text{ °C}$**. Determine the **mass of condensate (kg/hr)** removed by the dryer.

### Step 1 — Convert Inlet Volumetric Flow to Free-Air Equivalent

The flow meter reads flow at line conditions. Convert to **Normal m³/hr** (Nm³/hr) at $T_{N} = 273 \text{ K}$, $p_{N} = 1.013 \text{ bar(a)}$.

$$
Q_{N} = Q \cdot \frac{p_{line}}{p_{N}} \cdot \frac{T_{N}}{T_{in}}
$$

$$
Q_{N} = 10 \cdot 60 \cdot \frac{(7+1.013)}{1.013} \cdot \frac{273}{(35+273)}
$$

$$
Q_{N} = 600 \cdot 7.91 \cdot 0.887 \;=\; 4210 \;\text{Nm}^{3}/\text{hr}
$$

**Reasoning:** Higher line pressure (8.013 bar) packs more moles into each m³, but elevated temperature (308 K) spreads them out. Net factor ≈ 7.0×.

### Step 2 — Saturated Water Vapour Density at Inlet (35 °C)

From steam tables or Antoine equation, saturation vapour pressure at 35 °C:
$$
p_{sat}(35\,°C) \approx 5.628 \text{ kPa}
$$

Vapour density at inlet using ideal gas law:
$$
\rho_{v,in} = \frac{p_{sat}}{R_{v} \cdot T_{in}} = \frac{5628}{461.5 \times 308}
$$

$$
\rho_{v,in} = 0.0396 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}
$$

**Reasoning:** Saturated air at 35 °C carries about **39.6 g of water per m³** — a significant contamination load.

### Step 3 — Saturated Vapour Density at Outlet (+3 °C)

Saturation pressure at 3 °C:
$$
p_{sat}(3\,°C) \approx 0.758 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
\rho_{v,out} = \frac{758}{461.5 \times 276} = 0.00595 \;\text{kg/m}^{3}
$$

**Reasoning:** Cooling from 35 °C to 3 °C drops water-carrying capacity by **6.65×** — that's the dryer's job.

### Step 4 — Mass of Condensate per Hour

$$
\dot{m}_{w} = Q_{N} \cdot (\rho_{v,in} - \rho_{v,out})
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{w} = 4210 \times (0.0396 - 0.00595)
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{w} = 4210 \times 0.03365 \;=\; 141.7 \;\text{kg/hr}
$$

### Step 5 — Analytical Result

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{w} \approx 142 \text{ kg of water removed per hour}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a 24-hour day, that is ~3.4 tonnes of water removed daily** — equivalent to draining a small swimming pool. This is why refrigerated dryers must have **automatic condensate drains** (zero-loss or timed solenoid) — manual draining would flood the workshop.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Dryer

| Inlet Condition | Air Demand | Required PDP | **Recommended Dryer** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cool climate, indoor | < 50 Nm³/min | ≥ +3 °C | Refrigerated (chilled) dryer |
| Tropical (Kerala — KTU context) | < 50 Nm³/min | ≥ +3 °C | Refrigerated + after-filter |
| Long outdoor pipelines | Any | −20 °C | Desiccant twin-tower |
| Pharma / F&B / Electronics | Any | −40 °C | Desiccant + activated carbon |
| Remote / hazardous area | < 10 Nm³/min | −40 °C | Membrane dryer |

---

## 3.3 Pneumatic Control — Closed-Loop Block Diagram Analysis

A pneumatic control loop has **four canonical elements** (KTU textbook — Esposito, Anthony):

1. **Error Detector** — compares set-point to measured variable
2. **Controller** — generates corrective pneumatic signal (3–15 psi or 0.2–1.0 bar)
3. **Final Control Element** — pneumatic actuator (diaphragm/cylinder motor)
4. **Process** — the controlled variable (pressure, flow, level, temperature)

The **transfer function** of a pneumatic controller (PI mode) in Laplace form:

$$
G_{c}(s) = K_{p} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_{i} \cdot s}\right)
$$

Where:
- $K_{p}$ = proportional gain (dimensionless, often 1–100)
- $T_{i}$ = integral time constant (seconds)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A purely proportional controller always leaves an **offset (steady-state error)**. To eliminate offset, **add integral action (PI controller)** — a pneumatic flapper-nozzle amplifier with a bellows feedback loop achieves this naturally.

---

## 3.4 Pneumatic Components — Function & Symbols Table

| Component | Symbol | Function | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Filter (F)** | Triangle with mesh | Removes particles ≥ 5 µm | First stage of FRL |
| **Regulator (R)** | Spring-loaded diaphragm | Maintains constant downstream pressure | Second stage of FRL |
| **Lubricator (L)** | Wick/drip in air stream | Adds oil mist to air (≈ 1 drop/m³) | Third stage of FRL — **NOT for oil-free lines** |
| **Direction Control Valve (DCV)** | Squares with arrows | Routes air to actuator ports | 3/2, 4/2, 5/2, 5/3 — common in labs |
| **Flow Control Valve (FCV)** | Variable orifice | Controls cylinder speed | Meter-in / meter-out circuits |
| **Quick-Exhaust Valve** | Ball/duckbill check | Vents exhaust directly to atmosphere | Increases cylinder speed |
| **Pressure Safety Relief Valve** | Spring-loaded poppet | Vents at set pressure | Mandatory on every receiver |
| **Silencer / Muffler** | Porous bronze plug | Reduces exhaust noise to **< 75 dB(A)** | Every valve exhaust port |
| **Check Valve** | Ball in cone | Allows one-way flow | Holds pressure in circuit |
| **Pneumatic Cylinder** | Rectangle with piston rod | Converts air pressure to linear motion | Single-acting / double-acting |
| **Pneumatic Motor** | Circle with vanes | Converts air pressure to rotary motion | Power tools, drives |

---

## 3.5 Air Consumption of a Single-Acting Cylinder (Worked Example)

**Given:** Cylinder bore $D = 50 \text{ mm}$, stroke $L = 100 \text{ mm}$, supply pressure $p = 6 \text{ bar(g)}$, stroke time $t = 1 \text{ s}$.

**Step 1 — Cylinder volume per stroke**
$$
V_{stroke} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^{2} (0.10) = 1.963 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Free-air equivalent volume**
$$
V_{FAD} = V_{stroke} \cdot \frac{p_{line}}{p_{atm}} = 1.963 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{(6+1.013)}{1.013}
$$

$$
V_{FAD} = 1.36 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{stroke}
$$

**Step 3 — Air consumption rate**
$$
Q_{air} = \frac{V_{FAD}}{t} = \frac{1.36 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 1.36 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{s}
$$

$$
Q_{air} = 4.9 \text{ Nm}^{3}/\text{hr} \quad \text{(or 81.6 Nlpm)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this calculation to size compressors and receivers in Part B problems.**

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Compressed-Air Treatment Line — Process Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> B[Inlet Pre-Filter]
    B --> C[Air Compressor]
    C --> D[Aftercooler]
    D --> E[Moisture Separator]
    E --> F[Receiver Tank]
    F --> G[Refrigerated Dryer]
    G --> H[Coalescing Filter]
    H --> I[Desiccant Twin-Tower Dryer]
    I --> J[Activated Carbon Adsorber]
    J --> K[Particulate Filter 0.01 micron]
    K --> L[Pressure Regulator]
    L --> M[FRL Unit at Point of Use]
    M --> N[Pneumatic Actuator / Tool]
    N --> O[Silencer / Muffler Exhaust]
```

**Process Logic:**  
Each block represents one purification stage. Skipping any block reduces downstream air quality by one or more ISO 8573 classes. For **KTU lab-grade instrument air**, blocks **G → M** are mandatory.

---

## 4.2 FRL Unit — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressor Supply 7 bar] --> B[Filter F]
    B --> C[Regulator R]
    C --> D[Lubricator L]
    D --> E[Service Line]
    E --> F[Direction Control Valve]
    F --> G[Double Acting Cylinder]
    G --> H[Flow Control Valve FCV]
    H --> I[Silenced Exhaust]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Critical Note:** The **L (Lubricator)** is **omitted** in oil-free pneumatic systems. A 3-stage FRL (F + R + filter) replaces the classic FRL when downstream components (solenoids, pharmaceutical equipment) cannot tolerate oil.

---

## 4.3 Pneumatic Closed-Loop Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Set Point] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    PV[Process Variable Sensor] --> SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[PI Pneumatic Controller]
    CTRL --> AMP[Relay Amplifier]
    AMP --> FCV[Final Control Element FCV]
    FCV --> PROC[Process / Actuator]
    PROC --> PV
```

**Signal Convention:**  
- Set-point signal: **3–15 psi** (US) or **0.2–1.0 bar** (KTU / European standard)  
- Live-zero of 3 psi / 0.2 bar detects cable breaks — **fail-safe design**.

---

## 4.4 Twin-Tower Desiccant Dryer — Regeneration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TowerA[Tower A — Drying Mode]
        A1[Wet Air In] --> A2[Desiccant Bed] --> A3[Dry Air Out]
    end
    subgraph TowerB[Tower B — Regenerating Mode]
        B1[Purge Air In] --> B2[Heated Expansion] --> B3[Wet Exhaust Vent]
        B2 --> B4[Desiccant Bed Drying]
    end
    A3 --> C[Dry Air Manifold]
    B3 --> V[Atmosphere]
    T[Timer 10 min] -.->|Switches valves| TowerA
    T -.-> TowerB
```

**Cycle Logic:**  
Two towers alternate every **5–10 minutes**. A small fraction (~15%) of dried air is expanded back through the offline tower to strip accumulated moisture — this is the **purge air** or **regeneration air**.

---

## 4.5 Safety & FRL Mounting — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Receiver Tank] --> PRV[Pressure Relief Valve 10 bar]
    A --> GD[Guard]
    A --> DG[Drain Valve]
    A --> M[Pressure Gauge]
    A --> SV[Shut-off Valve]
    SV --> FRL[FRL Unit]
    FRL --> DOWN[Downstream Equipment]
    PRV --> VENT[Safe Vent to Atmosphere]
```
