# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PESFT752]`
**Define "Pressure Dew Point" (PDP) of compressed air and explain why it differs from atmospheric dew point.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Pressure Dew Point is the temperature at which water vapour begins to condense when the compressed air is at line (working) pressure. It is the **true measure of dryness** in a pneumatic system.
- **(1 mark)** Atmospheric dew point is measured at 1.013 bar; PDP is measured at line pressure (typically 7 bar). At higher pressure, the same mass of water vapour occupies a smaller volume, so its partial pressure is greater and condensation occurs at a higher temperature than at atmospheric pressure — **PDP > atmospheric dew point**.
- **(1 mark)** Industry rule-of-thumb: for line pressure of 7 bar, atmospheric dew point ≈ 0.71 × PDP. KTU expects quotation in the form *"+3 °C PDP @ 7 bar(g)"*.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PESFT752]`
**List any three differences between a refrigerated dryer and a desiccant (adsorption) dryer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
| Parameter | Refrigerated Dryer | Desiccant Dryer |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Cools air to +2 °C → condensate drains | Water adsorbs onto silica-gel/alumina beads |
| Achievable PDP | +3 °C | −40 °C to −70 °C |
| Power consumption | Moderate (compressor of refrigerant circuit) | High (heater + purge air = 15–20% of throughput) |
| Maintenance | Annual refrigerant check | Desiccant replacement every 3–5 years |
| Best suited to | General factory air, indoor use | Outdoor pipelines, instrument/pharma air |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PESFT752]`  *(Internal Choice Option)*

**A. (a)** Explain in detail the **different methods of drying compressed air**, with neat sketches of each. Compare their **pressure dew points** achievable.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part A(a):**

**(i) Refrigerated (Chilled) Air Dryer (2 marks)**
- Compressed air enters a heat exchanger where it is cooled to **+2 °C** by a refrigerant circuit (R-134a or R-410A).
- Water condenses and is drained via an **automatic float drain**.
- Achievable PDP: **+3 °C PDP @ line pressure**.
- Used for general factory air, indoor installations.

**(ii) Desiccant (Adsorption) Twin-Tower Dryer (2 marks)**
- Two towers filled with **silica gel, activated alumina, or molecular sieve 4A/13X**.
- Tower A dries the air while Tower B is being regenerated by either **heated purge** or **heat-of-compression**.
- Cycle time **5–10 minutes**, switched by solenoid valves.
- Achievable PDP: **−40 °C to −70 °C PDP**.

**(iii) Membrane Dryer (1 mark)**
- Hollow-fibre polymer membrane selectively permeates water vapour to the atmosphere.
- No moving parts, no electrical power.
- Achievable PDP: **−40 °C PDP**; flow drop of ~20%.

**(iv) Deliquescent Dryer (1 mark)**
- Salt tablets (calcium chloride) absorb water and dissolve.
- Low cost, low maintenance, intermittent duty.
- Achievable PDP: **−10 °C PDP**.

**(v) Heat-of-Compression (HOC) Dryer (1 mark)**
- Uses the **180 °C outlet** of an oil-free screw compressor to regenerate desiccant.
- No external heater; no purge-air loss.
- Achievable PDP: **−20 °C to −40 °C PDP**.

---

**A. (b)** A compressor delivers **5 m³/min** of free air at atmospheric conditions (30 °C, 70% RH). The air is compressed to **7 bar(g)** and cooled back to 30 °C in an aftercooler. Calculate (i) the **mass of water condensed per hour** in the aftercooler, and (ii) the **mass of water still remaining** in vapour form.  **(7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $Q = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$ = 300 m³/hr at atmospheric conditions
- $T = 30\,°C = 303 \text{ K}$
- $\phi = 70\%$
- $p_{line} = 7 \text{ bar(g)} = 8.013 \text{ bar(a)}$

**Step 1 — Partial pressure of water vapour (1 mark)**

From steam tables, $p_{sat}(30\,°C) = 4.246 \text{ kPa}$.
$$
p_{v} = \phi \cdot p_{sat} = 0.70 \times 4.246 = 2.972 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Vapour density at inlet (1 mark)**
$$
\rho_{v} = \frac{p_{v}}{R_{v} \cdot T} = \frac{2972}{461.5 \times 303} = 0.02124 \text{ kg/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water vapour entering per hour (1 mark)**
$$
\dot{m}_{v,in} = 300 \times 0.02124 = 6.37 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Saturated vapour density at 7 bar, 30 °C (2 marks)**

Since air at 7 bar is cooled back to 30 °C, the partial pressure of vapour (2.972 kPa) is unchanged, but total pressure is now 8.013 bar. Saturation at 30 °C still gives $p_{sat} = 4.246 \text{ kPa}$. Since $p_{v} = 2.972 < p_{sat}$, the air is **still unsaturated** — but at much higher relative humidity.

At 7 bar, **RH = 70%** is still below saturation; however, if the aftercooler cools the air below the **pressure dew point**, condensation occurs.

Re-evaluate the question assuming the air is **saturated at 7 bar / 30 °C** (worst-case from aftercooler):

$$
\rho_{v,out} = \frac{p_{sat}}{R_{v} \cdot T} = \frac{4246}{461.5 \times 303} = 0.03036 \text{ kg/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Condensate mass per hour (1 mark)**
$$
\dot{m}_{cond} = 300 \times (0.03036 - 0.02124) = 300 \times 0.00912
$$
$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{cond} = 2.74 \text{ kg/hr of water condensed}}
$$

**Step 6 — Remaining water vapour in the air (1 mark)**
$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{v,remain} = 300 \times 0.03036 = 9.11 \text{ kg/hr still in vapour form}}
$$

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PESFT752]`  *(Alternative Internal Choice)*

**B. (a)** Explain the **FRL unit** with a neat block diagram. State the **function of each element** and explain what happens if the **lubricator is omitted** in an oil-free pneumatic circuit.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part B(a):**

The **FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator)** is the standard treatment unit mounted between the receiver tank and the working circuit.

**(i) Filter (F) — 2 marks**
- **Function:** Removes particulates (dust, rust, pipe scale) down to **5–40 µm** (or 0.01 µm in sub-micronic types).
- **Mechanism:** Centrifugal action + sintered bronze / fibre element. Auto-drain at the bottom discharges collected water.

**(ii) Regulator (R) — 2 marks**
- **Function:** Maintains a **constant downstream pressure** (typically 6 bar) irrespective of upstream fluctuations (5–10 bar) and downstream demand.
- **Mechanism:** Spring-loaded diaphragm opposed by downstream feedback. A drop in downstream pressure opens the valve; rise closes it.

**(iii) Lubricator (L) — 2 marks**
- **Function:** Injects **oil mist (~1 drop per m³)** into the air stream to lubricate downstream moving parts (cylinders, valves, motors).
- **Mechanism:** Venturi effect draws oil from a reservoir up a siphon tube; air carries it as aerosol.
- **If Omitted:** In **oil-free circuits** (pharma, food, electronics), the lubricator is intentionally omitted. Downstream components must be **permanently lubricated** (e.g., PTFE-impregnated seals) or use **food-grade grease**. Failure to omit the lubricator would **contaminate** the product/process — losing ISO 8573 Class 0 purity.

**(iv) Block Diagram (1 mark)**

The block diagram is already shown in **Section 4.2** above.

---

**B. (b)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the operation of a **closed-loop pneumatic control system** using a **PI (Proportional + Integral) controller**. State the **transfer function** and explain why pure proportional control causes **offset**.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part B(b):**

**(i) Block Diagram (2 marks)** — See Section 4.3 above for the Mermaid diagram.

**(ii) Closed-Loop Operation (2 marks)**
- The **set-point** (e.g., 6 bar) is fed to the summing junction.
- A **pressure sensor** measures the actual process variable (PV) and feeds it back to the summing junction.
- The **error** $e = \text{SP} - \text{PV}$ is amplified by a **flapper-nozzle amplifier** (KTU standard pneumatic amplifier).
- The amplifier output (3–15 psi) drives a **final control element** (pneumatic valve positioner).
- The valve modulates air flow to the **process** (e.g., a heater, a tank), which changes PV.
- Feedback closes the loop, automatically maintaining PV = SP.

**(iii) PI Controller Transfer Function (1 mark)**
$$
G_{c}(s) = K_{p} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1}{T_{i} \cdot s}\right) = K_{p} + \frac{K_{i}}{s}
$$

**(iv) Why Proportional-Only Causes Offset (2 marks)**
- A proportional controller outputs a signal **proportional to the instantaneous error**.
- For the controller to hold PV at SP, the error **must be non-zero** — otherwise the controller output would be zero and the valve would close.
- This residual steady-state error is called **offset**.
- The integral term **accumulates** past errors and continues to integrate until the error is **zero**, thereby eliminating offset.
- Hence PI control is preferred over P-only control for precise pneumatic regulation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in this module (Exam Tips):**
> 1. **Dew Point Confusion (1–2 marks lost):** Forgetting to specify **PDP @ line pressure** — students write only "3 °C" without pressure reference. Always write **"+3 °C PDP @ 7 bar(g)"**.
> 2. **Oil-Free vs Dry — Not the Same:** Students often treat "dry" and "oil-free" as synonyms. They are **different purity parameters** under ISO 8573-1.
> 3. **FRL Order Wrong:** Drawing F-R-L as R-F-L or omitting the auto-drain. **Correct order: Filter → Regulator → Lubricator** (F-R-L).
> 4. **Forgot Lubricator in Oil-Free Circuit:** Many students keep the lubricator even in pharma circuits — must explicitly state **"Lubricator is OMITTED"** with justification.
> 5. **No Transfer Function in PI Question:** Pure proportional = offset; you must mention the **transfer function equation** to score full 7 marks on Part B(b).
> 6. **Units in Numerical Answers:** Always convert Nm³/hr, kg/hr, m³/min — KTU examiner penalises unit mismatches.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Read twice before exam):**

- ✅ **Compressed air is never naturally dry** — it must be treated.
- ✅ **Three ISO 8573-1 contaminants:** Solid particles, Water, Oil (aerosol + vapour).
- ✅ **PDP is the true dryness measure**; always quote with line pressure.
- ✅ **Refrigerated dryer → +3 °C PDP**; **Desiccant dryer → −40 °C PDP**.
- ✅ **Twin-tower desiccant dryer** cycles every 5–10 min; one tower dries, the other regenerates.
- ✅ **Membrane dryers** are point-of-use, no power, ~20% flow loss.
- ✅ **Coalescing filter removes oil aerosols**; **activated carbon removes oil vapours**.
- ✅ **Oil-free compressor designs** include scroll, tooth-roots, water-injected screw, and PTFE-ring piston.
- ✅ **FRL order is always F → R → L** (Filter first to protect regulator).
- ✅ **Lubricator is OMITTED** in oil-free / pharma / electronics circuits.
- ✅ **Silencers** must be fitted to every valve exhaust port for **< 75 dB(A)** noise compliance.
- ✅ **Pressure relief valves** mandatory on every receiver — set at **+10% above working pressure**.
- ✅ **Quick-exhaust valves** increase cylinder speed by up to **50%**.
- ✅ **PI controller eliminates offset**; **P-only controller always has steady-state error**.
- ✅ **Pneumatic signal standard:** 3–15 psi (US) or 0.2–1.0 bar (European/KTU).
- ✅ **Air consumption for sizing:** $Q_{air} = \dfrac{V_{stroke} \cdot p_{line}}{p_{atm} \cdot t}$.
- ✅ **Water removed by dryer:** $\dot{m}_{w} = Q \cdot (\rho_{v,in} - \rho_{v,out})$.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme | PESFT752 Fluid Power Safety**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Cylinders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Cylinders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the domain of **Fluid Power Safety**, a **cylinder** is defined as a linear mechanical actuator that converts the hydraulic or pneumatic energy of a pressurized working fluid into a unidirectional (or bidirectional) straight-line mechanical force and motion. It is the work-producing element of any fluid power system and is the primary component responsible for performing useful mechanical work against a load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Classification of cylinders is assessed under the cognitive levels *Remember (L1)*, *Understand (L2)*, and *Apply (L3)* of **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy**, with direct questions frequently appearing for **3-mark** (Part A) and **7-mark** (Part B sub-questions) definitions.

A fluid power cylinder fundamentally consists of:
- **Barrel (Tube)**: The cylindrical housing that contains the pressurized fluid.
- **Piston**: The disc-shaped sliding element that transmits force.
- **Piston Rod (Ram)**: The external shaft that delivers the mechanical output.
- **End Caps**: Sealed closures at both ends of the barrel.
- **Seals**: Static and dynamic seals to prevent leakage and contamination.
- **Ports**: Inlet/outlet openings for fluid entry and exit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Medical Syringe:**
> Think of a cylinder as a giant, industrial-grade medical syringe. When a doctor pushes the plunger, fluid is expelled with force. Similarly, when pressurized oil or air is pushed into the cylinder barrel, the piston is forced to move, and that movement (with great amplification of force) pushes the load. A **double-acting cylinder** is like a syringe that can both suck in and push out fluid from either end, allowing controlled two-way motion.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Cylinder Design

- **Bore Diameter (D)**: Internal diameter of the barrel (in **mm** or **inches**).
- **Rod Diameter (d)**: Diameter of the piston rod (in **mm**).
- **Stroke Length (L)**: Maximum axial travel of the piston rod.
- **Working Pressure (P)**: Nominal system pressure in **bar** (1 bar = 10⁵ N/m²) or **MPa**.
- **Piston Area (A)**: Effective cross-sectional area in **m²** or **mm²**.
- **Output Force (F)**: In **Newton (N)** or **kN**.
- **Standard Reference Metric**: ISO 3320 (fluid power systems — cylinder bore and piston rod areas).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hydraulic/Pneumatic Cylinders

Fluid power cylinders can be classified on **two distinct bases**:

### (A) Based on Functional Operation
1. **Single-Acting Cylinder (SAC)**
2. **Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC)**
3. **Tandem Cylinder**
4. **Telescopic Cylinder**
5. **Cushioned Cylinder**

### (B) Based on Mechanical Construction
1. **Tie-Rod Cylinder**
2. **Welded (Mill-Type) Cylinder**
3. **Bolted Cylinder**
4. **Ram Cylinder**

---

## 2.2 Detailed Functional Theory

### 2.2.1 Single-Acting Cylinder
- Fluid pressure acts on **only one side** of the piston.
- The return stroke is achieved by an **external force** such as a spring, gravity, or a mechanical lever.
- **Advantages**: Simple design, lower cost, easier sealing.
- **Disadvantages**: Only produces work in one direction; spring limits stroke length; asymmetric force profile.
- **Safety Consideration**: External load must always be controllable; mechanical stops required at both stroke ends.

### 2.2.2 Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC)
- Fluid pressure acts on **both sides** of the piston alternately.
- Produces force in **both extension and retraction** strokes.
- **Two sub-categories**:
  - **(i) Differential (Asymmetric) Cylinder** — rod on one side only.
  - **(ii) Non-Differential (Symmetric / Double-Rod) Cylinder** — rod on both sides.
- **Advantages**: Full control in both directions; high versatility.
- **Disadvantages**: More ports, more seals, more complex valving.

### 2.2.3 Tandem Cylinder
- Essentially **two pistons on a single rod** inside one barrel.
- Effective piston area is the **sum** of both piston areas → provides **double output force** at the same pressure.
- Used where space constraints prevent increasing bore diameter.
- **Safety Implication**: Double force means double hazard — must size relief valves and structural mounts accordingly.

### 2.2.4 Telescopic Cylinder
- Comprises **multiple nested sleeves/stages** that extend sequentially.
- Achieves a **very long stroke** from a **compact retracted length**.
- Commonly used in **dump trucks, refuse collectors, cranes**.
- **Safety Concern**: Synchronization failure can cause erratic motion; emergency bleed-down valves mandatory.

### 2.2.5 Cushioned Cylinder
- Built-in **deceleration device** (cushion) at one or both ends of stroke.
- The cushion is a small chamber that traps fluid at end-of-stroke, forcing it through a **meter-out orifice**, gradually decelerating the piston.
- **Critical for Safety**: Prevents impact damage, reduces noise, protects seals, eliminates rebound.
- **Safety Implication**: A failed cushion causes violent end-of-stroke impact → catastrophic seal failure risk.

### 2.2.6 Tie-Rod Cylinder
- Construction uses **external tie-rods** to hold end caps against the barrel.
- Easy to disassemble for maintenance.
- Standard for **medium-pressure** industrial applications.

### 2.2.7 Welded (Mill-Type) Cylinder
- End caps are **welded** directly to the barrel — no tie-rods.
- Higher pressure rating, more compact, more rigid.
- Common in **heavy industry, mobile hydraulics, presses**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Effective Piston Area (full) | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | m² | D = bore diameter |
| 2 | Annulus (Rod-side) Area | $A_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ | m² | d = rod diameter |
| 3 | Extension Force (Diff. DAC) | $F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p$ | N | Full bore area acts |
| 4 | Retraction Force (Diff. DAC) | $F_{ret} = P \cdot A_r = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ | N | Reduced by rod area |
| 5 | Extension Velocity | $v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | m/s | Q = flow rate |
| 6 | Retraction Velocity | $v_{ret} = \dfrac{Q}{A_r}$ | m/s | Faster than extension |
| 7 | Cylinder Efficiency (Force Ratio) | $\eta_c = \dfrac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} = 1 - \left(\dfrac{d}{D}\right)^2$ | dimensionless | Always < 1 for differential |
| 8 | Tandem Force | $F_{tandem} = P \cdot (A_{p1} + A_{p2})$ | N | Sum of piston areas |
| 9 | Buckling Load (Euler) | $F_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}$ | N | Rod safety check |
| 10 | Pressure Intensity | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa or N/m² | Pascal's Law |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Safety Rule**: When computing retraction force, students very often forget to subtract the rod area — this loses **1–2 marks** consistently in KTU valuation.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

Cylinder selection directly impacts **machine safety, energy efficiency, and operator protection**. In **press safety**, cushioned double-acting cylinders prevent die-closure impact injuries. In **mobile hydraulics**, welded cylinders survive vibration and shock loading. In **foundry/steel mill applications**, water-cooled or fire-resistant fluid (HFC, HFD) cylinders are mandatory for fire safety compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cylinder Force Output (Pascal's Law Application)

**Statement (Pascal's Law):** The pressure applied to a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted equally in all directions.

**Step 1** — Pressure definition:
A force $F$ applied perpendicular to a flat surface of area $A$ produces pressure:

$$
P = \frac{F}{A}
$$

**Step 2** — Rearranging to express Force as a function of Pressure and Area:

$$
F = P \cdot A
$$

**Step 3** — Substituting the area of a circular piston of bore diameter $D$:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2
$$

**Step 4** — Final Force Equation (Extension stroke of double-acting cylinder):

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2
$$

**Step 5** — For the retraction stroke, the rod occupies part of the bore on the rod side. Effective area becomes an annulus:

$$
A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 - \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)
$$

**Step 6** — Retraction Force:

$$
F_{ret} = P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cylinder Efficiency (Force Ratio) for Differential DAC

**Step 1** — Define mechanical efficiency of cylinder as the ratio of retraction force to extension force:

$$
\eta_c = \frac{F_{ret}}{F_{ext}}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute formulas:

$$
\eta_c = \frac{P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)}{P \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} D^2}
$$

**Step 3** — Cancel common terms $P$ and $\frac{\pi}{4}$:

$$
\eta_c = \frac{D^2 - d^2}{D^2}
$$

**Step 4** — Split the numerator:

$$
\eta_c = \frac{D^2}{D^2} - \frac{d^2}{D^2}
$$

**Step 5** — Final compact expression:

$$
\eta_c = 1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2
$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $d = D/2$, then $\eta_c = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$ → cylinder delivers 75% of extension force during retraction.
- If $d = D\sqrt{2}/2$, then $\eta_c = 0.50$ → only half the extension force is available.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Velocity Ratio

**Step 1** — Volume flow rate $Q$ entering a cylinder of piston area $A$ in time $t$ advances the piston by distance $L$:

$$
Q = \frac{A \cdot L}{t}
$$

**Step 2** — Since $v = L/t$:

$$
Q = A \cdot v
$$

**Step 3** — Therefore, piston velocity:

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A}
$$

**Step 4** — For differential DAC, the ratio of velocities:

$$
\frac{v_{ret}}{v_{ext}} = \frac{A_p}{A_r} = \frac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}
$$

**Conclusion:** Retraction is **always faster** than extension because the same flow is squeezed through a smaller effective area.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A double-acting differential hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 100$ mm and rod diameter $d = 60$ mm. The system pressure is $P = 160$ bar. The pump delivers $Q = 60$ L/min. Find:
(i) Extension force
(ii) Retraction force
(iii) Cylinder efficiency
(iv) Extension and retraction velocities

**Solution — Step (i): Extension Force**

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F_{ext} = P \cdot A_p = (160 \times 10^5) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ext} = 1.2566 \times 10^5 \text{ N} \approx 125.66 \text{ kN}
$$

**Solution — Step (ii): Retraction Force**

$$
A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.100^2 - 0.060^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.01 - 0.0036) = 5.026 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
F_{ret} = (160 \times 10^5) \times (5.026 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ret} = 8.042 \times 10^4 \text{ N} \approx 80.42 \text{ kN}
$$

**Solution — Step (iii): Cylinder Efficiency**

$$
\eta_c = 1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2 = 1 - \left(\frac{60}{100}\right)^2 = 1 - 0.36 = 0.64 \text{ or } 64\%
$$

**Solution — Step (iv): Velocities**

Convert $Q = 60$ L/min $= 60 \times 10^{-3} / 60 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.1273 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_r} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{5.026 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.1990 \text{ m/s}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Classification of Cylinders

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fluid Power Cylinders]:::root --> B[Functional Classification]
    A --> C[Constructional Classification]

    B --> B1[Single Acting Cylinder]
    B --> B2[Double Acting Cylinder]
    B --> B3[Tandem Cylinder]
    B --> B4[Telescopic Cylinder]
    B --> B5[Cushioned Cylinder]

    B1 --> B1A[Spring Return Type]
    B1 --> B1B[Gravity Return Type]

    B2 --> B2A[Differential Asymmetric]
    B2 --> B2B[Non Differential Symmetric]

    B2A --> B2A1[Extension Force P times Ap]
    B2A --> B2A2[Retraction Force P times Ar]

    B5 --> B5A[End of Stroke Cushion]
    B5 --> B5B[Both Ends Cushioned]

    C --> C1[Tie Rod Cylinder]
    C --> C2[Welded Mill Type]
    C --> C3[Bolted Cylinder]
    C --> C4[Ram Type Cylinder]

    C1 --> C1A[Easy Maintenance]
    C2 --> C2A[High Pressure Use]
    C3 --> C3A[Heavy Duty Press]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Schematic — Double-Acting Differential Cylinder with Safety Components

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Hydraulic Power Unit]:::source --> V[Directional Control Valve 4 by 3]:::valve
    V --> CE[Cap End Port]:::port
    V --> RE[Rod End Port]:::port

    CE --> CB[Cushion at Cap End]:::cushion
    RE --> RB[Cushion at Rod End]:::cushion

    CB --> PC[Piston Chamber Full Bore Area Ap]:::chamber
    RB --> AC[Annulus Chamber Area Ar]:::chamber

    PC --> RD[Piston Rod Output]:::output
    AC --> RD

    RD --> LD[External Load]:::load
    LD --> RV[Counterbalance Safety Valve]:::safety

    classDef source fill:#0f766e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px
```
