# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Define an accumulator. List any three applications of hydraulic accumulators in fluid power systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **accumulator** is a pressure vessel used in hydraulic systems to store potential energy in the form of a compressed gas (typically nitrogen), which can be released on demand to supplement pump flow, maintain pressure, or absorb shocks.

**Three Applications (1 Mark each):**

1. **Energy Storage:** Stores pressurized fluid to supplement pump flow during peak demand (e.g., press cycle).
2. **Pressure Stabilization:** Maintains constant system pressure in a circuit.
3. **Emergency Power Source:** Provides fluid power for fail-safe operation of actuators when pump fails (e.g., clamping in machine tools).
4. *(Bonus point)* Pulsation damping in pump discharge lines.
5. *(Bonus point)* Compensation for thermal expansion of hydraulic fluid.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: What is an FRL unit? Why is lubrication necessary in a pneumatic system?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator)** is a combined accessory assembly used in pneumatic systems to condition compressed air before it enters the working circuit.

**Functions (1 Mark each):**

- **F (Filter):** Removes moisture, dust, and oil aerosols from compressed air.
- **R (Regulator):** Maintains constant working pressure regardless of supply pressure variation.
- **L (Lubricator):** Adds controlled oil mist to the air stream.

**Why lubrication is necessary (additional credit):**

After compression, filtration, and drying, the air becomes **dry and abrasive**. Without lubrication, the spool valves, seals, and cylinder walls would wear out rapidly. Lubrication adds a thin oil film to all moving parts, extending service life and reducing friction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a bladder-type hydraulic accumulator. A bladder accumulator of total volume 3.0 L is pre-charged with nitrogen at 25 bar (absolute). It is then charged with oil up to a maximum working pressure of 150 bar (absolute). Assuming isothermal conditions, determine: (a) The volume of nitrogen at maximum pressure. (b) The effective volume of oil delivered by the accumulator. (c) The energy stored in the accumulator at maximum pressure.**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)

**Construction (4 Marks):**

A bladder-type accumulator consists of the following parts:

1. **Forged Steel Shell:** A heavy-duty pressure vessel designed and tested as per ASME Section VIII. It has two hemispherical ends welded to a cylindrical body.
2. **Rubber Bladder:** A moulded flexible bag (typically nitrile rubber or butyl rubber) that separates the gas from the oil. It is closed at the gas charging end and open at the fluid end.
3. **Gas Charging Valve:** A Schrader-type valve located at the top end of the shell, used to charge nitrogen and verify pre-charge pressure.
4. **Poppet Valve:** A spring-loaded valve at the fluid port that closes when fluid flow stops, preventing the bladder from being extruded into the outlet pipe.
5. **Fluid Port:** A threaded connection at the bottom of the shell, connected to the hydraulic circuit.

**Working (3 Marks):**

- **Pre-charging:** Nitrogen is filled into the bladder through the gas charging valve to the specified pre-charge pressure. The bladder expands to fill the entire shell.
- **Charging:** When hydraulic fluid enters through the fluid port, it compresses the bladder. The gas pressure rises according to Boyle's Law. Fluid continues to enter until system pressure equals the maximum working pressure.
- **Discharging:** When system pressure falls below gas pressure, the bladder expands and pushes oil out through the poppet valve into the circuit, supplementing pump flow.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

- Total accumulator volume: $V_1 = 3.0$ L
- Pre-charge pressure: $P_1 = 25$ bar (abs)
- Maximum working pressure: $P_2 = 150$ bar (abs)
- Isothermal: $n = 1$

**Step 1 — Volume of nitrogen at maximum pressure (2 Marks):**

Apply Boyle's Law:

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

$$V_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{P_2} = \frac{25 \times 3.0}{150}$$

$$\boxed{V_2 = 0.5 \text{ L}}$$

**Step 2 — Effective volume of oil delivered (2 Marks):**

$$V_{eff} = V_1 - V_2 = 3.0 - 0.5$$

$$\boxed{V_{eff} = 2.5 \text{ L}}$$

**Step 3 — Energy stored at maximum pressure (3 Marks):**

For an isothermal gas process, the energy stored is given by:

$$E = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

Note: This is the work done by the gas during isothermal compression, using $W = \int P \, dV$ with substitution from Boyle's law.

**Substituting:**

$$E = (25 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}) \times (3.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3) \times \ln\left(\frac{150}{25}\right)$$

$$E = 7500 \times \ln(6)$$

$$\ln(6) = 1.7918$$

$$E = 7500 \times 1.7918 = 13{,}438.5 \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 13.44 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: Explain the different types of hydraulic accumulators with neat sketches. A piston-type accumulator has a piston of diameter 80 mm and stroke 200 mm. The gas side is pre-charged at 30 bar and the maximum fluid pressure is 120 bar. Calculate the volume of fluid delivered when the accumulator discharges from 120 bar to 30 bar. Assume isothermal conditions.**

#### Part (a) — Types of Accumulators (7 Marks)

**1. Weight-Loaded Accumulator (1 Mark):**

- A vertical cylinder with a piston loaded with heavy weights.
- Pressure is constant regardless of piston position.
- Bulky, used only in low-pressure systems.

**2. Spring-Loaded Accumulator (2 Marks):**

- A piston is loaded by a mechanical spring inside a cylinder.
- Pressure varies linearly with compression.
- Compact, used for low-pressure pulsation damping.

**3. Gas-Loaded (Hydro-Pneumatic) Accumulator (4 Marks):**

This is the most widely used type. It uses an inert gas (nitrogen) separated from the hydraulic fluid by a flexible or sliding barrier.

Three subtypes:

- **(a) Bladder Type:** Uses a rubber bladder. Most common for general applications. Fast response, low leakage, easy to maintain.
- **(b) Piston Type:** Uses a free-floating piston with seals. Used for high-pressure, high-capacity applications. Slower response but no bladder rupture risk.
- *(Sketch in exam: vertical cylinder with piston, bladder, or diaphragm separator, gas inlet at top, fluid port at bottom.)*

**4. Diaphragm Type (Bonus):** Compact, used for small volumes and instrumentation pulsation damping.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

- Piston diameter: $D = 80$ mm $= 0.080$ m
- Stroke (max gas expansion length): $L = 200$ mm $= 0.200$ m
- Pre-charge pressure: $P_1 = 30$ bar (abs)
- Max fluid pressure: $P_2 = 120$ bar (abs)
- Isothermal: $n = 1$

**Step 1 — Total gas volume (V₁) at pre-charge (1 Mark):**

$$
V_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 \times 0.200
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.200
$$

$$
V_1 = 0.0005027 \times 0.200 = 1.005 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{V_1 = 100.5 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 0.1005 \text{ L}}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume of gas at maximum pressure V₂ (3 Marks):**

Apply Boyle's Law:

$$
P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{P_2} = \frac{30 \times 0.1005}{120}
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{3.015}{120} = 0.0251 \text{ L}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_2 = 0.0251 \text{ L} = 25.13 \text{ cm}^3}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume of fluid delivered (3 Marks):**

$$
V_{eff} = V_1 - V_2 = 0.1005 - 0.0251
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{eff} = 0.0754 \text{ L} = 75.4 \text{ cm}^3 \approx 75.4 \text{ mL}}
$$

The piston-type accumulator can deliver **75.4 mL** of hydraulic fluid during discharge.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert pressures to absolute units** before applying Boyle's Law. If the problem gives gauge pressure (e.g., "120 bar gauge"), students must add atmospheric pressure (≈ 1 bar) to get absolute pressure. A **−2 mark** penalty is common.
> 2. **Confusing gas pre-charge pressure with maximum fluid pressure.** The pre-charge is always LOWER than minimum system pressure (typically 0.9 × P_min).
> 3. **Not stating the assumption** of isothermal vs adiabatic. KTU examiners explicitly look for "assuming isothermal conditions" in the solution.
> 4. **Drawing accumulators without labels** in sketches. Every part must be labelled: shell, bladder/diaphragm/piston, gas charging valve, fluid port.
> 5. **Using air instead of nitrogen** as the charging gas. This is a **safety violation** and may cost **1 mark** if written in safety-focused questions.
> 6. **Wrong units in numerical answers.** Always quote final answer in proper SI or commercial units (L, bar, m³).
> 7. **Skipping the safety factor** when sizing an accumulator for real application.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Accessories** are safety-critical components in fluid power systems, though they do not perform energy conversion themselves.
- **Filters** are classified by location: suction strainer, pressure line filter, return line filter, offline (kidney loop) filter.
- **Beta Ratio** is the industry standard for expressing filter efficiency.
- **Heat Exchangers** remove excess heat to keep oil in the safe operating range of **30°C to 60°C**.
- **Reservoir** capacity rule: $V_{tank} = (2 \text{ to } 3) \times Q_{pump}$.
- **FRL** = Filter + Regulator + Lubricator; essential for any pneumatic system.
- **Accumulator** = Hydraulic "battery" that stores energy as compressed gas (nitrogen).
- **Pre-charge gas is always nitrogen**, never air or oxygen (fire risk).
- **Three types** of gas-loaded accumulators: bladder, piston, diaphragm.
- **Bladder type** is the most common — used for general purpose, fast response, easy maintenance.
- **Piston type** is used for high-pressure, high-capacity, heavy-duty applications.
- **Diaphragm type** is compact, used for small volumes and instrumentation.
- **Boyle's Law** governs isothermal accumulator processes: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$.
- **Polytropic Law** governs general processes: $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$, with $n = 1.4$ for adiabatic.
- **Pre-charge pressure rule:** $P_{pre} \leq 0.9 \times P_{min}$.
- **Applications** of accumulators: energy storage, pressure stabilization, pulsation damping, emergency power, leakage and thermal expansion compensation.
- **Safety aspects:** Regular pre-charge pressure checks, periodic bladder inspection, hydrostatic testing at $1.5 \times P_{max}$, use of nitrogen only.
- **Effective volume** $V_{eff} = V_1 - V_2$ is the actual fluid delivered by the accumulator between max and min pressures.
- **Energy stored** (isothermal): $E = P_1 V_1 \ln(P_2 / P_1)$ — derived from $W = \int P \, dV$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure Switches, Fluid Power Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pressure Switches, Fluid Power Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Pressure Switch

A **pressure switch** is a safety and control device that senses the pressure of a fluid (hydraulic oil or compressed air) and **opens or closes an electrical contact** when the fluid pressure crosses a pre-set threshold value. It is the principal "translator" between the fluid domain and the electrical/electronic control domain in any fluid power system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A pressure switch is a normally open or normally closed electro-mechanical device that actuates an electrical signal at a predetermined fluid pressure, providing system protection, sequencing, and unloading functions in hydraulic and pneumatic circuits.

### 1.2 Classification of Pressure Switches (as per KTU Module 4)

| **Type** | **Sensing Element** | **Accuracy** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diaphragm Type | Elastic metal diaphragm | Medium | Low–medium pressure (up to 25 bar) |
| Piston Type | Sliding piston with seal | High | High pressure (up to 700 bar) |
| Bourdon Tube Type | Curved metal tube | Medium | Industrial pressure gauges |
| Electronic / Solid-State | Strain-gauge or piezoresistive transducer | Very High | Precision CNC, aerospace |
| Differential Pressure Switch | Dual sensing ports | High | Filter monitoring, heat exchangers |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuse of the Fluid World"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Plain English):** Think of a pressure switch exactly like the **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)** in your house electrical panel. Just as an MCB trips when current exceeds a safe value to protect wiring from burning, a pressure switch "trips" (opens or closes an electrical contact) when hydraulic/pneumatic pressure exceeds a safe limit to protect hoses, actuators, and seals from bursting. It is the silent guardian of every fluid power circuit.

### 1.4 Definition of a Fluid Power System

A **fluid power system** is an energy-transmission system in which a confined fluid (mineral oil for hydraulics, atmospheric air for pneumatics) is used to generate, control, and transmit power from a prime mover (electric motor / IC engine) to an actuator (cylinder / motor) through a closed-loop circuit comprising a **power unit, control elements, conditioning elements, and actuators**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** In Module 4 (Safety), the pressure switch is the *first line of defense* against overpressure transients, pump starvation, filter clogging, and accumulator failure. Every KTU question on fluid power safety implicitly references pressure switches.

### 1.5 Key Terminology You Must Memorize

- **Set / Cut-out Pressure (P_out):** The pressure at which the switch *activates* (changes contact state).
- **Reset / Cut-in Pressure (P_in):** The pressure at which the switch *returns* to its normal state.
- **Differential Pressure (ΔP):** The dead-band between cut-in and cut-out.
- **Proof Pressure:** Maximum pressure the switch can withstand without permanent damage (typically **1.5 ×** rated pressure).
- **Burst Pressure:** Pressure at which the sensing element ruptures (typically **3 ×** rated pressure).
- **Hysteresis:** The intentional pressure gap that prevents rapid contact chattering.
- **Normally Open (NO) / Normally Closed (NC):** Default contact state at zero pressure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The "Why" Behind the "How"

The operating logic of any pressure switch can be broken into **four sequential physical events**:

1. **Pressure Sensing:** The fluid enters the pressure port and acts on a mechanical element (diaphragm, piston, or bourdon tube). The force generated is $F = P \times A$, where $A$ is the effective sensing area.
2. **Mechanical Deflection:** The sensing element deflects against a restoring spring. At equilibrium, the spring force equals the fluid force: $P \cdot A = k \cdot x$, where $k$ is the spring stiffness and $x$ is the deflection.
3. **Electrical Actuation:** When $x$ reaches a calibrated set-point, a snap-action mechanism (or electronic comparator) toggles a microswitch / transistor output.
4. **Signal Output:** The electrical contact either energizes a relay (shutting off a pump, opening a solenoid valve) or de-energizes it (alarm, indicator light).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Hysteresis (ΔP)?** If the cut-in and cut-out were at the *exact* same pressure, any tiny fluctuation near that value would cause the contacts to "chatter" — opening and closing hundreds of times per minute, destroying the microswitch. The differential band is a **deliberate design feature** for contact life.

## 2.2 Force-Balance Equation — The Heart of Pressure Switch Design

For a piston-type pressure switch with effective piston area $A_p$ and a linear return spring:

$$P_{set} = \frac{k \cdot x_{set}}{A_p} \quad [\text{Pa}]$$

For a diaphragm-type switch (deflection is small, behaviour is non-linear beyond elastic limit):

$$P_{set} = \frac{16 \cdot E \cdot t^3 \cdot \delta}{3 \cdot R^4 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)}$$

where $E$ = Young's modulus of diaphragm, $t$ = thickness, $\delta$ = centre deflection, $R$ = effective radius, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet for Module 4

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD:** The following table contains the *most-tested* equations for pressure switches and fluid power systems. Commit them to memory; the KTU board examiner expects instant recall.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Fundamental pressure definition | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $P_{set} = \dfrac{k \cdot x}{A}$ | Spring-balanced switch setting | Pa |
| 3 | $\Delta P = P_{out} - P_{in}$ | Pressure switch differential / dead-band | Pa or bar |
| 4 | $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$ | Polytropic gas law (accumulator precharge) | — |
| 5 | $P_{hydro} = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure head | Pa |
| 6 | $P_{power} = \dfrac{P \cdot Q}{600}$ | Hydraulic power (P in bar, Q in L/min) | kW |
| 7 | $P_{pneumatic} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot Q}{1000}$ | Pneumatic power (P in bar, Q in L/s) | kW |
| 8 | $Q_{pump} = V_d \cdot N$ | Pump theoretical flow rate | m³/s |
| 9 | $E_{stored} = \dfrac{(P_1 - P_0) \cdot V_{acc}}{n - 1}$ | Accumulator energy (gas-loaded) | Joules |
| 10 | $\Delta P_{filter} = \dfrac{\mu \cdot Q \cdot R_{m}}{A_f}$ | Filter pressure drop (clogging indicator) | Pa |

> **Note on table syntax:** The vertical bar `|` is forbidden in KTU markdown tables; we have used `\vert` style equivalents where absolute-value notation was needed in derivations. All formulas above are **dimensionally consistent** and **exam-verifiable**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pressure switches are ubiquitous in:

- **Injection Moulding Machines** — clamp-force safety; if mould pressure drops, machine halts.
- **Hydraulic Presses** — overload protection; prevents die and frame damage.
- **Mobile Hydraulics (JCBs, Excavators)** — load-holding valve actuation; warning of filter clog.
- **Pneumatic Brake Systems (Trucks, Buses)** — low-pressure alarm; park-brake interlock.
- **Offshore & Subsea** — differential pressure switch detects pipeline leak by monitoring pressure decay.
- **Lubrication Systems** — pressure failure indicator triggers shutdown to prevent bearing failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A *single* properly calibrated pressure switch can prevent a ₹50 lakh hydraulic cylinder from failing catastrophically. The ROI of pressure switches is the highest in the entire safety-instrumentation hierarchy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Balance and Spring Selection for a Piston Pressure Switch

**Given:** A hydraulic system operates at a maximum working pressure of $P_{max} = 200$ bar. A piston-type pressure switch with effective piston area $A_p = 100 \text{ mm}^2$ is to be calibrated. The available spring has stiffness $k = 50 \text{ N/mm}$ and a free length of $L_0 = 40 \text{ mm}$.

**Find:** (a) The spring deflection required to set the cut-out pressure, (b) The cut-in deflection if the differential is set to 30 bar.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Convert all units to SI (SI is the *only* unit system accepted by KTU board for derivation questions).

$$
P_{max} = 200 \text{ bar} = 200 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 2.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
A_p = 100 \text{ mm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the fundamental pressure equation to find the force on the piston at maximum working pressure.

$$
F_{fluid} = P_{max} \times A_p
$$

$$
F_{fluid} = (2.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2) = 2000 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the spring force balance $F_{fluid} = k \cdot x$ to find the cut-out deflection $x_{out}$.

$$
x_{out} = \frac{F_{fluid}}{k} = \frac{2000 \text{ N}}{50 \text{ N/mm}} = 40 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The required deflection equals the *free length* of the spring! This means the chosen spring is **under-designed**. A real engineer would select a stiffer spring (e.g., $k = 100$ N/mm → $x_{out} = 20$ mm).

**Step 4:** Calculate the cut-in pressure. The differential is $\Delta P = 30$ bar.

$$
P_{in} = P_{out} - \Delta P = 200 - 30 = 170 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 5:** Convert cut-in pressure to force and find the cut-in deflection $x_{in}$.

$$
F_{in} = P_{in} \times A_p = (170 \times 10^{5}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-4}) = 1700 \text{ N}
$$

$$
x_{in} = \frac{F_{in}}{k} = \frac{1700 \text{ N}}{50 \text{ N/mm}} = 34 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6:** Express the **differential deflection** (the hysteresis travel).

$$
\Delta x = x_{out} - x_{in} = 40 - 34 = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:** For this pressure switch, a 6 mm change in spring deflection produces a 30 bar change in fluid pressure. This ratio $\dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{30 \text{ bar}}{6 \text{ mm}} = 5 \text{ bar/mm}$ is the *sensitivity* of the switch.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Accumulator Sizing for a Pressure-Switch-Controlled Pneumatic System

**Given:** A pneumatic system has a receiver (storage tank) of volume $V = 0.5 \text{ m}^3$. The compressor delivers air at pressure $P_{comp} = 8$ bar (gauge). The pressure switch is calibrated to cut-out at $P_{out} = 7$ bar and cut-in at $P_{in} = 5$ bar (gauge). Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 1.013$ bar. Assume isothermal compression for slow processes ($n = 1$).

**Find:** (a) Air delivered per pump cycle, (b) Time between cut-out and cut-in (unloading interval).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Convert all gauge pressures to absolute pressures (Boyle's Law requires absolute values).

$$
P_{out,abs} = 7 + 1.013 = 8.013 \text{ bar} \approx 8.0 \text{ bar}
$$

$$
P_{in,abs} = 5 + 1.013 = 6.013 \text{ bar} \approx 6.0 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Boyle's Law (isothermal, $n = 1$): $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ to find the air mass (in terms of free-air equivalent volume at atmospheric pressure) stored in the receiver at the two pressure levels.

At cut-out pressure:

$$
V_{air,out} = \frac{P_{out,abs} \cdot V}{P_{atm}} = \frac{8.0 \times 0.5}{1.013} = 3.949 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (free air)}
$$

At cut-in pressure:

$$
V_{air,in} = \frac{P_{in,abs} \cdot V}{P_{atm}} = \frac{6.0 \times 0.5}{1.013} = 2.962 \text{ m}^3 \text{ (free air)}
$$

**Step 3:** Air delivered per cycle (the *useful* air the system consumes between cut-out and cut-in).

$$
\Delta V_{air} = V_{air,out} - V_{air,in} = 3.949 - 2.962 = 0.987 \text{ m}^3 \text{ per cycle}
$$

**Step 4:** Express the result in litres for practical engineering readability.

$$
\Delta V_{air} = 987 \text{ litres per cycle}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that the receiver volume (0.5 m³) is much smaller than the equivalent free-air volume stored (≈4 m³). This is the *amplification effect* of pressure — a small pressure vessel stores a huge amount of useful air.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Pressure Switch Type

| **Design Parameter** | **Diaphragm Type** | **Piston Type** | **Electronic Type** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure Range | 0.2 – 25 bar | 10 – 700 bar | 0.05 – 1000 bar |
| Accuracy Class | ±2% of FS | ±1% of FS | ±0.25% of FS |
| Response Time | 5 – 50 ms | 10 – 100 ms | < 1 ms |
| Hysteresis | Fixed (10–15%) | Adjustable | Software-configurable |
| Vibration Tolerance | Poor (membrane fatigue) | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cost Index (relative) | 1× | 1.5× | 4× |
| Best for KTU Safety Application | Compressor cut-off | Hydraulic overload | Precision CNC, aerospace |

## 3.4 Safety Margin Calculation (Bonus for Advanced Students)

The **factor of safety** for a pressure switch selection is:

$$
FoS = \frac{P_{proof}}{P_{max,operating}}
$$

KTU Code of Practice (and IS 10416) recommends $FoS \geq 1.5$ for hydraulic pressure switches and $FoS \geq 1.25$ for pneumatic. Always check manufacturer datasheet and verify against the system's **maximum allowable working pressure (MAWP)** stamped on the receiver/accumulator.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Pressure Switch in a Fluid Power Safety Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electric Motor] --> B[Variable Displacement Pump]
    B --> C{Pressure Switch\nCut-out Setting}
    C -->|Below set-point| D[Flow to Directional Valve]
    D --> E[Hydraulic Cylinder / Pneumatic Motor]
    E --> F[Return Line]
    F --> G[Filter with Differential Switch]
    G --> H[Reservoir / Receiver Tank]
    H --> A
    C -->|Above set-point| I[Solenoid Unloading Valve]
    I --> J[Pump Unloaded to Tank at Zero Pressure]
    C -->|Pressure Signal| K[PLC / Microcontroller Input]
    K --> L[Alarm Buzzer and Indicator Lamp]
    G -->|Pressure Drop Signal| K
```

**Figure 4.1:** Block-level functional architecture showing the *three roles* of pressure switches in a hydraulic safety loop — (1) unloading control, (2) filter clogging indication, (3) system-wide alarm feedback to the PLC.

## 4.2 Operating Logic Flowchart — Cut-in / Cut-out Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[System Pressure Rising] --> Q1{P > P_out?}
    Q1 -->|No| P1
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Switch Contact Toggles]
    R1 --> S1[De-energize Pump Contactor]
    S1 --> T1[Unloading Valve Opens]
    T1 --> U1[System Pressure Begins to Fall]
    U1 --> V1{P < P_in?}
    V1 -->|No| U1
    V1 -->|Yes| W1[Switch Resets to Normal]
    W1 --> X1[Re-energize Pump]
    X1 --> P1
```

**Figure 4.2:** Hysteresis loop showing how the pressure switch prevents rapid on/off cycling by requiring the pressure to drop from $P_{out}$ to $P_{in}$ before re-starting the pump.

## 4.3 Internal Construction — Diaphragm-Type Pressure Switch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    I1[Pressure Port Inlet] --> J1[Pressure Chamber]
    J1 --> K1[Elastomeric Diaphragm]
    K1 --> L1[Push Rod]
    L1 --> M1[Snap-Action Mechanism]
    M1 --> N1[Micro Switch NO/NC Contacts]
    M1 --> O1[Return Spring Adjustable]
    N1 --> P1[Electrical Terminal Block]
    O1 --> K1
```

**Figure 4.3:** Schematic cross-section of a diaphragm-type pressure switch. The pressure pushes the diaphragm, the push rod transfers motion to the snap-action mechanism, which toggles the microswitch. The adjustable return spring sets the cut-in pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** The examiner expects you to **label at least 6 components** in a pressure switch diagram. A labelled sketch of the diaphragm, spring, push rod, snap-action, microswitch, and terminal block fetches full marks. Memorise the snap-action mechanism — it is the *most-asked* detail.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Pressure Switch. List its main types. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pressure switch** is an electro-mechanical or solid-state device that opens or closes an electrical circuit when the fluid pressure in a hydraulic or pneumatic system reaches a pre-determined value. It serves three safety functions: (i) overpressure protection, (ii) pump unloading, and (iii) filter clogging indication.

**Main Types (3 of the 6):**
1. **Diaphragm-type** — for low-to-medium pressure applications.
2. **Piston-type** — for high-pressure hydraulic systems.
3. **Bourdon tube-type** — for industrial process instrumentation.
4. **Electronic (strain-gauge / piezoresistive)** — for precision control.
5. **Differential pressure switch** — for filter and heat-exchanger monitoring.

**(Scoring tip: 1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 types = 3 marks total.)**

---

### Question 2: Explain the terms Cut-in Pressure, Cut-out Pressure, and Differential Pressure with a sketch. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Cut-out Pressure ($P_{out}$):** The pressure at which the pressure switch *changes state* (actuates). It is the **higher** of the two settings.
- **Cut-in Pressure ($P_{in}$):** The pressure at which the switch *returns* to its normal position. It is the **lower** setting.
- **Differential Pressure ($\Delta P$):** The numerical difference $\Delta P = P_{out} - P_{in}$. It is also called the *dead band* or *hysteresis band*.

$$
\Delta P = P_{out} - P_{in}
$$

**Typical example:** A compressor pressure switch is set to cut-out at 8 bar and cut-in at 6 bar, giving $\Delta P = 2$ bar. This 2 bar gap prevents rapid on-off cycling of the motor contactor.

**(Scoring tip: 1 mark each for the three terms.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** The most common mistakes students make on this topic are (1) confusing gauge vs absolute pressure in gas-law problems, (2) omitting unit conversions from bar to Pa, and (3) forgetting to state the direction of the hysteresis. **Three marks are routinely lost** for these. Always write "gauge" or "absolute" beside every pressure value!

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **diaphragm-type pressure switch**. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

A diaphragm-type pressure switch consists of the following components (refer to Figure 4.3 in Section 4):

1. **Pressure port** — threaded inlet connected to the system piping.
2. **Pressure chamber** — confines the fluid against the diaphragm.
3. **Diaphragm** — a circular disc of phosphor-bronze or stainless steel (thickness 0.1 – 0.5 mm).
4. **Push rod** — transmits diaphragm deflection to the snap-action.
5. **Snap-action mechanism** — a spring-loaded lever that provides a crisp, bistable toggle (prevents contact chattering).
6. **Microswitch** — SPDT (single-pole double-throw) electrical contact rated 5 A at 240 V AC.
7. **Adjustment screw** — sets the spring pre-load, hence the cut-in/cut-out pressure.
8. **Terminal block** — for external electrical wiring (NO, NC, COM).

**Working:** Fluid pressure enters the chamber and exerts a force $F = P \cdot A$ on the diaphragm. The diaphragm deflects by an amount $\delta$ against the return spring. When the deflection exceeds the calibrated set value, the push rod triggers the snap-action, which toggles the microswitch. The differential is created by an internal *range spring* that must be overcome before the switch resets.

**Advantages:** Compact, low cost, suitable for clean fluids, good repeatability (±1% of full scale), no moving seals in the wetted area.

**Limitations:** Limited pressure range (typically ≤ 25 bar), diaphragm fatigue in pulsating systems, not suitable for high-viscosity or dirty fluids.

**(b)** A hydraulic system operates at a maximum working pressure of **250 bar**. A piston-type pressure switch is used with effective piston area **$A_p = 150 \text{ mm}^2$** and a return spring of stiffness **$k = 80 \text{ N/mm}$**. The pressure differential is set to **40 bar**.

Calculate: (i) The spring deflection at cut-out, (ii) The spring deflection at cut-in, (iii) The force exerted on the microswitch actuator at cut-out. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
$P_{out} = 250 \text{ bar} = 250 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2.5 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$
$A_p = 150 \text{ mm}^2 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
$k = 80 \text{ N/mm} = 80{,}000 \text{ N/m}$
$\Delta P = 40 \text{ bar} = 40 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 4.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$

**(i) Spring deflection at cut-out ($x_{out}$):**

Fluid force at cut-out:

$$
F_{out} = P_{out} \times A_p = (2.5 \times 10^7) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 3750 \text{ N}
$$

Spring deflection (using Hooke's law $F = k \cdot x$):

$$
x_{out} = \frac{F_{out}}{k} = \frac{3750 \text{ N}}{80 \times 10^3 \text{ N/m}} = 0.0469 \text{ m} = 46.9 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Spring deflection at cut-in ($x_{in}$):**

Cut-in pressure:

$$
P_{in} = P_{out} - \Delta P = 250 - 40 = 210 \text{ bar} = 2.1 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}
$$

Fluid force at cut-in:

$$
F_{in} = P_{in} \times A_p = (2.1 \times 10^7) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-4}) = 3150 \text{ N}
$$

Spring deflection:

$$
x_{in} = \frac{F_{in}}{k} = \frac{3150}{80 \times 10^3} = 0.0394 \text{ m} = 39.4 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Force on the microswitch actuator at cut-out:**

The microswitch actuator experiences the same fluid force transmitted through the push rod, minus the spring reaction at the moment of snap-action. Since at the moment of trip the spring is fully loaded:

$$
F_{actuator} = F_{out} - F_{spring,loaded} = F_{out} - k \cdot x_{out}
$$

But since equilibrium holds ($F_{out} = k \cdot x_{out}$ at the moment of trip), the *net* force on the actuator is the snap-action over-travel force — practically a few newtons. The **maximum fluid force to be withstood by the diaphragm and the piston seal is 3750 N**.

> **Answer:** $x_{out} = 46.9 \text{ mm}$, $x_{in} = 39.4 \text{ mm}$, $F_{max} = 3750 \text{ N}$.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss the **role of pressure switches in fluid power system safety**. Cover overpressure protection, pump unloading, filter monitoring, and low-pressure alarms. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Pressure switches are the **first line of electronic defence** in a fluid power safety hierarchy. Their four critical safety roles are:

1. **Overpressure Protection (System Burst Prevention):** The pressure switch monitors the system line and trips the motor contactor (or activates a relief valve) the instant the line pressure exceeds the MAWP. This prevents catastrophic failure of hoses, cylinders, and accumulators. *Example:* A 350 bar hose rated to 400 bar — pressure switch set at 380 bar.

2. **Pump Unloading (Energy Conservation):** When the system reaches the cut-out pressure, the switch de-energises the pump or opens an unloading valve, allowing the pump to deliver zero flow at near-zero pressure. This reduces power consumption by 60 – 80% during idle periods.

3. **Filter Clogging Indication (Maintenance Trigger):** A differential pressure switch is installed across the filter element. As the filter clogs, $\Delta P$ increases. When it exceeds a threshold (typically 0.8 bar), the switch triggers a maintenance alarm — preventing pump cavitation and valve stiction due to suction-side vacuum.

4. **Low-Pressure Alarm (Failure Detection):** A pressure switch set at a low threshold (e.g., 80% of normal) detects pump failure, hose burst, or actuator seal leakage. It triggers an immediate machine stoppage and an audio-visual alarm. *Critical application:* Pneumatic brake systems in commercial vehicles — if brake-line pressure drops below 4.5 bar, the vehicle must not move.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus for Top Marks:** Mention the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** classification. Pressure switches in safety circuits are typically rated **SIL 1 or SIL 2** per IEC 61508. Hydraulic press protection requires **SIL 3**.

**(b)** A pneumatic system has a receiver of volume **$V = 1.0 \text{ m}^3$**. The compressor delivers air at **$P_{comp} = 9$ bar (gauge)**. The pressure switch is calibrated to **cut-out at 8 bar and cut-in at 6 bar (gauge)**. Atmospheric pressure is **1.013 bar**. The leak rate of the system is **$Q_{leak} = 5 \text{ L/min}$** (at atmospheric pressure). Calculate: (i) Air delivered per cycle (in free-air litres), (ii) The time interval between successive pump starts (unloading time). **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
$V_{receiver} = 1.0 \text{ m}^3$
$P_{out} = 8 \text{ bar (gauge)}$, $P_{in} = 6 \text{ bar (gauge)}$
$P_{atm} = 1.013 \text{ bar}$
$Q_{leak} = 5 \text{ L/min}$ (free-air equivalent)

**Absolute Pressures:**
$P_{out,abs} = 8 + 1.013 = 9.013 \text{ bar}$
$P_{in,abs} = 6 + 1.013 = 7.013 \text{ bar}$

**(i) Air delivered per cycle (Boyle's Law, isothermal, $n = 1$):**

Free-air volume at cut-out:

$$
V_{air,out} = \frac{P_{out,abs} \times V}{P_{atm}} = \frac{9.013 \times 1.0}{1.013} = 8.897 \text{ m}^3
$$

Free-air volume at cut-in:

$$
V_{air,in} = \frac{P_{in,abs} \times V}{P_{atm}} = \frac{7.013 \times 1.0}{1.013} = 6.923 \text{ m}^3
$$

Air delivered per cycle:

$$
\Delta V = V_{air,out} - V_{air,in} = 8.897 - 6.923 = 1.974 \text{ m}^3 = 1974 \text{ L (free air)}
$$

**(ii) Unloading time (time for pressure to drop from $P_{out}$ to $P_{in}$ at leak rate $Q_{leak}$):**

The receiver must lose the equivalent free-air volume $\Delta V$ at the leak rate:

$$
t_{unload} = \frac{\Delta V}{Q_{leak}} = \frac{1974 \text{ L}}{5 \text{ L/min}} = 394.8 \text{ min} \approx 6.58 \text{ hours}
$$

> **Answer:** (i) $\Delta V = 1974 \text{ L/cycle}$ (free air), (ii) $t_{unload} \approx 6.58 \text{ hours}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** The leak rate $Q_{leak}$ must be expressed in the **same pressure basis** as $\Delta V$. Here, $Q_{leak} = 5$ L/min is at atmospheric (free-air) condition — so we use the free-air $\Delta V$ directly. If $Q_{leak}$ were at line pressure, you would need to convert it to free-air basis first by dividing by $P_{line,abs} / P_{atm}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Last-Minute Revision (Save This Section!):**

- A **pressure switch** converts a fluid pressure signal into an electrical on/off signal — the "translator" between hydraulic and electronic domains.
- The **three** main types are **diaphragm, piston, and electronic (transducer)**; choose by pressure range, accuracy, and cost.
- The **fundamental force equation** is $P \cdot A = k \cdot x$; this determines the spring selection for a given switch setting.
- **Cut-out > Cut-in.** The difference is the **differential** (dead-band), which prevents contact chattering. Typical differential = 10–20% of set pressure.
- **Proof pressure** ≥ **1.5 × rated**, **Burst pressure** ≥ **3 × rated** — always verify these in the manufacturer datasheet.
- In **pneumatic** receiver sizing, always use **Boyle's Law** with **absolute** pressures: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ (isothermal) or $P_1 V_1^n = P_2 V_2^n$ (polytropic, $n = 1.4$ for adiabatic).
- **Four safety roles** of a pressure switch: (1) Overpressure trip, (2) Pump unloading, (3) Filter clogging alert, (4) Low-pressure alarm.
- For the **hydraulic power** formula, remember the constant **600** (for bar × L/min → kW) — most-asked formula in Module 4.
- Always specify **gauge or absolute** in your answer — a 1-mark deduction occurs otherwise in KTU board valuation.
- **Snap-action mechanism** is the *single most-asked sketch component* — be ready to draw and label it.
- The **factor of safety** for hydraulic switches should be **≥ 1.5**; for pneumatic **≥ 1.25** (per IS 10416).
- **Differential pressure switches** are used across filters and heat exchangers — they measure *$\Delta P$*, not absolute pressure.
- A **single pressure switch failure** must be detectable — always use a *normally closed* contact for safety shutdowns (fail-safe principle).
- KTU examiners reward **neat, fully labelled diagrams** — practise the diaphragm switch cross-section before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — SIMPLE HYDRAULIC CIRCUITS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Simple Hydraulic Circuit** is a closed, self-contained fluid power system that uses an incompressible working fluid (typically mineral-based hydraulic oil conforming to **ISO VG 32 / VG 46** viscosity grades) to transmit, control, and convert energy between a prime mover (electric motor + pump) and one or more linear (hydraulic cylinder) or rotary (hydraulic motor) actuators. The circuit must comply with **ISO 1219-1:2012** (graphic symbols) and **ISO 4413** (general rules for hydraulic systems and components).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *Module 4 emphasizes the design logic, component selection, and functional analysis of elementary hydraulic circuits — including single-acting, double-acting, regenerative, speed-control, and pressure-control circuits.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a hydraulic circuit like the **human circulatory system**:
- The **pump** is the heart, pushing fluid continuously.
- The **oil reservoir** is the body's blood pool.
- The **valves** are the valves in veins, directing flow.
- The **cylinder** is a muscle, doing the actual mechanical work.
- The **relief valve** is a safety pressure release, like a pressure-release valve in the brain.

When you press a lever (directional control valve), fluid flows into a chamber, and a piston pushes outward — like squeezing a water-filled syringe to push a rod forward.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value/Range |
|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Oil Density** | **ρ ≈ 870 kg/m³** (at 40 °C) |
| **Bulk Modulus (Oil)** | **β ≈ 1.4 – 2.0 GPa** |
| **Kinematic Viscosity** | **ν = 32–46 cSt** (ISO VG 32/46) |
| **Standard Working Pressure** | **70 – 210 bar** (typical industrial) |
| **Standard Test Pressure** | **1.5 × Working Pressure** |
| **System Efficiency Target** | **η_total ≥ 80%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Hydraulic Circuit:** A combination of interconnected hydraulic components arranged to perform useful work, controlled by the flow rate (Q) and pressure (P) of the working fluid according to the fundamental power equation **P = ΔP × Q**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Building Blocks of a Simple Hydraulic Circuit

Every simple hydraulic circuit, regardless of complexity, contains the following five functional subsystems:

1. **Power Source (P):** Electric motor + hydraulic pump (fixed or variable displacement).
2. **Reservoir (T):** Storage, cooling, de-aeration, and contamination control of the fluid.
3. **Control Elements:** Directional Control Valves (DCV), Pressure Control Valves (PCV), Flow Control Valves (FCV).
4. **Actuator (A/B):** Single-acting cylinder (SAC), Double-acting cylinder (DAC), or hydraulic motor.
5. **Auxiliary Elements:** Filters, accumulators, pressure gauges, hoses, fittings, and heat exchangers.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Flow

- **Step 1:** The electric motor drives the pump, which draws oil from the reservoir through a suction strainer.
- **Step 2:** The pressurized oil is delivered to a Pressure Relief Valve (PRV), set slightly above maximum working pressure.
- **Step 3:** A Directional Control Valve (DCV) directs the flow either to the cap-end (head end) or rod-end of the cylinder.
- **Step 4:** The cylinder piston converts fluid pressure into linear mechanical force.
- **Step 5:** Exhaust oil returns to the reservoir through a return-line filter.
- **Step 6:** A Flow Control Valve (FCV) regulates actuator speed by metering flow rate.

### 2.3 Classification of Simple Hydraulic Circuits

| Circuit Type | Primary Function | Key Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Acting Cylinder Circuit** | Extension via pump; retraction by gravity/spring | 3/2 DCV, check valve |
| **Double-Acting Cylinder Circuit** | Powered extension & retraction | 4/2 or 4/3 DCV |
| **Regenerative Circuit** | Faster extension using both pump & exhaust flow | 4/3 DCV (tandem center) + check valves |
| **Speed Control Circuit** | Meter-in / Meter-out speed regulation | FCV, DCV, check valve |
| **Pressure Control Circuit** | Pressure sequencing, unloading, counterbalancing | PCV (relief, sequence, unloading, counterbalance) |
| **Synchronizing Circuit** | Equal motion of two cylinders | Flow dividers, equal-area cylinders |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = P \times A$ | Force = Pressure × Piston Area | N, Pa, m² | Cylinder thrust force |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2}$ | Full bore (cap-end) area | m² | Effective piston area |
| 3 | $A_{r} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (D^{2} - d^{2})$ | Annular (rod-end) area | m² | Effective rod-side area |
| 4 | $Q = A \times v$ | Flow = Area × Velocity | m³/s | Cylinder extension speed |
| 5 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Velocity of piston | m/s | Speed calculation |
| 6 | $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \times Q$ | Hydraulic Power | W | Power transmission |
| 7 | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A \times L}{Q}$ | Cylinder stroke time | s | Time delay |
| 8 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | % | Pump/cylinder losses |
| 9 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{theoretical}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | % | Friction losses |
| 10 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$ | Overall efficiency | % | System performance |
| 11 | $P_{pump} = \dfrac{\eta_{pump}}{Q_{pump} \times p_{pump}}$ | Pump input power | W | Motor sizing |
| 12 | $\Delta P_{line} = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi d^{4}}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille line loss | Pa | Pressure drop in pipe |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU 2024 Note:** When using formula 1 in problems, always use **gauge pressure** (not absolute), and account for the **back-pressure** in the return line when computing rod-end force.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Injection Molding Machines:** Clamping and injection units use double-acting cylinders with regenerative circuits.
- **Earth-moving Equipment (JCB, Excavators):** Multiple DAC circuits with counterbalance valves for safe load holding.
- **Aircraft Landing Gear:** Hydraulic DAC systems with sequence valves for synchronized retraction.
- **Hydraulic Presses:** High-pressure circuits (up to **700 bar**) with pressure intensification.
- **Ship Steering Gear:** Double-acting cylinders with follow-up control.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cylinder Output Force

The most fundamental relationship in hydraulic circuits. Pressure acting on a piston area produces linear force.

**Given:**
- Cylinder bore diameter: $D$ (m)
- System pressure: $P$ (Pa)
- Rod diameter: $d$ (m)

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify the piston face area exposed to pressure.} \\
\text{Cap-end (full bore) area} \quad A_{c} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Apply Pascal's Law — pressure acts equally on all areas.} \\
F_{c} &= P \times A_{c} \\[6pt]
F_{c} &= P \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: For rod-end, subtract the rod cross-section.} \\
A_{r} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (D^{2} - d^{2}) \\[6pt]
F_{r} &= P \times A_{r} = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (D^{2} - d^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The rod-end force is always less than the cap-end force for the **same pressure** because the effective area is reduced by the rod.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cylinder Extension/Retraction Velocity

Flow rate entering the cylinder chamber determines piston velocity.

**Given:** Flow rate $Q$ (m³/s), Cylinder bore $D$ (m), Rod diameter $d$ (m).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Volumetric flow into a chamber = Area × Velocity.} \\
Q &= A \times v \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Solve for velocity during extension (cap-end fill).} \\
v_{ext} &= \frac{Q}{A_{c}} = \frac{Q}{\frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Solve for velocity during retraction (rod-end fill).} \\
v_{ret} &= \frac{Q}{A_{r}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stroke Time Calculation

**Given:** Cylinder bore $D$ (m), Rod diameter $d$ (m), Stroke $L$ (m), Pump flow $Q$ (m³/s).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Volume of oil required to fill cap-end chamber.} \\
V_{c} &= A_{c} \times L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2} \times L \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Time for full extension (assuming no losses).} \\
t_{ext} &= \frac{V_{c}}{Q} = \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4Q} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Time for full retraction.} \\
t_{ret} &= \frac{V_{r}}{Q} = \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2}) L}{4Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Since $A_{r} < A_{c}$, it follows that $t_{ret} < t_{ext}$ for the same flow rate. Therefore, **retraction is always faster** than extension (for the same Q).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hydraulic Power & Pump Sizing

**Given:** System pressure $P$ (Pa), Flow rate $Q$ (m³/s), Pump efficiency $\eta_{pump}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Hydraulic (output) power of the pump.} \\
P_{hyd} &= \Delta P \times Q \quad [\text{Watts}] \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Mechanical (input) power required at pump shaft.} \\
P_{mech} &= \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{\Delta P \times Q}{\eta_{pump}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Electric motor power rating (with motor efficiency).} \\
P_{motor} &= \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 80$ mm, rod diameter $d = 45$ mm, stroke $L = 500$ mm. The pump delivers $Q = 40$ L/min at a system pressure of $P = 120$ bar. Calculate: (a) Extension force, (b) Retraction force, (c) Extension time, (d) Retraction time, (e) Hydraulic power.

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 0.080 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.045 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.500 \text{ m} \\
P &= 120 \text{ bar} = 120 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 1.20 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
Q &= 40 \text{ L/min} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(a) Extension Force (Cap-end):} \\
A_{c} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.080)^{2} \\
&= 0.7854 \times 0.0064 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\[6pt]
F_{c} &= P \times A_{c} = 1.20 \times 10^{7} \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 60{,}319 \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(b) Retraction Force (Rod-end):} \\
A_{r} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (D^{2} - d^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.0064 - 0.002025) \\
&= 0.7854 \times 0.004375 = 3.437 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\[6pt]
F_{r} &= P \times A_{r} = 1.20 \times 10^{7} \times 3.437 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 41{,}240 \text{ N} \approx 41.24 \text{ kN} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(c) Extension Time:} \\
t_{ext} &= \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4Q} = \frac{\pi \times (0.080)^{2} \times 0.500}{4 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{0.01005}{2.667 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.77 \text{ s} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(d) Retraction Time:} \\
t_{ret} &= \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2}) L}{4Q} = \frac{\pi \times 0.004375 \times 0.500}{4 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{0.006873}{2.667 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.58 \text{ s} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(e) Hydraulic Power:} \\
P_{hyd} &= \Delta P \times Q = 1.20 \times 10^{7} \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 8000 \text{ W} = 8.00 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Extension Force $F_{c}$ | **60.32 kN** |
| Retraction Force $F_{r}$ | **41.24 kN** |
| Extension Time $t_{ext}$ | **3.77 s** |
| Retraction Time $t_{ret}$ | **2.58 s** |
| Hydraulic Power $P_{hyd}$ | **8.00 kW** |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Simple Circuit Types

| Circuit | Valve Used | Pump Type | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single-Acting | 3/2 DCV | Fixed | Simple, cheap | Lower force, gravity dependent |
| Double-Acting | 4/2 DCV | Fixed | Force both ways | More components |
| Regenerative | 4/3 DCV (Tandem) | Fixed | Faster extension | Reduced extension force |
| Meter-in Speed | 2-way FCV | Fixed | Controlled speed | Heat generation |
| Meter-out Speed | 2-way FCV | Fixed | Prevents over-running | Cavitation risk |
| Counterbalance | Pilot-operated PCV | Fixed | Holds load safely | Heat dissipation |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Simple Hydraulic Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[M1: Electric Motor 3-phase 415V] --> B[P1: Hydraulic Pump Fixed/Variable]
    B --> C[T1: Reservoir 200L capacity]
    C --> B
    B --> D[V1: Relief Valve Set 150 bar]
    D --> E[V2: DCV 4/3 Solenoid]
    E --> F[F1: Flow Control Valve Meter-in]
    F --> G[A1: Double-Acting Cylinder Bore 80mm]
    G --> E
    E --> H[F2: Return Line Filter 10 micron]
    H --> C
    G --> I[Load External 50kN]
    E --> J[V3: Pressure Gauge 0-250 bar]
    E --> K[V4: Accumulator 10L pre-charge 100 bar]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Reading:** Follow the arrows in the direction of **power flow** (pump → control → actuator → return). This is the KTU-recommended way to sketch functional schematics in theory exams.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Single-Acting Cylinder Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1([P: Pump]) --> PRV{V1: Relief Valve}
    PRV --> DCV1{V2: 3-2 DCV Spring Offset}
    DCV1 -->|Energized| SAC1[A: Single-Acting Cylinder]
    DCV1 -->|De-energized| RET1[Return to Tank]
    SAC1 -->|Spring Return| RET1
    PRV --> RET1
    RET1 --> T1([T: Reservoir])
    SAC1 --> LOAD1((Load Lifts Up))
```

**Operating Sequence:**

| Step | DCV Position | Cylinder Action | Fluid Path |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal (Spring Offset) | Up | Pump → Tank (unloaded) |
| 2 | Actuated | Down | Pump → Cap-end → Cylinder extends |
| 3 | Released | Up (Spring) | Cylinder retracts → Tank |

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Double-Acting Cylinder Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP([P1: Pump]) --> RV{V1: Relief Valve}
    RV --> DCV{V2: 4-3 DCV Closed Center}
    DCV -->|Position A| CAP[A1 Cap-end Line]
    DCV -->|Position B| ROD[A2 Rod-end Line]
    CAP --> CYL[Cylinder Bore D 80mm]
    ROD --> CYL
    CYL --> LOAD((External Load 60kN))
    DCV --> FCV{V3: FCV Meter-out}
    FCV --> FILTER[F1: Return Filter 10 micron]
    FILTER --> TANK([T1: Reservoir 200L])
    RV --> TANK
    DCV --> PG((PG: Pressure Gauge))
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Regenerative Circuit (Speed Boost)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PUMP([P1: Pump Q 40 L/min]) --> DCV{V2: 4-3 DCV Tandem Center}
    DCV -->|Path 1| CV1{CV1: Check Valve}
    CV1 --> CAP[A1: Cap-end]
    CAP --> CYL[Cylinder Piston]
    CYL --> ROD[A2: Rod-end]
    ROD --> CV2{CV2: Check Valve}
    ROD --> DCV
    CV1 --> DCV
    DCV --> RET[Return Line]
    RET --> TANK([T1: Reservoir])
```

**How Regeneration Works (KTU 2024 Concept):**

| Phase | Flow Path | Effective Q | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| Without Regen | Pump → Cap-end only | $Q$ | Normal speed |
| With Regen | Pump + Rod-end → Cap-end | $Q + Q_{rod}$ | **2× speed (approx.)** |
| Trade-off | Force reduced by ratio $A_{r}/A_{c}$ | — | Lower thrust |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic:** Regenerative circuits are commonly asked in Part B. Remember the force reduction factor equals $A_{r}/A_{c}$.

---

## 4.5 Circuit Symbols Reference (ISO 1219-1)

| Component | ISO Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Pump (Fixed) | Circle with solid arrow | Constant displacement |
| Pump (Variable) | Circle with diagonal arrow | Variable displacement |
| DCV 4/3 | Two adjacent squares with arrows | Directional control |
| Relief Valve | Square with diagonal arrow + spring | Pressure limiting |

| Filter | Dashed rectangle with X | Contamination control |
| Cylinder (DAC) | Rectangle with piston rod | Linear actuator |
| Reservoir | Open trapezoid | Fluid storage |
