# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define a hydraulic circuit. List any four essential components of a simple hydraulic circuit with one-line functions.**

**Model Answer:**

A hydraulic circuit is a closed-loop arrangement of hydraulic components that transmits power using pressurized fluid according to $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \times Q$.

**Essential Components:**

1. **Hydraulic Pump** — Converts mechanical energy to hydraulic energy.
2. **Reservoir** — Stores, cools, and de-aerates the fluid.
3. **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** — Directs fluid flow path.
4. **Actuator (Cylinder/Motor)** — Converts hydraulic energy back to mechanical work.

*(Acceptable: Relief Valve, Filter, Accumulator, Flow Control Valve — any four valid)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between meter-in and meter-out speed control circuits in hydraulics.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Meter-In Control | Meter-Out Control |
|---|---|---|
| Location of FCV | On inlet line to actuator | On outlet line from actuator |
| Controls | Flow entering actuator | Flow leaving actuator |
| Best For | Resistance loads | Over-running loads |
| Cavitation Risk | Low | Higher (if mis-tuned) |
| Heat Dissipation | Heat enters actuator | Heat stays in return line |
| Typical Use | Horizontal pushing | Vertical lowering with load |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **double-acting cylinder circuit** using a 4/3 directional control valve. List the function of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 100$ mm and rod diameter $d = 60$ mm. It operates at a pressure of 140 bar with a pump flow of 60 L/min. Calculate the extension force, retraction force, extension time, and retraction time for a stroke of 600 mm. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Double-Acting Cylinder Circuit

**Components and Functions:**

1. **Pump (P1):** Delivers pressurized oil (Q = 60 L/min, P = 140 bar).
2. **Reservoir (T1):** Stores hydraulic oil ISO VG 46; capacity 250 L.
3. **Relief Valve (V1):** Safety device set at 150 bar to protect against overpressure.
4. **4/3 DCV (V2):** Directs flow to either cap-end (extend) or rod-end (retract). Spring-centred, solenoid-operated.
5. **Double-Acting Cylinder (A1):** Bore 100 mm, Stroke 600 mm. Performs linear work.
6. **Pressure Gauge (PG):** Monitors system pressure (0–250 bar).
7. **Return Filter (F1):** 10-micron filtration, ISO 4406 18/16/13 cleanliness.

**Working (Extension Phase — DCV in Position A):**

- Solenoid A energised → DCV shifts to left position.
- Pump flow enters cap-end of cylinder.
- Piston extends; rod-end oil returns to tank through DCV.

**Working (Retraction Phase — DCV in Position B):**

- Solenoid B energised → DCV shifts to right position.
- Pump flow enters rod-end of cylinder.
- Piston retracts; cap-end oil returns to tank through DCV.

**Neutral (Spring-Centred):** Pump flow unloaded to tank; cylinder is hydraulically locked.

---

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Calculations

**Given Data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 0.100 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.060 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.600 \text{ m} \\
P &= 140 \text{ bar} = 1.40 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
Q &= 60 \text{ L/min} = 1.00 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) Extension Force (Cap-end):} \\
A_{c} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.100)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\
F_{ext} &= P \times A_{c} = 1.40 \times 10^{7} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 1.0996 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} = \mathbf{109.96 \text{ kN}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(ii) Retraction Force (Rod-end):} \\
A_{r} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.100^{2} - 0.060^{2}) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \\
&= 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2} \\
F_{ret} &= 1.40 \times 10^{7} \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 7.037 \times 10^{4} \text{ N} = \mathbf{70.37 \text{ kN}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(iii) Extension Time:} \\
t_{ext} &= \frac{\pi D^{2} L}{4Q} = \frac{\pi \times 0.01 \times 0.600}{4 \times 1.00 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{0.01885}{4.00 \times 10^{-3}} = \mathbf{4.71 \text{ s}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(iv) Retraction Time:} \\
t_{ret} &= \frac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2}) L}{4Q} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0064 \times 0.600}{4 \times 1.00 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{0.01206}{4.00 \times 10^{-3}} = \mathbf{3.02 \text{ s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Extension Force | **109.96 kN** |
| Retraction Force | **70.37 kN** |
| Extension Time | **4.71 s** |
| Retraction Time | **3.02 s** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – July 2023 Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Explain the principle, construction, and working of a **regenerative circuit** with a neat circuit diagram. State two advantages and one disadvantage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic press uses a single-acting cylinder of bore 150 mm. The pump delivers 25 L/min at 200 bar. Calculate: (i) Maximum downward thrust, (ii) Maximum extension velocity, (iii) Stroke time for 300 mm travel, (iv) Hydraulic power. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — Regenerative Circuit

**Principle:** A regenerative circuit reuses the oil exhausting from the rod-end of the cylinder by routing it back to the cap-end (combined with pump flow), effectively doubling the extension flow rate and increasing speed.

**Construction (using ISO 1219 symbols):**
- Fixed displacement pump.
- 4/3 DCV with **tandem-center** position.
- Two check valves (CV1 and CV2).
- Double-acting cylinder.
- Pressure relief valve for safety.

**Working (Extension — Regenerative Mode):**
- DCV in regenerative position connects rod-end return to cap-end inlet.
- Pump flow $Q$ + rod-end flow $Q_{r}$ both enter cap-end.
- Effective flow into cap-end = $Q_{total} = Q + Q \times (A_{r}/A_{c})$.
- **Result:** Extension speed increases by factor of $(1 + A_{r}/A_{c})$.

**Working (Retraction — Normal Mode):**
- DCV shifts; pump flow goes to rod-end.
- Cap-end oil returns to tank through DCV.

**Advantages:**
1. **Faster extension** without using a larger pump (energy-efficient).
2. **Smaller pump capacity** required for the same speed.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Reduced extension force** because the rod-end back-pressure opposes the cap-end pressure. Effective force = $P \times A_{r}$ (instead of $P \times A_{c}$).

---

#### Solution to (b) — Hydraulic Press Numerical

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 0.150 \text{ m}, \quad L = 0.300 \text{ m} \\
P &= 200 \text{ bar} = 2.00 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
Q &= 25 \text{ L/min} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Calculations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{(i) Maximum Downward Thrust:} \\
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.150)^{2} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^{2} \\
F &= P \times A = 2.00 \times 10^{7} \times 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \\
&= 3.534 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} = \mathbf{353.4 \text{ kN}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(ii) Maximum Extension Velocity:} \\
v &= \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} \\
&= 2.358 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s} = \mathbf{23.58 \text{ mm/s}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(iii) Stroke Time:} \\
t &= \frac{A \times L}{Q} = \frac{1.767 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.300}{4.167 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{5.301 \times 10^{-3}}{4.167 \times 10^{-4}} = \mathbf{12.72 \text{ s}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{(iv) Hydraulic Power:} \\
P_{hyd} &= \Delta P \times Q = 2.00 \times 10^{7} \times 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 8333 \text{ W} = \mathbf{8.33 \text{ kW}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error (Major):** Students frequently write $P = 140$ bar directly in the formula $F = P \times A$ without converting to Pa. This leads to a 10⁵ magnitude error and **3-mark deduction** in numericals.
> 2. **Mixing up Rod-end and Cap-end areas:** Always remember $A_{r} < A_{c}$. The rod-end area uses $(D^{2} - d^{2})$, not $d^{2}$ alone.
> 3. **Forgetting volumetric efficiency:** In real KTU problems with given $\eta_{vol}$, divide theoretical flow by efficiency before computing velocity: $Q_{actual} = Q_{pump} \times \eta_{vol}$.
> 4. **Missing return-line back-pressure:** When the question specifies a "back-pressure of X bar" in the tank line, subtract it from rod-end calculations.
> 5. **Sketch missing components:** A circuit drawn without **relief valve, filter, and reservoir labeling** loses 1–2 marks in 7-mark descriptive questions.
> 6. **Confusing regenerative force with normal force:** In regen circuits, force is **reduced** to $F = P \times A_{r}$, not $F = P \times A_{c}$. This is a very common 2-mark error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pascal's Law** is the foundation: pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions.
- ✅ The **fundamental power equation** is $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \times Q$ (W), where $\Delta P$ is in Pa and $Q$ in m³/s.
- ✅ **Cap-end area** $A_{c} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^{2}$ ; **Rod-end area** $A_{r} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(D^{2} - d^{2})$.
- ✅ Cylinder **force**: $F = P \times A$ ; **velocity**: $v = Q/A$ ; **time**: $t = A \times L / Q$.
- ✅ **Retraction is always faster** than extension at the same flow rate (smaller area to fill).
- ✅ **Retraction force is always less** than extension force at the same pressure.
- ✅ **Regenerative circuit** = faster extension + reduced force (force = $P \times A_{r}$).
- ✅ **Meter-in** controls speed by throttling inlet flow; **Meter-out** controls outlet flow.
- ✅ **Relief valve** is a safety device — must always be present in a hydraulic circuit.
- ✅ **Filter placement**: suction strainer (100 μm) + pressure filter (10 μm) + return filter (25 μm) is the KTU-recommended three-stage filtration.
- ✅ **ISO 1219** governs symbols; **ISO 4413** governs hydraulic safety rules.
- ✅ Standard cleanliness target: **ISO 4406 — 18/16/13** (NAS 7 approx.).
- ✅ Always convert bar → Pa: multiply by $10^{5}$.
- ✅ Always convert L/min → m³/s: multiply by $10^{-3}/60 = 1.667 \times 10^{-5}$.
- ✅ **Pump volumetric efficiency** $\eta_{vol} = Q_{actual}/Q_{theoretical}$ (typically 0.92–0.98).
- ✅ **Counterbalance valve** = back-pressure holding valve for vertical loads.
- ✅ **Sequence valve** = pressure-operated valve that opens at a preset pressure.
- ✅ **Unloading valve** = diverts pump flow to tank at low pressure, saving energy.

---

> **END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — SIMPLE HYDRAULIC CIRCUITS**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 guidelines, and ISO 4413 safety standards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydropneumatic and Electrohydraulic, System Maintenance and Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT752 - FLUID POWER SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hydropneumatic and Electrohydraulic, System Maintenance and Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydropneumatic Systems

**Formal KTU Definition:**
A **Hydropneumatic System** is an integrated fluid power circuit that combines the incompressible, high-force characteristics of hydraulic fluid with the compressible, energy-storage characteristics of a compressed gas (typically nitrogen). The heart of this system is the **Hydropneumatic Accumulator**, a pressure vessel that uses a pre-charged inert gas to store hydraulic energy, dampen pressure pulsations, and provide emergency or auxiliary power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term *hydropneumatic* specifically refers to the use of a gas pre-charged **accumulator** coupled to a hydraulic circuit. It is *not* a hybrid of separate pneumatic and hydraulic machines operating in parallel; rather, the gas is *internal* to the hydraulic loop.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Piggy Bank" 🎯**
Imagine a bicycle shock absorber: inside the steel tube is a piston, and behind the piston is trapped air. When you hit a bump, the piston pushes into the air, the air compresses and absorbs the energy, then it springs back smoothly. A hydropneumatic accumulator works on the *exact same principle* but scaled up for industrial force. The trapped gas acts like a **spring**, while the hydraulic oil transmits the force. When the pump fills the accumulator, it is essentially "saving money" in an energy bank. When the system needs a sudden burst of flow (like a press stamping metal), it "withdraws" this energy instantly — far faster than any pump could supply.

**Key Physical Constants & Standards:**
- Standard pre-charge gas: **Nitrogen (N₂)** — inert, non-flammable, dry.
- Typical pre-charge pressure (P₀): **25% to 90%** of the maximum hydraulic working pressure (P₂).
- Hydraulic fluid: Mineral oil (ISO VG 32, 46, or 68).
- Operating temperature range: **−20 °C to +80 °C**.

---

## 1.2 Electrohydraulic Systems

**Formal KTU Definition:**
An **Electrohydraulic System** is a closed-loop or open-loop fluid power system in which the *command signal* is electrical (low-power, easy to process) and the *actuation* is hydraulic (high-power, precise motion). The interface between the two domains is achieved through **electrohydraulic actuators** — most commonly the proportional solenoid valve, servo valve, or the On/Off solenoid-operated directional control valve (DCV).

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Brain Talking to the Bicep" 🧠💪**
Think of how your body lifts a feather versus a dumbbell. Your brain sends a *tiny electrical signal* through nerves, and your bicep muscle generates a *huge mechanical force*. The brain cannot directly lift weights; it must use the muscle as a power amplifier. In an electrohydraulic system, the **PLC or microcontroller** is the "brain," the **solenoid coil** is the "nerve," and the **hydraulic cylinder/motor** is the "muscle." A 24 V DC signal of just 0.5 A can command a cylinder producing **20,000 N** of force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why combine them?** Electronics are excellent at *thinking* (logic, feedback, precision) but poor at *lifting*. Hydraulics is excellent at *lifting* but poor at *thinking*. Electrohydraulics combines the **intelligence of electronics** with the **muscle of hydraulics**.

---

## 1.3 System Maintenance & Safety — The Overarching Framework

**Formal Definition:**
System Maintenance refers to the **scheduled and condition-based set of procedures** used to ensure that a fluid power installation retains its design performance, efficiency, and safety margins throughout its operational life. System Safety refers to the engineered and procedural controls (relief valves, lockouts, PPE) that protect personnel, machinery, and the environment from the inherent hazards of high-pressure fluid power.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Car Servicing & Seatbelts" 🚗**
A car's owner does two things: (1) takes the car for periodic servicing (oil change, brake check) — this is **Maintenance**. (2) Wears a seatbelt and checks the airbags — this is **Safety**. Skipping servicing leads to breakdowns; skipping safety leads to fatalities. In fluid power, a neglected hydraulic hose can burst at 200 bar and slice through a worker's leg; a missing pressure relief valve can cause a cylinder to explode.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Safety Mandate:** Per **KTU 2024 Scheme CO4**, students must be able to *identify, evaluate, and mitigate* hazards in fluid power systems. Maintenance and Safety are **NOT** separate topics — they are inseparable pillars. A poorly maintained valve is an unsafe valve.

**Maintenance Classification (ISO 13373 / BSI Standard):**
- **Preventive Maintenance (PM):** Scheduled, time-based (e.g., change filter every 500 hours).
- **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** Condition-based using sensors (vibration, oil analysis, thermography).
- **Corrective Maintenance (CM):** Repair *after* failure — least preferred, highest cost.

**Core Safety Hazards (must be memorized for KTU):**
1. **Stored hydraulic energy** in accumulators (latent, invisible).
2. **High-pressure injection injuries** — a 0.1 mm pinhole at 200 bar can penetrate skin.
3. **Fire hazard** from oil mist ignition at temperatures > **350 °C**.
4. **Noise pollution** — hydraulic pumps can exceed **100 dB(A)**.
5. **Cavitation** — vapor bubble collapse damaging metal surfaces.
6. **Hydraulic shock (water hammer analogue)** from sudden valve closure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hydropneumatic Accumulator — The Heart of the System

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
A bladder, piston, or diaphragm-type accumulator divides a pressure vessel into two chambers:
- **Gas side (pre-charge):** Filled with nitrogen at P₀ at a reference temperature T₀.
- **Fluid side:** Connected to the hydraulic line at system pressure P.

When system pressure **exceeds** P₀, the gas compresses and the bladder contracts, pushing oil out into the circuit (discharge phase — supplies flow).

When system pressure **falls below** P₀, the gas expands, pushing oil back into the circuit (charging phase — accepts flow).

> [!NOTE]
> **Boyles-Mariotte Law (Isothermal Assumption):** The process is assumed isothermal for *slow* charge/discharge cycles because gas heat exchanges with the vessel wall. For *rapid* cycles (adiabatic), the polytropic relation is used.

### 2.1.2 The Polytropic Process (Generalized Gas Law)

The actual compression/expansion of nitrogen in an accumulator follows:

$$P_0 \cdot V_0^{n} = P_1 \cdot V_1^{n} = P_2 \cdot V_2^{n} = \text{constant}$$

where the exponent **n** is the **polytropic index**:
- **n = 1** → Isothermal (slow processes, time > 1 minute).
- **n = 1.4** → Adiabatic (rapid processes, time < 1 second, no heat transfer).
- **1 < n < 1.4** → Polytropic (real-world, often taken as **n = 1.4** for KTU problems to be conservative).

### 2.1.3 Effective Gas Volume at Any Pressure
Starting from the gas law:

$$V_2 = V_0 \left(\frac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}}$$

The **effective working volume** of the accumulator (the volume of useful oil delivered between max pressure P₂ and min pressure P₁) is:

$$\Delta V = V_1 - V_2 = V_0 \cdot P_0^{\frac{1}{n}} \cdot \left(P_1^{-\frac{1}{n}} - P_2^{-\frac{1}{n}}\right)$$

### 2.1.4 Pumping Fluid Savings
By installing an accumulator, the average pump flow requirement is reduced because the accumulator supplies flow during peak demand. The **pump flow reduction** is:

$$Q_{\text{pump,avg}} = Q_{\text{continuous,load}} - \frac{\Delta V}{t_{\text{cycle}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** A correctly sized accumulator can reduce pump size by **30–60%** in a punch press or injection moulding machine, saving energy, heat, and capital cost.

---

## 2.2 Electrohydraulic System — Block Architecture

A typical closed-loop electrohydraulic position-control system has the following block diagram elements (in the signal-flow direction):

**Reference Input (Command)** → **Controller (PLC/Analog)** → **Amplifier (Power)** → **Servo/Proportional Valve** → **Hydraulic Actuator (Cylinder/Motor)** → **Load** → **Feedback Sensor (LVDT/Encoder)** → back to Controller.

### 2.2.1 Proportional vs Servo Valves (KTU Hot Topic)

| Feature | Proportional Valve | Servo Valve |
|---|---|---|
| **Spool type** | Single-stage, mechanical feedback on spool | Flapper-nozzle, 2-stage, 3-stage |
| **Bandwidth** | 10–50 Hz | 100–500 Hz |
| **Hysteresis** | 3–6% | < 1% |
| **Cost** | Low–Medium | Very High |
| **Application** | Mobile, presses, machine tools | CNC, aerospace, robotics |
| **Control signal** | PWM or ±10 V analog | ±10 V analog, high current |
| **Filter cleanliness (ISO 4406)** | 18/16/13 | 15/13/10 |

### 2.2.2 Transfer Function (Linearized, 1st-Order Approximation for KTU)
For a proportional valve with spool displacement $x_v$ proportional to input current $i$:

$$Q_L(s) = K_v \cdot x_v(s) - K_c \cdot P_L(s)$$

where $K_v$ is the **flow gain** and $K_c$ is the **pressure-flow coefficient** (leakage). The cylinder load flow to velocity is:

$$Q_L = A \cdot \frac{dx_p}{dt} + C_t \cdot P_L + \frac{V_t}{4\beta_e} \cdot \frac{dP_L}{dt}$$

These are not typically derivable in 14-mark KTU questions but are essential for an **understanding-level (CO2)** answer.

---

## 2.3 System Maintenance — The 5 Pillars

1. **Fluid Cleanliness & Filtration** — Hydraulic fluid contamination is the **#1 cause** of failure (responsible for ~75% of breakdowns).
2. **Temperature Management** — For every **10 °C rise** above 60 °C, oil life halves (Arrhenius rule of thumb).
3. **Hose, Tube & Fitting Inspection** — Replace hoses every **2 years** or **4000 hours**, whichever comes first.
4. **Leak Detection & Sealing** — External leaks are environmental hazards; internal leaks reduce efficiency.
5. **Condition Monitoring** — Vibration analysis, oil analysis (spectrography, particle count), thermography.

---

## 2.4 System Safety — The Hierarchy of Controls

Following the **NIOSH Hierarchy of Controls** (most effective to least effective):

1. **Elimination** — Redesign to remove hazard (best).
2. **Substitution** — Use safer fluid (e.g., water-glycol instead of mineral oil in hot environments).
3. **Engineering Controls** — Pressure relief valves, rupture discs, accumulators with safety blocks, guards.
4. **Administrative Controls** — Lockout/Tagout (LOTO), training, signage.
5. **PPE** — Gloves, goggles, hearing protection, face shield (last line of defense).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Boyle's Law (Isothermal) | $P_0 V_0 = P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Pa·m³ | Slow accumulator cycles, n=1 |
| 2 | Polytropic Law (General) | $P_0 V_0^{n} = P_1 V_1^{n}$ | Pa·m³, n=1.4 | All KTU problems unless stated |
| 3 | Gas Volume at System Pressure | $V_2 = V_0 \left(\frac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n}$ | m³ | Sizing accumulator |
| 4 | Effective Oil Volume (Usable) | $\Delta V = V_0 P_0^{1/n}\left(P_1^{-1/n} - P_2^{-1/n}\right)$ | m³ | Energy storage calculation |
| 5 | Hydraulic Force on Piston | $F = P \cdot A$ | N (P in Pa, A in m²) | Cylinder sizing, braking |
| 6 | Pump Flow Rate | $Q = \frac{V_D \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s | Pump selection |
| 7 | Pressure Drop (Hagen-Poiseuille, laminar) | $\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi d^{4}}$ | Pa | Filter/line sizing |
| 8 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \frac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Flow regime check |
| 9 | Hydraulic Power | $P_h = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | W | Energy balance |
| 10 | Oil Life (Arrhenius rule) | Life halves per 10 °C above 60 °C | hours | Maintenance schedule |
| 11 | Sound Level Comparison | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} + 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)$ | dB | Noise control |
| 12 | Accumulator Energy Stored | $E = \int_{V_0}^{V_2} P \, dV = \frac{P_2 V_2 - P_0 V_0}{1-n}$ | Joules | Emergency power sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pro Tip:** In **14-mark derivation problems**, examiners love the Polytropic Law. Always state your assumption (n=1 or n=1.4) **before** writing the formula. Marks are lost when students assume n without justification.

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Hydropneumatic accumulators** are mandated in **vehicle hydraulic brakes** (BHARAT NCAP, ECE R13) and **wind turbine pitch control**.
> - **Electrohydraulic servo systems** are the actuation backbone of the **Boeing 787** flight control surfaces and **Boeing777x** main landing gear steering.
> - **Predictive maintenance** using oil spectrography can detect wear **8 weeks before failure**, saving ₹₹₹ in downtime costs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Oil Volume from a Bladder Accumulator (KTU Favourite)

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU-style):**
A bladder-type accumulator has a total internal volume $V_0 = \mathbf{10 \text{ liters}}$. It is pre-charged with nitrogen at $P_0 = \mathbf{50 \text{ bar}}$ (absolute) at room temperature. The minimum operating (cut-in) pressure is $P_1 = \mathbf{100 \text{ bar}}$ and the maximum (cut-out) pressure is $P_2 = \mathbf{200 \text{ bar}}$. Assuming an isothermal process (n = 1), find the **effective oil volume** the accumulator can deliver.

**Step 1 — Identify knowns and convert to SI units:**

$$V_0 = 10 \text{ L} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3} = 0.01 \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$P_0 = 50 \text{ bar} = 50 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 5.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_1 = 100 \text{ bar} = 1.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_2 = 200 \text{ bar} = 2.0 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$$

$$n = 1 \text{ (isothermal, given)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Polytropic Gas Law to find the gas volume at P₁ (fully charged state):**

Using the relation $P_0 V_0^{n} = P_1 V_1^{n}$:

$$V_1 = V_0 \cdot \left(\frac{P_0}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}}$$

Substitute values (n = 1):

$$V_1 = 0.01 \cdot \left(\frac{5.0 \times 10^{6}}{1.0 \times 10^{7}}\right)^{1}$$

$$V_1 = 0.01 \cdot (0.5)$$

$$V_1 = 0.005 \text{ m}^{3} = 5 \text{ liters}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the same law to find gas volume at P₂ (discharged state):**

$$V_2 = V_0 \cdot \left(\frac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}}$$

$$V_2 = 0.01 \cdot \left(\frac{5.0 \times 10^{6}}{2.0 \times 10^{7}}\right)^{1}$$

$$V_2 = 0.01 \cdot (0.25)$$

$$V_2 = 0.0025 \text{ m}^{3} = 2.5 \text{ liters}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the effective oil volume (the difference in gas volumes, since gas volume decreases as oil is pushed out):**

$$\Delta V = V_1 - V_2$$

$$\Delta V = 0.005 - 0.0025$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = 0.0025 \text{ m}^{3} = 2.5 \text{ liters}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation (the "Why"):**
Out of the 10 L total volume, only **2.5 L** of oil is *useful* between 100 and 200 bar. The remaining 7.5 L is either pre-charge gas space (at 200 bar) or non-usable above 200 bar. This is why **over-sizing** the accumulator based only on total volume is a classic KTU pitfall.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Adiabatic (n = 1.4) Accumulator Sizing

**Problem Setup:**
Same accumulator ($V_0 = 10$ L, $P_0 = 50$ bar), but the system is a **fast-acting press** with cycle time < 1 s. Take **n = 1.4**. Find the effective volume.

**Step 1 — Use the same general formula with n = 1.4:**

$$V_1 = 0.01 \cdot \left(\frac{P_0}{P_1}\right)^{1/1.4} = 0.01 \cdot (0.5)^{0.7143}$$

Compute $(0.5)^{0.7143}$:
- $\ln(0.5) = -0.6931$
- $-0.6931 \times 0.7143 = -0.4951$
- $e^{-0.4951} = 0.6096$

$$V_1 = 0.01 \times 0.6096 = 0.006096 \text{ m}^{3} = 6.096 \text{ L}$$

**Step 2 — Compute V₂:**

$$V_2 = 0.01 \cdot \left(\frac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/1.4} = 0.01 \cdot (0.25)^{0.7143}$$

Compute $(0.25)^{0.7143}$:
- $\ln(0.25) = -1.3863$
- $-1.3863 \times 0.7143 = -0.9902$
- $e^{-0.9902} = 0.3715$

$$V_2 = 0.01 \times 0.3715 = 0.003715 \text{ m}^{3} = 3.715 \text{ L}$$

**Step 3 — Effective volume:**

$$\Delta V = V_1 - V_2 = 6.096 - 3.715$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{\text{adiabatic}} = 2.381 \text{ L}}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison & Insight:**

| Process | Effective Volume |
|---|---|
| Isothermal (n = 1) | 2.500 L |
| Adiabatic (n = 1.4) | 2.381 L |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** The adiabatic process delivers *less* oil for the *same* pressure range. This is because during adiabatic compression, gas temperature rises, increasing pressure, so the gas resists further compression. **Designers must use n = 1.4 for fast systems to avoid under-sizing.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Hydraulic Press Force and Pump Selection

**Problem Setup:**
A hydraulic press operates at 250 bar. The cylinder bore diameter is 100 mm, rod diameter is 60 mm (single-rod, extending stroke). The press must extend in 3 seconds. Find:
- (a) Maximum extending force
- (b) Pump flow rate required (assuming no accumulator)
- (c) Pump size if an accumulator of 5 L effective volume is fitted (so pump must refill the accumulator in 3 s plus supply leakage).

**Step 1 — Compute piston area (annulus for extension):**

$$A_{\text{piston}} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^{2} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute maximum extending force:**

$$F = P \cdot A = 250 \times 10^{5} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F = 250 \times 10^{5} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 1.9635 \times 10^{5} \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F = 196.35 \text{ kN} \approx 20 \text{ tonnes-force}}$$

**Step 3 — Cylinder volume (oil needed for full extension of 200 mm stroke):**

$$V_{\text{cyl}} = A \cdot L = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 = 1.571 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3} = 1.571 \text{ L}$$

**Step 4 — Pump flow rate (no accumulator case):**

$$Q = \frac{V_{\text{cyl}}}{t} = \frac{1.571 \times 10^{-3}}{3} = 5.236 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 5.236 \times 10^{-4} \times 60 \times 10^{3} = 31.4 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 5 — With accumulator, pump must refill ΔV = 5 L in 3 s (plus a 10% leakage margin):**

$$Q_{\text{pump}} = \frac{1.1 \times (V_{\text{cyl}} + \Delta V_{\text{acc}})}{t} = \frac{1.1 \times (1.571 + 5)}{3} = \frac{1.1 \times 6.571}{3} = 2.409 \text{ L/s}$$

$$Q_{\text{pump}} = 2.409 \times 60 = 144.6 \text{ L/min}$$

**Wait — this is *larger*?** Re-examine logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrected logic with accumulator:** The accumulator **supplies** the high-flow demand (peak); the pump only needs to supply the *average* flow. For a press with duty cycle 10% (1 s press, 9 s idle), the pump only needs to refill the accumulator during the 9 s idle time. So:

$$Q_{\text{pump, average}} = \frac{\Delta V_{\text{acc}} + \text{leakage}}{t_{\text{idle}}} = \frac{5 + 0.1}{9} \approx 0.567 \text{ L/s} = 34 \text{ L/min}$$

> The peak demand of 31.4 L/min for 1 s is supplied entirely by the accumulator's 5 L reserve at very high instantaneous flow (which a 5 L bladder can deliver at > 200 L/min easily). This is the **pump-downsizing benefit** of accumulators.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram 1 — Hydropneumatic Press Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reservoir Tank] --> B[Electric Motor 7.5 kW]
    B --> C[Variable Displacement Pump]
    C --> D[Check Valve NRV1]
    D --> E[Pressure Relief Valve PRV set at 210 bar]
    E --> A
    D --> F[Pressure Line 200 bar]
    F --> G[Bladder Accumulator V0 = 10 L P0 = 50 bar]
    G --> H[Safety Block Manual Shut-off]
    H --> I[3 by 4 Directional Control Valve DCV]
    I --> J[Double Acting Cylinder Bore 100 mm]
    J --> K[Die and Workpiece]
    K --> L[Return Line Filter 10 micron]
    L --> A
    I --> M[Return to Tank]
    M --> A
    F --> N[Pressure Gauge PG1]
    F --> O[Pressure Transducer PT1 to PLC]
```

**Functional Narrative:**
- The **pump (C)** supplies oil at 200 bar.
- The **accumulator (G)** is connected in parallel to the pressure line and stores energy during the dwell portion of the cycle.
- During the **rapid extend** stroke, the DCV (I) shifts; the accumulator (G) supplies peak flow alongside the pump.
- The **safety block (H)** isolates the accumulator for safe maintenance — it is a manually-operated valve that *bleeds* the gas-side residual pressure and locks it out.
- The **PRV (E)** protects against overpressure, dumping to tank.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram 2 — Closed-Loop Electrohydraulic Servo System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Operator HMI or CNC Program] --> A2[Setpoint Signal Reference]
    A2 --> A3[PID Controller PLC or Analog Card]
    A3 --> A4[Valve Amplifier Power Driver 24 VDC to 1.5 A]
    A4 --> A5[Servo Valve 3 stage Flapper Nozzle]
    A5 --> A6[Hydraulic Cylinder or Motor]
    A6 --> A7[Load Machine Slide]
    A7 --> A8[Linear Position Sensor LVDT or Encoder]
    A8 --> A9[Signal Conditioner]
    A9 --> A3
    A6 --> A10[Pressure Sensor Load Cell]
    A10 --> A3
```

**Functional Narrative:**
- This is a **closed-loop position-control system**.
- The **LVDT (A8)** provides real-time position feedback; the **load cell (A10)** provides force feedback.
- The **PID controller (A3)** continuously compares setpoint vs. actual and adjusts the servo valve (A5) opening.
- The **servo valve (A5)** modulates the oil flow to the cylinder (A6) with **bandwidth > 100 Hz**, allowing precise tracking of the reference.
- Hydraulic fluid returns through a cooler and filter to tank (not shown, parallel path).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram 3 — Maintenance Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Daily Check Visual Inspection] --> M1{Any Leakage or Noise?}
    M1 -- Yes --> M2[Tag Out Equipment and Investigate]
    M1 -- No --> M3[Check Fluid Level and Temperature]
    M3 --> M4[Sample Oil for Spectrographic Analysis Every 500 hours]
    M4 --> M5{Particle Count Exceeds ISO 18 by 16 by 13?}
    M5 -- Yes --> M6[Replace Filter and Bleed System]
    M5 -- No --> M7[Check Hoses and Fittings Age Visual]
    M7 --> M8{Hose Older than 2 Years or Damaged?}
    M8 -- Yes --> M9[Replace Hose and Re Torque to Spec]
    M8 -- No --> M10[Log Data and Resume Operation]
    M6 --> M10
    M2 --> M11[Lockout Tagout LOTO and Repair]
    M9 --> M10
```

**Functional Narrative:**
- This is a **preventive-to-predictive maintenance flow** (the modern ISO 55000 / Total Productive Maintenance approach).
- The key decision point is **M5** — oil cleanliness check — because contamination is the leading cause of hydraulic failure.
- The **M11** branch invokes **LOTO (Lockout/Tagout)** which is the single most important safety procedure in fluid power maintenance.

---

## 4.4 Component-Level Diagram 4 — Accumulator Safety Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[System Pressure Line] --> S2[Manual Isolation Valve Ball Type]
    S2 --> S3[Accumulator Port]
    S3 --> S4[Accumulator Body with Bladder]
    S4 --> S5[Gas Pre charge Valve Schrader Type on Top]
    S2 --> S6[Bleed Valve to Tank]
    S2 --> S7[Pressure Gauge for Trapped Pressure]
    S4 -.trapped pressure after isolation.-> S7
```

**Safety Logic:** Closing S2 isolates the accumulator. S6 is then opened to bleed any *trapped* fluid-side pressure. The gas pre-charge at S5 *cannot* be released by S2 — only the gas valve (S5) can do that, with a special tool. This **double-block** is mandatory per ASME BPVC Section VIII and IS 2825.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024]**
**Define a hydropneumatic accumulator. Explain why nitrogen is used as the pre-charge gas instead of air.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A hydropneumatic accumulator is a pressure vessel that stores hydraulic energy using a pre-charged compressible gas (typically nitrogen) separated from the hydraulic fluid by a flexible bladder, piston, or diaphragm. (1 mark)

Nitrogen is used because:
1. It is **inert** — does not react with hydraulic oil or cause oxidation. (1 mark)
2. It is **dry and oil-free** — prevents moisture contamination that would degrade oil. (0.5 mark)
3. It has a **predictable gas-law behaviour** and is non-flammable, satisfying safety codes. (0.5 mark)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023]**
**List any three differences between proportional valves and servo valves.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each for any three correct points):**

| Parameter | Proportional Valve | Servo Valve |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | 10–50 Hz | 100–500 Hz |
| Hysteresis | 3–6% | < 1% |
| Cost | Low–Medium | Very High (5–10×) |
| Typical use | Mobile, presses | CNC, aerospace |
| Filter cleanliness | ISO 18/16/13 | ISO 15/13/10 |
| Spool stages | Single-stage | Two- or three-stage |

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Module 4, Dec 2024, Modified]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the effective oil volume $\Delta V$ delivered by a bladder-type hydropneumatic accumulator operating between pressures $P_1$ (minimum) and $P_2$ (maximum). State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydropneumatic accumulator has a total volume of 20 L and is pre-charged to 60 bar (gauge). If the system operates between 120 bar (cut-in) and 240 bar (cut-out), and the process is **isothermal**, calculate:
- (i) Gas volume at the cut-out pressure
- (ii) Effective oil volume delivered per cycle
- (iii) The energy stored in the accumulator at 240 bar **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumption 1:** The pre-charge gas behaves as an **ideal gas**.
**Assumption 2:** The process is **isothermal** (slow charge/discharge, n = 1). For fast processes, n = 1.4 (adiabatic).
**Assumption 3:** The accumulator is a **bladder type** (gas and oil separated by an elastic membrane).
**Assumption 4:** Hydraulic fluid is **incompressible**.

Consider a gas pre-charged at pressure $P_0$ in volume $V_0$. When the system reaches the minimum working pressure $P_1$, the gas has been compressed to volume $V_1$:

$$P_0 V_0^{n} = P_1 V_1^{n}$$

Solving for $V_1$:

$$V_1 = V_0 \left(\frac{P_0}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

When the system reaches the maximum working pressure $P_2$, the gas volume further reduces to $V_2$:

$$V_2 = V_0 \left(\frac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

The **effective oil volume** is the oil pushed out as the gas compresses from $V_1$ to $V_2$:

$$\Delta V = V_1 - V_2 = V_0 \left(\frac{P_0}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} - V_0 \left(\frac{P_0}{P_2}\right)^{1/n}$$

Factoring:

$$\boxed{\Delta V = V_0 \, P_0^{1/n} \left[ P_1^{-1/n} - P_2^{-1/n} \right]} \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Mark allocation:** 1 mark for each assumption (4 assumptions → 4 marks), 1.5 marks each for steps (i) and (ii), 1 mark for final expression.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $V_0 = 20 \text{ L} = 0.020 \text{ m}^{3}$
- $P_0 = 60 \text{ bar (gauge)} = 60 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 6.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$
- $P_1 = 120 \text{ bar} = 1.2 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$
- $P_2 = 240 \text{ bar} = 2.4 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$
- $n = 1$ (isothermal)

**Note (for absolute vs gauge):** Pre-charge pressure is typically stated as **gauge** in industrial practice, but for strict gas-law calculations, **absolute** values are required. Add 1 bar atmospheric to $P_0$ if not specified. Here, we proceed with gauge values (a common engineering simplification in KTU problems). Examiner's discretion.

**(i) Gas volume at cut-out (P₂ = 240 bar):**

$$V_2 = V_0 \left(\frac{P_0}{P_2}\right) = 0.020 \times \left(\frac{60}{240}\right) = 0.020 \times 0.25$$

$$\boxed{V_2 = 0.005 \text{ m}^{3} = 5 \text{ L}}$$

**(ii) Effective oil volume (between P₁ and P₂):**

First find $V_1$:

$$V_1 = V_0 \left(\frac{P_0}{P_1}\right) = 0.020 \times \left(\frac{60}{120}\right) = 0.020 \times 0.5 = 0.010 \text{ m}^{3} = 10 \text{ L}$$

Then:

$$\Delta V = V_1 - V_2 = 10 - 5 = 5 \text{ L}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = 0.005 \text{ m}^{3} = 5 \text{ liters}}$$

**(iii) Energy stored at 240 bar:**

Energy stored in an isothermal gas process is the integral of PdV:

$$E = \int_{V_0}^{V_2} P \, dV = P_0 V_0 \ln\left(\frac{V_0}{V_2}\right)$$

(Using the identity $\int P \, dV$ for isothermal expansion.)

$$E = 6.0 \times 10^{6} \times 0.020 \times \ln\left(\frac{0.020}{0.005}\right)$$

$$E = 1.2 \times 10^{5} \times \ln(4)$$

$$E = 1.2 \times 10^{5} \times 1.3863$$

$$\boxed{E = 1.6636 \times 10^{5} \text{ J} = 166.36 \text{ kJ}}$$

This is enough energy to lift a 10-tonne car by **~1.7 metres** — illustrating why accumulators are emergency power reserves.

**Mark allocation:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks.

---

### **Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Module 4, Modified, Internal Choice]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **closed-loop electrohydraulic position-control system**. Label all components and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **principles of preventive and predictive maintenance** in a hydraulic system. Include a maintenance schedule for a typical industrial hydraulic press. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Electrohydraulic Closed-Loop System (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (drawn by student in exam):**

```
Setpoint → Controller (PID) → Amplifier → Servo Valve → Cylinder → Load
                ↑                                                                    ↓
                └────────── Position Feedback (LVDT) ←──────────────────────────┘
```

**Component-wise function (1 mark each, 7 components):**

1. **Setpoint Input** — Desired position (e.g., from CNC program, in mm).
2. **PID Controller** — Compares setpoint and feedback; generates an error signal; computes corrective action using Proportional-Integral-Derivative algorithm. Located in PLC or motion controller.
3. **Amplifier (Valve Driver)** — Boosts the low-current controller output (e.g., ±10 V, 10 mA) to the high-current signal required by the servo valve coil (e.g., ±40 mA, ±200 mA).
4. **Servo Valve** — Modulates hydraulic flow proportional to input current. High-bandwidth (100–500 Hz), low-hysteresis.
5. **Hydraulic Cylinder / Motor** — Converts pressurized oil flow into linear/rotary mechanical motion.
6. **Load** — The machine element being moved (e.g., press slide, robot arm).
7. **Position Feedback (LVDT / Encoder)** — Measures actual position and sends it back to the controller to close the loop. Error is computed, and the cycle repeats.

> The system achieves **sub-millimetre positional accuracy** in CNC and aerospace applications.

#### Part (b) — Maintenance (7 marks)

**Preventive Maintenance (PM)** is time-based, scheduled activity to prevent failure:
- Daily: Visual inspection, fluid level, temperature, leak check.
- Weekly: Filter differential pressure check, hose visual inspection.
- 500 hours: Oil sampling, spectrographic analysis.
- 2000 hours: Full filter replacement, breather replacement.
- 4000 hours / 2 years: Hose replacement (mandatory safety).

**Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** is condition-based, using sensor data to predict failure:
- **Vibration analysis** — detects pump bearing wear, cavitation.
- **Oil spectrography** — measures Fe, Cu, Al particles indicating wear.
- **Thermography** — detects hot spots in valves, motors.
- **Ultrasonic leak detection** — finds high-pressure leaks inaudibly.
- **Pressure and flow trending** — detects internal leakage (valve wear).

**Typical Hydraulic Press Maintenance Schedule (KTU Table):**

| Interval | Task |
|---|---|
| Daily | Check oil level, temperature, gauges; listen for abnormal noise |
| Weekly | Check hose condition, tightness of fittings |
| Monthly | Sample oil (ISO 4406 particle count); check filter ΔP |
| 500 hrs | Replace return-line filter; oil analysis (FTIR + ICP) |
| 2000 hrs | Replace pressure filter; inspect accumulator pre-charge |
| Annually | Calibrate gauges, transducers; test relief valve pop-off |
| 2 years | Replace all flexible hoses; inspect bladder accumulator |

**Key principle:** PM reduces *unexpected* failures; PdM reduces *unnecessary* maintenance. Together, they form **Total Productive Maintenance (TPM)**.

**Mark allocation:** PM definition + 3 examples = 2 marks; PdM definition + 3 examples = 2 marks; Schedule table = 2 marks; Conclusion = 1 mark.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Module:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing n = 1 and n = 1.4:** Students often use the wrong polytropic index. If the problem says "rapid press cycle," use **n = 1.4**. If it says "slow reservoir charging," use **n = 1**. Stating the assumption = 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to convert bar → Pa:** Many students lose 1 mark by writing 250 bar directly into a formula. Always convert: **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa**.
> 
> 3. **Confusing pre-charge with system pressure:** $P_0$ is the *pre-charge* (gas side, low pressure), $P_1, P_2$ are *system pressures* (high). A common pitfall: writing $P_0 = 200$ bar instead of 50 bar.
> 
> 4. **Treating gauge and absolute pressures as the same:** The gas law strictly requires **absolute** pressure. If the question gives gauge pressure, add 1.013 bar to get absolute.
> 
> 5. **In electrohydraulic block diagrams:** Students often miss the **feedback path** (LVDT/encoder). A control system *without* a feedback loop is **open-loop** and **not** a servo system. Examiners look specifically for the feedback arrow.
> 
> 6. **Safety question trap:** Students list PPE (gloves, goggles) as the *first* line of defense. Per the **Hierarchy of Controls**, PPE is the *last* line. Putting PPE first = -1 mark.
> 
> 7. **Maintenance vs Safety conflation:** Maintenance questions test **scheduled activities**. Safety questions test **hazard identification and engineered controls**. Do not mix them in the answer; the examiner marks per rubric.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Hydropneumatic system** = hydraulic circuit + gas-charged accumulator.
- ✅ **Pre-charge gas** = **Nitrogen (N₂)**, not air (oxygen + moisture = corrosion).
- ✅ **Polytropic index n:** 1 (slow/isothermal) vs 1.4 (fast/adiabatic).
- ✅ **Gas law:** $P_0 V_0^{n} = P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}$.
- ✅ **Effective volume:** $\Delta V = V_1 - V_2$ (always positive).
- ✅ **Energy stored (isothermal):** $E = P_0 V_0 \ln(V_0 / V_2)$.
- ✅ **Accumulator functions:** Energy storage, pulsation damping, emergency power, leakage compensation, thermal compensation.
- ✅ **Electrohydraulic** = electrical command + hydraulic actuation. **Interface = solenoid/servo valve.**
- ✅ **Servo valve bandwidth (100–500 Hz) ≫ Proportional valve bandwidth (10–50 Hz).**
- ✅ **Filter cleanliness codes (ISO 4406):** 18/16/13 (proportional), 15/13/10 (servo).
- ✅ **75% of hydraulic failures** are caused by **fluid contamination** — single most important statistic.
- ✅ **Hose replacement interval:** 2 years OR 4000 hours (whichever comes first).
- ✅ **Oil life halves** for every **10 °C rise** above **60 °C** (Arrhenius rule).
- ✅ **LOTO (Lockout/Tagout)** is the **#1 maintenance safety procedure**.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ✅ **PRV (Pressure Relief Valve)** setting = 110% of max working pressure (industry rule).
- ✅ **Hydraulic injection injury** — even 0.1 mm leak at 200 bar can penetrate skin → requires immediate surgical attention.
- ✅ **High-pressure fluid injection** injuries are often **initially painless** but cause **tissue necrosis** — never underestimate a small "pin prick" leak.
- ✅ **Accumulator safety block** is mandatory for any maintenance on systems with accumulators.
- ✅ **Three T's of maintenance:** Time-based (PM), Trend-based (PdM), Trouble-based (CM) — PM ≫ PdM ≫ CM in terms of cost-efficiency.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 CO4 mapping:** This module directly addresses **CO4: Apply maintenance and safety procedures in fluid power installations.**

> **Final KTU Tip:** When in doubt on a 14-mark question, **draw the block diagram first** (2 marks guaranteed), then state assumptions, then derive step by step. Examiners reward structured, *justified* answers, not just final numerical values.
